thickness during the papermaking process. You can see various parts, some of which are lighter and some of
which are darker than the surrounding paper.
The security thread is also incorporated into the paper at the manufacturing stage. When the banknote is
held up to the light you will notice a dark line across the entire width of the banknote. If you look very
carefully at the thread against the light you will see the word “EURO” together with the value numeral (either
the right way round or back to front).
While being viewed against the light, the banknote can also be
checked for the see-through register. This feature is in the top
left-hand corner on the front of the banknote. Irregular marks,
printed on the front and the reverse of the banknote join to create a
complete and perfect value numeral when held up to the light.
On the front right-hand side of the low-value banknotes (€5, €10
and €20) there is a hologram foil stripe. When you move the
banknote from side to side, look for a brightly coloured euro symbol
and the value numeral (5, 10 or 20).
On the front of the high-value banknotes (€50, €100, €200 and €500) there is a hologram foil patch.
When you move the banknote look for a brightly coloured numeral and the image of the architectural motif
depicted on the banknote.
Another feature which can be easily spotted on the four highest-value banknotes is the colour-shifting ink.
You see it in the value numeral in the lower right-hand corner on the reverse sides of these four banknotes.
This numeral looks purple when viewed straight on, but olive green or even brown when viewed at an angle.
The lower denominations, the €5, €10 and €20 banknotes have an iridescent stripe. This only appears on
the reverse side. It shines under a bright light and you can see a euro symbol inside the stripe together with
the value numeral.
Always check more than one of the security features in order to ensure that a banknote is genuine.
The law on counterfeiting was updated by Part 5 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences)
Act, 2001. The new offences created by the act are:
• Counterfeiting currency notes and coins (s.33)
• Passing counterfeit currency notes and coins (s. 34)
• Custody or control of counterfeit currency notes and coins (s. 35)
• Making or possessing materials and implements for counterfeiting (s. 36)
• Importing or exporting counterfeit notes or coins into or from a Member State of the EU (s. 37)
Importantly the definition of currency in section 32(1) extends beyond the currency notes and coins
customarily used as money in the State to currency notes and coins customarily used as money in any other
State or territorial unit within it. Thus it is an offence in Ireland to counterfeit Canadian dollars here.
Section 32(2) provides that something is a counterfeit of a currency note or coin if it is not a currency note or
coin but resembles a currency note or coin (on one side or both) to such an extent that it is reasonably capable
of passing for a currency note or coin of that description. In other words, it is no defence to say that you are a
bad counterfeiter. In addition, a thing is a counterfeit if it is a currency note or coin which has been so altered

13.19. Computers and crime
Computer Crime Investigation Unit (CCIU) is the National Central Reference Point for High-Tech Crime and
Computer Forensics. Its role is to:
• Investigate or assist with the investigation of complex or serious criminal misuse of information and
communications technology (ICT)
• The forensic retrieval and examination of digital evidence stored on computer storage media
• Advise and assist investigators who encounter computer networks, including the internet, during the
course of their enquiries
The potential evidence and/or intelligence that may be obtained from modern electronic devices have become
a very important aspect of almost all criminal investigations. If the data on these devices is required as
evidence it is essential to prove that it was legally obtained and that its integrity (i.e. chain of custody) is such
that it can be relied upon by a Court.
Although it is not possible to give advice on every eventuality that may occur, there are two very broad
categories of situations that members will encounter; Non-Hostile and Hostile search sites (searches
under warrant).
In Non-Hostile situations, where you have co-operation and assistance, the provisions of sections 5 and 6 of
the Criminal Evidence Act, 1992 may assist in obtaining printouts (documentary evidence) of ‘business’
activities. An example of this would be a computerised payroll system where the software application provides
the ability to print a listing of payments to employees and so on.
Other data not associated with a ‘business’ activity will require careful consideration and should be handled in
accordance with the guidelines for ‘first responders’ (see below). An example of this might be a case of
harassment where email or SMS (text messaging) is being used and securing exact electronic copies of the
original messages must be one of the primary tasks.
When dealing with computers keep a detailed record of ‘who, how and when’ tasks are completed and
where necessary seek advice from the Computer Crime Investigation Unit at Garda Bureau of Fraud
Investigation (GBFI).
In a hostile situation, the type of investigation being conducted will dictate the member’s actions. In child
pornography investigations proving that a person ‘knowingly’ had possession of illegal images is an essential
ingredient of the offence. Therefore, the initial questioning of a suspect can be critical and may be vital in
proving ownership and establishing what the computer was being used for (Judges Rules may apply to any
such admissions).
Where a complaint originates from activity that is outside the direct control of a suspect such as a place of
work, school or library it is highly probable that the suspect has access to a computer or may have possession
of evidence in their home. Therefore it is essential to establish sufficient evidence to apply for a search
warrant. If the computer is part of a computer network that may be wired or wireless, the data you are
looking for could be on another computer. For example, on a computer network the suspect may have
carefully concealed or deleted their internet activity from their personal computer, but establishing the
architecture of the network may provide other evidence of the activity from proxy servers, routers, firewalls
and so on.

Chapter 13 Fraud

Whether the situation is Hostile or Non-Hostile always consider getting some advice or guidance from the
Computer Crime Investigation Unit at the GBFI.
The seizure of electronic devices requires careful
consideration in order to protect the data they may
contain. For example, Personal Digital Assistants
(PDAs), electronic organisers and mobile phones use
volatile and non-volatile (flash, SIM etc.) memory
devices. If the battery or power source that supports
the volatile memory is removed; it will generally cause
the data stored in this type of memory to be lost. Fax
machines, for example, may store incoming and
outgoing faxes in some form of volatile memory and
when unplugged, they are irretrievably lost. Some nonvolatile memory devices can be used to store data and
this may not be apparent without specialist software.
Remember, turning a device off could activate lockout
or other security features such as a requirement to
enter a PIN when restarting.
Digital CCTV recording devices use a wide variety of proprietary software for recording digital video. Although
they use large capacity hard disk drives to store the recordings they eventually run out of space and start
overwriting previous recordings. For this reason it is important to consider securing the necessary recordings as
early as possible. Also, as these are digital recordings, copying them to an analogue medium (VHS Tape) may
not secure the digital authentication that is built into most of these systems.
Computers and other removable storage media (diskettes, Zip’s, CD’s, USB Drives etc.) have become ‘plug and
play’ devices; however, with modern computer operating systems, switching on a computer or plugging in a
device will cause data to be written to the computer or device. This interference may subsequently affect the
integrity and reliability of any evidence obtained.

First Responder Guidelines
The following guidelines are for members who find themselves acting as first responders to an incident and
they cover the seizure and handling of computers and electronic storage media.
Where a complaint involves the use of a computer or communications via the internet the member taking the
complaint should establish what software applications were being used (email, instant messaging, Internet
Relay Chat IRC etc.) and a chronology of the events that occurred. This will enable the member to decide
whether the entire computer should be examined or just to secure essential ‘data files’. The provisions of
sections 5 and 6 of the Criminal Evidence Act, 1992 may assist in gathering documentary evidence from
computers, otherwise evidence should be secured in its most basic electronic form (i.e. raw ‘data files’).
Advice on identifying and securing digital evidence can be obtained from the Computer Crime Investigation
Unit GBFI.

Search Under Warrant
When conducting a search under warrant the guidelines are as follows:

Preparing for the Search
If possible search preparation should include some consideration of the potential for encountering electronic
devices. This will be highly dependant on the type of case being investigated and the premises being
searched. If it is a fraud investigation and a business premises is being searched, all computers on the premises
may be networked. This may necessitate specialist advice and may even require a specialist being included in

33

chapter 13 final final:Layout 1

04/12/2007

10:58

Page 34

Chapter 13 Fraud

the search team (provisions of section 48(5) Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act 2001). Also ensure
that you have sufficient bags (various sizes), labels and markers and that the persons attending the search are
properly tasked and know what they are looking for.

Conducting the Search
The safety of members is of paramount importance: remember, improper handling of electronic devices can
cause injury.
Secure the Scene
If devices are ‘off’ do not turn ‘on’. Any device that is ‘on’ should be handled with the utmost caution. It is
best to get specialist advice in this instance. Do not allow anyone near the device or allow them to instruct
you on how to use it. Immediate disconnection of a device could close encrypted data areas or disconnect
from ‘online’ storage areas.
Photograph and Label
All devices should be photographed, labelled and sealed in an evidence bag. If photographing is not possible,
make a sketch diagram of the device and cabling (you might have to reassemble the device and connections).
Interviewing Persons
It is advisable that any persons found at the search site be interviewed regarding ownership and use of the
device. If possible, record any statements of suspects in writing after caution if appropriate.
Transport
Great care should be taken when transporting this type of equipment. Advice in this regard should be sought
from the Computer Crime Investigation Unit personnel.
When dealing with complaints or information regarding misuse of the Internet, hacking or criminal damage to
data the member concerned should immediately contact the Computer Crime Investigation Unit.

Requests for the assistance of CCIU in the forensic retrieval of computer based
evidence
When requesting forensic retrieval and examination services the provisions of HQ 01/04 should be complied
with. When the case is assigned for examination, arrangements for delivery of the relevant computer
equipment to CCIU will be communicated to the investigating member. Equipment will not be accepted by
the Unit unless the above procedure has been complied with.
When the device has been copied or examined it will be returned to the investigating member who will be
responsible for its subsequent safe storage. Where devices have been stored for a long period, further advice
may be required to ensure that they remain operational.
Requests for information regarding email and Internet Protocol (IP) addresses must be addressed to Detective
Chief Superintendent Crime & Security Branch Garda Headquarters Phoenix Park Dublin.
The modern investigator must appreciate the evidence potential of information and communications
technology and also have the ability to identify and secure digital evidence from a variety of electronic devices.

13.20. The Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation
The Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation (GBFI) was established in 1995 following the publication of the report
of the Government Advisory Committee on Fraud in 1992. The Bureau is managed by a Detective Chief
Superintendent who reports to Assistant Commissioner National Support Services. The role of the Bureau is
as follows:

34

Page 35

Chapter 13 Fraud

• To investigate serious and complex fraud complaints, cheque and payment card fraud, money
laundering offences, counterfeit currency offences, the investigation of computer crimes and the
forensic retrieval of computer based evidence.
• To investigate criminal breaches of the Companies Acts and the Competition Act through staff seconded
to the Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement and The Competition Authority.
• To collect information on financial crime and to play a proactive role in this area.
• To gather intelligence on fraud-related crime.
• To play a proactive role in the prevention of fraud related crime.

Sections within the Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation
The staff allocated to the Bureau are attached to the following units:
• Assessment Unit
• Commercial Fraud Investigation Units
• Computer Crime Investigation Unit
• Money Laundering Investigation Unit
• Cheque/Payment Card, Counterfeit Currency and Advance Fee Fraud Unit
• Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement (on secondment)
• The Competition Authority (on secondment)
Details on the role and function of the above units are set out at Garda Code 37.12 and further detail is set
out below. Members are also referred to the relevant portions of Garda Code Chapter 23 which contains
instructions in relation to the investigation of certain fraud-related crimes.
The Bureau is staffed by specialist Garda and civilian personnel including two full-time forensic accountants. As
the specialist agency within An Garda Siochana, GBFI targets fraud-related crime. To this end, staff at the
Bureau continue to work closely with the Revenue Commissioners, the Office of the Director of Corporate
Enforcement, The Competition Authority and The Financial Regulator.

13.21. The Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement
Statutory Framework
The Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement (ODCE) was established in November 2001 under Section
7 of the Company Law Enforcement Act 2001.
Functions of the ODCE
The principal functions of the Director of Corporate Enforcement are to:
• Encourage compliance with company law
• Investigate and enforce suspected breaches of the legislation

35

Page 36

Chapter 13 Fraud

Role of GBFI Personnel
In February 2002, a complement of Garda staff attached to the Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation under the
direction of a Detective Inspector was seconded to the ODCE. The principal function of these members is to
investigate breaches of Company Law. These members are designated as “Officers of the Director” and are
issued with an Instrument of Delegation, which confers on them certain powers under the Companies Acts
1963/2003 for use in the performance of their duties.
These members continue to be vested with and may exercise or perform the powers and duties of a member
of An Garda Siochana and remain under the general direction and control of the Commissioner through
Detective Chief Superintendent, GBFI.
Gardai attached to this office also provide support to other members of An Garda Siochana requiring
assistance in relation to investigations where information relating to companies may be useful. They are also
available to advise as required.
Potential complaints, which are exclusively related to company law, may be passed through the appropriate
channels for investigation by this unit.

Common Company Law Offences
The Company Law Enforcement Act 2001 increased the maximum penalty for indictable offences under the
Companies Acts from three to five years’ imprisonment. As a result, where no alternative penalty is disclosed in
the legislation, all company law offences became arrestable offences. The most commonly occurring breaches
include:
• Failure to keep proper books of account (S.202, Companies Act, 1990 as amended)
• Auditing a company while not qualified to do so (S.187, Companies Act, 1990 as amended)
• Acting as an officer of a company while bankrupt (S.183, Companies Act, 1963 as amended)
• Furnishing false information in purported compliance with the Companies Acts (S.242, Companies Act,
1990)
• Fraudulent trading (S297, Companies Act, 1963 as amended)
• Excessive directors’ loans (S.31, Companies Act, 1990 as amended)
• Falsification of books and documents (S.243, Companies Act, 1990 as amended)
• Acting as a director of a company while disqualified or restricted (S.161, Companies Act, 1990 as
amended)

Powers and Duties of the Director of Corporate Enforcement
The Director of Corporate Enforcement is vested with a number of powers to assist in the investigation of
breaches of Company Law. In addition, the Director can delegate all powers to “Designated Officers of the
Director” which include the Gardai attached to the Office. These include the power to apply for a search
warrant under section 20 of the Companies Act 1990, and for orders under section 7a Bankers Book Evidence
Act 1879 for financial institutions in relation to indictable breaches of company law.
The duties of the Director include:

• The initiation of fact-finding company investigations
• The prosecution of persons for suspected breaches of the Companies Acts
• The supervision of companies in official and voluntary liquidation and of un-liquidated insolvent companies
• The restriction and disqualification of directors and other company officers
• The supervision of liquidators and receivers
• The regulation of un-discharged bankrupts acting as company officers

Requests for Assistance
Requests for assistance of a serious nature should be made through Detective Chief Superintendent GBFI.
Where information only is required requests can be made directly to the Garda Unit at:
Office of the Director of Corporate Enforcement

13.22. The Competition Authority
Statutory Framework
The Competition Authority is a statutory body established under the Competition Act 1991. The Competition
(Amendment) Act 1996 criminalised certain offences under sections 4 and 5 of that Act. The Competition Act
2002 increased the penalties for these offences to make them arrestable offences. In addition, fines of up to
€4 million or 10% or turnover are provided for individuals and corporate entities.

Functions of the Competition Authority
The Competition Authority has a number of statutory duties under the Competition Acts, 1991/2002. These
include investigating breaches of Irish and EU competition law, determining whether mergers lessen
competition and the analysis of competition in broad markets or sectors. It investigates anti-competitive
business practices and where appropriate, brings those engaged in such practices before the Courts. Section
30 of the Competition Act 2002 sets out the functions of the Competition Authority.
In order to conduct their investigations, officers of the Competition Authority have power to apply to a judge
of the District Court for a warrant to enter and inspect premises and to seize, copy or take extracts from any
books, documents or records located there in the course of an investigation (Section 45, Competition Act,
2002). The Authority may also summon witnesses to appear before it, question them on oath and require
them to produce any books, documents or records which are within their custody or control.

Role of GBFI Personnel
The GBFI personnel seconded to the Competition Authority are authorised officers pursuant to section 45 of
the Competition Act, 2002. Their role is to assist the Competition Authority in the investigation of the criminal
offences created by the Competition Acts (these are generally referred to as Cartel Offences). The members
assist in the full investigation process from the initial assessment of complaints through to the final disposal of
cases before the courts in appropriate cases.

Common Offences under the Competition Act, 2002
The Price-fixing Cartel
Price-fixing agreements can take many forms. These include agreements to charge the same price in addition
to agreements on discounts, price increases, margins or minimum prices. The objective of a price-fixing
agreement is usually to ensure common net prices. The fact that prices are the same in a particular market, or
that they have moved together, is not sufficient to establish the existence of an illegal agreement under the
Acts. There is a need to identify something more, which establishes that the pattern of price behaviour was
unlikely to have occurred without at least a contact, if not an agreement, between firms.
Warning signs of price-fixing are listed as follows:
• Any evidence that two or more sellers of a particular product have agreed to price their products in a
certain way
• Similar price changes by a number of sellers of very similar products, at or about the same time
• A statement by a firm that it cannot sell to you because of an agreement with another firm
• Price movements which would not be expected in the prevailing circumstances in the absence of some
form of contact
• Price changes which, over time, reveal so regular and systematic a leader/follower situation as to be
inexplicable in the absence of some kind of contact
• The use of similar phrases or explanations used when announcing price changes
• ‘Give away’ phrases such as ‘the industry has decided that margins must be increased to a more
reasonable level’
• Indications of exchanges of information.
The Market-sharing Cartel
As an alternative to a price-fixing cartel, firms can attempt to achieve the same effect by other means. For
example, they may divide up the country between them and agree not to sell in each other’s designated area,
thereby enabling each to set prices knowing that the others will not undercut them. At its simplest, a marketsharing cartel may be no more than an agreement among firms not to approach each other’s customers or not
to sell to those in a particular area. This may involve secretly allocating specific territories to one another or
agreeing lists of which customers are to be allocated to which firm.
The Collusive Tendering Cartel
Collusive tendering is one variant of illegal market sharing agreements. Private companies and public sector
agencies are the usual victims of collusive tendering behaviour. Collusive tendering occurs when two or more
firms agree not to bid against one another to supply goods or services. Such practices are difficult to detect in
practice, certainly from a single tendering round.
Collusive tendering in public sector procurement is particularly harmful to society as it diverts funds which
could be used to provide other worthwhile services to the public.
Information coming to notice regarding breaches of the Competition Acts
Where members source information regarding breaches of the Competition Acts, this information should be
referred through Detective Chief Superintendent, Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation for the attention of the
Garda Unit at the Competition Authority, Parnell House, 14, Parnell Square, Dublin 1.

Chapter 13 Fraud

Appendix I
Recruitment Procedures Checklist
Many frauds are committed by people employed in the organisation concerned. They possess detailed and indepth knowledge of the organisation, its systems and its weaknesses. The recruitment process provides the
initial opportunity to reduce the risk of fraud being committed by an employee.
Steps which can be taken during the recruitment process include:
Advertising:
• When advertising a position make it clear that a person of integrity is sought and that references will be
required.
The interview:
• The interview provides an important means of establishing the integrity of the person involved.
• Ask questions requiring more than a ‘yes’ or ‘no’ answer.
• Pose ethical questions using hypothetical situations, for example: “If you had to decide whether a
particular course of action was ethical or honest how would you go about making that decision?”
• Be conscious that applicants may wish to give you the answer you want to hear.
• Look for signs (including body language, rapid eye movement, nervous touching of the face and so on)
that the interviewee is particularly uncomfortable considering matters of integrity and honesty.
• Use the interview to confirm all details on the application form or CV. Be wary of inconsistencies or
hesitant answers to questions probing the background to the information presented. Any variance from
the detailed information provided should be taken as a warning sign and great care should be exercised
prior to progressing the application.
• Panel interviews (with two or more interviewers), have the advantage of the interviewers being able to
compare notes and having a second opinion readily to hand.
References:
• References provide very useful third party confirmation of your opinion / assessment of an applicant.
• Always take up references. In particular, verify any reference received directly from the candidate with the
referee.
• Assess the referees. Avoid candidates with poor referees.
• Insist on receiving a reference from the current or most recent employer.
• Referees should be contacted by letter. (See Sample Letter to Referee below)
• Remember that many firms have standard references that they issue in response to such requests.
• Follow up referees’ responses by telephone. You will obtain a better understanding of the reference by
talking to the referee. A key question to ask is whether the referee would re-employ the candidate.
Identity and Qualifications:
• Consider obtaining proof of identity, for example driver’s licence or passport.
• Verify qualifications to original certificates or seek confirmation from the qualifying institution.
Contract of Employment
• The contract of employment should specify that the employee is required to act all times with honesty and
integrity
• It should refer to the disciplinary procedures in operation should the employee not adhere to the terms of
the contract.
• It might also refer to the firm’s security policy and the need for the employee to take appropriate action to
apply that policy.
Security Policy:
• Consider having a written security policy for distribution to all staff, including new recruits which includes
the following guidelines and information:

39

04/12/2007

10:58

Page 40

Chapter 13 Fraud

• Employees are responsible to ensure that any assets of the organisation in their charge are properly
safeguarded
• All reasonable precautions are taken to maintain a secure environment
• Contact details for the person to whom any suspicions of fraud or dishonesty should be reported
• In the event of a ‘security alert’ or fraudulent attack, that the employer requires immediate unhindered
access to the employee’s personal work area, desk, briefcase, filing cabinet, personal lockers, computer
equipment (including organisers), electronic files, company car etc, in certain circumstances. In the
event of a suspected fraud, this will facilitate a detailed search of personal and business files etc. which
may provide valuable evidence.
Probation:
• All candidates should be taken on for a period of probation, usually six months.
• Use this period to evaluate the employee’s integrity and honesty.
Ongoing Assessment and Review:
• Put procedures in place to assess and review employees’ performance on a regular basis following the
completion of the probation period.
• Ongoing assessment and review of the employee’s integrity and honesty should be part of this process.
Personal File:
The recruitment process provides the basic information you require to begin building up a personal file for
the successful applicant. In this context, key material to be retained includes:
• A photograph of the employee, copy passport etc
• All information obtained during the recruitment process, application form, CV, verification of
qualifications, references taken up etc
• The signed contract of employment
Personal details will change over time such as when employees move home. It is important therefore to
keep the details recorded on the file up to date. In the event of fraud, information obtained from the
personal file may be very useful to both the Gardaí and the organisation in bring a successful prosecution.
Part-time Employees and Contractors:
Apply the above recruitment procedures consistently. They should also be applied to the hiring of part time
employees and contractors.

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

14.1. Introduction
The evidential value of document and handwriting examination can be of great assistance in all types of Garda
investigations. The investigating Garda or the Scenes of Crime Examiner should never overlook it, and must be
aware of the evidence that may be obtained even from a blank piece of paper left at the scene of a crime.
The document examiner can provide definitive evidence as to whether a document is genuine or false or
whether it has been altered in any way. The document examiner may also give evidence as to who wrote a
questioned document or may be in a position to link one document with another document, one document
with a crime scene or one document with a suspect.
An investigation is more likely to be successful if it is accepted that there is no ‘one best technical aid’,
but that one or more aids will be helpful in the varied circumstances which will be encountered. It is the
collective force of evidence, which is important. Aids are not always substitutes for each other but can
often be complementary.
Whenever a question arises about the evidential value of a document or whether a document might be of
assistance in an investigation, advice should be sought from the Document and Handwriting Examination
Section, Garda Technical Bureau.

14.2. Document examination as an aid to crime investigation
The following is a list of examinations carried out at the Document and Handwriting Examination Section.
• Handwriting Examination: examination and comparison of handwriting to establish authorship or
association between documents
• Signature Examination: to determine if a signature is genuine, freehand copy, traced or copied
• ESDA (Electrostatic Detection Apparatus): examine and interpret indented impressions on paper, card or
other material, which are caused by writing on one document while resting on another
• Altered Documents: examination of a document to establish if an alteration, addition or obliteration has
taken place
• Ink Examination: to differentiate between two inks on a document
• Damaged Documents: examination of documents damaged by fire, chemicals, water or shredding
• Counterfeits: examination of documents to establish if they are genuine or counterfeit
• Typescript Comparison: examination of office-machinery and typed documents
• Office Equipment Identification: to determine the type of printer that was used to print a questioned
document
• Printing Techniques: examination of printed material in an attempt to establish how a document was
produced
• Stamp Impressions: examination of ink stamps and stamp impressions
• Identity Documents: to determine if an identity document is genuine

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

14.3. Handwriting Examination
Handwriting comparison relies on a visual examination
of the characteristics of two pieces of handwriting and an assessment of the similarities and differences
between them. It is only possible to compare handwriting of a similar style and similar letters with one
another i.e. a capital G with a capital G, or a cursive (joined) g with a cursive g and so on. In addition, the
formation of each letter can be influenced by its position in a word. Consequently, initial letters in a word must
be compared with initial letters in the specimen writing and terminal letters with terminal and so on.
While signatures are another form of cursive writing the document examiner tends to treat them separately
from handwriting comparison. Signatures are the piece of writing that people most commonly perform
and often it differs from a person’s standard writing. The individual can adopt a special method of writing
a signature.
There are three different styles of handwriting:
Cursive: Handwriting having letters connected. Sometimes referred
to as longhand or joined writing.
Script: The letters are not connected. The letters generally have the
form of lower case letters of typescript.
Block Lettering: Also referred to as block capitals. Generally all the
letters have capital formations.

14.4. Handwriting specimens
Handwriting specimens may be classified as request specimens or incidental specimens.

Request specimens
Request specimens are those specimens obtained from the suspect specifically for the investigation. The
advantage of the request specimen is that it can be in the wording of the document in question whilst its
disadvantage is that it may be disguised.

Incidental Specimens
Incidental specimens are those written by the suspect in the ordinary course of business, social or domestic
activity. These may be letters, application forms or other provable documents that were written by the
suspect. The advantage of the incidental specimen is that it will be free of disguise, but the fact that it will not
be in the wording of the document in question may be a disadvantage.
Ideally, a combination of both request and incidental specimens should be available for comparison purposes
as this will show the full range of variation in that person’s handwriting.

14.5. Role of investigator
In an investigation where handwriting comparison will be made, one of the functions of the investigating
member is to provide sufficient quantities of request and incidental specimens for comparison.

2

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

14.6. Admissibility of specimens as evidence
It is very important that the investigating member states clearly in his/her report, which specimens can be
proven to be those of the suspect. Differentiation must be drawn between those specimens which can
definitely be used in court and those which can reasonably be assumed to be in the handwriting of the
suspect. Specimens showing evidence of previous criminal record or involvement in previous crime are
not admissible.

14.7. Proof of handwriting
Handwriting may be proved by:
• The admission of the writer or by the evidence of a witness who saw the specimens being written
• Evidence of a witness who is familiar with the handwriting of the accused by having seen the person write
or by having dealt with such correspondence in the ordinary course of business
• Comparison by a skilled witness (handwriting expert) of the disputed writing with any writing proved to
the satisfaction of the Court to be genuine

14.8. Obtaining Request Specimens of Handwriting
The suspect should have an opportunity of becoming accustomed to the surroundings and to the ‘feel’ of the
pen. With this objective in mind they should first be asked to write their name and address and other general
information, as well as a sentence such as “The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog” which contains all
letters of the alphabet.
Suitable paper should be provided. The general rule is to use paper having the same space available for the
disputed writing as that on the questioned document. A separate piece of paper must be used for each
specimen. The questioned writing should be out of view of the suspect and each specimen given should be
out of the view while writing the next one. Unless the writer begins to write in the style required, he must be
shown the style in which he is to write i.e. cursive, script or block. Assistance should not be given on spelling
or paragraphing.
All of the detail in dispute should be dictated, including dates, figures and words. Dictation should be at a
comfortable speed initially with a gradual increase to leave less time for concentration on disguise. A similar
writing instrument to that used on the questioned document should be used. When considerable disguise is
introduced in specimens obtained on a first visit, a second visit may be necessary. Any writing paper in the
possession of the suspect should be taken and preserved for ESDA examination.

14.9. Natural Variation and Disguise
Natural variation is to be found in the handwriting of every individual. Even consecutive signatures can vary (it
is highly unlikely that any person will write his or her own name exactly the same way in an entire lifetime).
Human beings do not function like machines or robots and therefore cannot produce exact replicas.
It is extremely difficult for any writer to disguise consistently their handwriting over a large quantity of writing.
Because of certain differences occasioned by natural variation and the possible differences created by disguise,
significant handwriting habits cannot be determined or the value and force of characteristics known without a
sufficient quantity of specimen writing.

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

14.10. Quantity of specimens required for examination purposes
Investigators are advised to obtain as much specimen writing as possible. Eight or ten signatures in a disputed
signature case would not be excessive. Two pages in the wording of an anonymous letter plus repetition of
the introduction and conclusion are also likely to be needed.
Ideally, a combination of both request and incidental specimens should be available for comparison purposes
as this will show the range of natural variation in that person’s handwriting.

14.11. ESDA examination
The Electrostatic Detection Apparatus (commonly referred to as the ESDA)
is used to detect indented writing on documents. Indented writing is the
impression from the writing instrument left on the sheets of paper below
the one that contains the original writing. The use of this process often
results in the detection of clear impressions, which may be undetected
with oblique lighting or by other methods of visual examination.
By using the ESDA, black and white record transparencies of the
indentations can quickly be obtained. Documents will not be damaged or
contaminated and it does not interfere with other forensic tests or
examinations and the same document can be processed repeatedly.
Indentations can be detected to a depth of four or five pages and may
provide valuable information as to the origin of
a document.

Submitting Documents for ESDA Examination
When forwarding documents for examination by the ESDA process, the following rules should be observed:
• Do not write on top of a document, e.g. by placing it in an envelope and addressing it
• Do not rub a pencil over a document in order to develop the indentations as this will permanently damage
the document
• Documents for examination must not undergo fingerprint examination in advance of examination as this
will destroy the indented impressions
• Be aware that there may be DNA evidence under the flap of the envelope or under the stamp, the
document examiner will remove these if considered worthwhile
• Do not put documents intended for ESDA examination into plastic bags as this may lead to weakened
impressions

Anonymous and Threatening Letters
When a series of anonymous or threatening letters are received by one or more persons, the likelihood of a
successful investigation is increased. When it is anticipated that further letters will be received, the recipients
should be asked not to open them but to deliver them immediately to the investigator so they can be tested
for fingerprint evidence after the ESDA test.
As in many other investigations, establishing the motive of the writer is an important step. The motive, such as
revenge, malice, blackmail or spite, can often be determined from the wording of the letters and the history of
the victim. The wording can also give an indication as to the seriousness of the threat.

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

The recipient should be asked not to tell too many people about receiving the letters as this could sometimes
flush out the suspect when no reaction is observed.
A feature of some of these cases is that in order to avert suspicion the writer may write a letter to themselves.

Statement Examination
ESDA examination is also regularly used to examine Garda statements of evidence, memos of interviews,
custody records and Garda notebooks where there is an allegation of obliteration, addition or any suspicion
of partial or total rewriting of these documents. This examination will be carried out by a Garda Document
Expert on direction of the trial Judge in serious cases or may be carried out by a defence document expert on
an application through the Courts.

14.12. Examination for Alterations
Video Spectral Comparator (VSC)
The VSC is a piece of equipment which incorporates various light sources (ultra-violet, infrared, transmitted,
oblique, and blue/green lighting) and filters as well as colour and black & white digital cameras. It allows an
examiner to differentiate inks and substances, to visualise hidden security features or faded writing and to
reveal any alteration or obliteration on a document. The VSC is used to examine many different types of
suspect documents including cheques, currency, passports, identity cards, driving licences, vehicle documents,
disputed contracts and so on.
The use of different Light Sources in Document Examination
• Paper comparison: distinction can be made between various types of papers with different reactions under
ultra-violet light.
• Alterations: if the document has been altered by mechanical erasure or chemical obliteration, the VSC may
be able to read the original entry.
• Inks: two inks which appear to the eye to be the same can sometimes be differentiated when examined
under specialised lighting conditions.
• Secret inks: secret communications by physiological fluids such as urine, milk and saliva can easily be
detected under these lights.
• Re sealed envelopes: an envelope which has been steamed and re sealed with a different adhesive may be
revealed under ultra violet or blue/green light.
• Security Features: the VSC is used to examine and compare the security features in identity documents and
other documents of value.
• Unopened letters: if the paper is transparent to infrared and the ink is non transparent to this radiation, it
may be possible to read the contents of the letter without opening it.
• Charred documents: burned documents can sometimes be deciphered by infrared. Success depends upon
the nature of the ink or pencil and the degree of charring.
• Faded writing: naturally faded documents can usually be read under specialised lighting conditions.

14.13. Ink Analysis
Most examinations relating to
ink identification and comparison
are performed by the Document
and Handwriting Examination
Section of the Garda Technical
Bureau where a stereo
microscope and the VSC are
used to assess the similarities and
differences between the
characteristics of two inks.
On occasion there may be some
additional examination required
where the Forensic Science
Laboratory may be able to assist.
Typically the Forensic Science
Laboratory is asked to compare
inks using chromatographic
techniques. This may allow the
scientist to discriminate between
inks which are indistinguishable
by microscopy or by the range of
examination techniques available
in the Handwriting and
Documents Section.
The amount of ink required for a comparison is small but it should be noted that ink analysis by
chromatography is a destructive technique. If the ink is on a piece of paper, a section measuring about 1
millimetre square may be required for each test. In addition, some experimentation is often required to
establish the best conditions for analysis (which solvents work best and so on), and this will consume more of
the reference material.

Requirements
A sample of the questioned material, such as handwriting or an ink sample, is required as well as a sample
of the known material - a pen or another sample of writing, for example. A control sample of the paper on
which the writing was done is required to allow for any interference from the substrate or background.
Usually a sample of writing is submitted to the laboratory and the scientist will remove a small amount of
ink using a scalpel blade or other small implement.
If items are to be examined for finger-marks or other evidence, this should be stated clearly when they
are submitted.

Limitations
Inks are mass-produced materials and finding that two inks are similar does not mean that they necessarily
came from the same pen or even from the same type of pen, as the same ink may be used in more than
one type of pen. If the inks did come from the same pen, it does not mean that the writing was necessarily
contemporaneous. Equally, finding that the inks are different does not mean that they were necessarily
written at different times.
For a valid comparison, the questioned and known samples should have the same history with respect to
exposure to heat, light and so on.

6

04/12/2007

Page 7

Chapter 14 Examination of Handwriting and Documents

It is not possible to date inks with precision in order to say if two samples of writing were produced at a
similar time. There is published literature indicating that it may be possible with some ballpoint inks to
determine whether a sample is recent (less than six to eight months old) or old (more than two years), but
the Forensic Science Laboratory has not employed this technique for ink examination.

Sample Submission
Samples of writing should be protected from physical damage and exposure to light. As outlined above,
if the samples are to be examined for other evidence types (e.g. fingerprints, DNA) then this should be
stated clearly.

14.14. Typescript Examination
Even though the use of typewriters is in decline the comparison of typescript may prove to be useful in tracing
the origin of anonymous letters or in establishing that an addition or deletion has occurred in certain
documents. The typewriter is still regularly used to alter the details on various documents. Examples include
the amount on a cheque or the details on a passport following photo substitution. The success of the
examination is dependent on the supply of adequate suitable specimen typescript or the suspect typewriter.
The basis for the expression of an opinion on the origin of typescript is provided by those defects in the
typewriter which are permanent. These will either have existed since the manufacture of the machine or
evolved through use and abuse of the typewriter. It is important to note that defects such as damage to
letters, uneven inking or lateral and horizontal misalignment must be permanent. If possible, the typewriter
should be brought to the Document Section along with any type elements such as printwheels or golf balls,
correcting tapes, sheets, ribbons and so on. If the typewriter cannot be forwarded, evidential material should,
if possible, be acquired in accordance with the following guidelines:
• Remove the ribbon or cartridge and replace with another ribbon or cartridge
• Type the contents of the suspect document three times, keeping the same layout
• Type out the entire keyboard three times
Documents known to have been typed on the same typewriter at the same time as the suspect document
should, if available, be submitted for examination. Electronic typewriters and word processors may contain
valuable information which is liable to be lost if the machine is disconnected. Advice on how this information
can be retrieved should be sought before disconnecting. It is also possible to read a typewriter ribbon and get
a typescript of the contents or to search a typewriter ribbon for a specific document.

14.15. Office Equipment Identification
The Document Examiner is able to give an indication as to what type of printer was used to print a document,
and where a colour photocopier or colour laser printer was used may even be able to establish the make,
model and serial number of the particular machine through a process known bitmap analysis.
Photocopied documents can be examined and linked to other documents or may be directly linked with an
individual photocopier.
It should also be noted that where a computer has been used to design or produce a questioned document,
evidence of this remains on the computer hard drive even after being deleted by the suspect. The Computer
Crime Unit of the Garda Bureau of Fraud Investigation can recover this information.

14.16. Examination of a Document to Determine Authenticity
The Document and Handwriting Examination Section examine
an exhaustive list of documents to determine their authenticity.
These include passports, travel documents, currency, money
orders, postal orders, cheques, driving licences, birth certificates,
insurance certificates, tax discs, utility bills, concert tickets and
match tickets. All members of the section have been trained in
security printing and the recognition of counterfeit and
forged documents.
A counterfeit may be described as a complete reproduction of a genuine document.
A forgery may be described as a genuine document that has been unlawfully altered.

Detecting Counterfeit and Forged Documents
In order to combat counterfeiting and forgery of valuable documents various security features are incorporated
into them. These security features serve to prevent the unlawful copying or alteration of the document. They
include watermarks, ultraviolet features, security threads, intaglio printing, microprinting, optically variable
inks, scrambled indicia, holograms and so on. Absence of these security features may be a good indication
that the document is not genuine.
The Document & Handwriting Examination Section collect genuine specimens of all regularly used documents
and have access to various databases to assist them in the recognition of counterfeit documents. These
databases include Edison, FADO, Interpol, Europol.
In cases of counterfeit currency an expert can give evidence on whether or not a note is counterfeit as well as
complete background information on that particular counterfeit as outlined in the European Central Bank
Database known as the Counterfeit Monitoring System (CMS).
For additional information regarding counterfeit and forgery, refer to chapter 13, Theft and Fraud.

Why do People Counterfeit/Forge Documents?
In many instances the explanation is simply for monetary gain. For example, counterfeiting currency or altering
a cheque amount. In relation to identity documents there can be many reasons, including the following:
• To conceal their own identity
• To assume another identity
• To conceal an adverse immigration or travel history
• To benefit from the previous holder’s status
• To avoid a visa requirement
• To avoid an issuing authority
• As part of a people smuggling or trafficking operation

Identity Documents
Ireland has become a multicultural society so every member of An Garda Siochana requires a basic knowledge
of how to recognise a counterfeit or altered passport, identity card or foreign driving licence when it is
produced to the Garda or located during a the course of a search.

14.16.1. A Quick Guide to Checking the Validity of a Passport
• Check the cover and overall construction of the document. Is the gold blocking well defined and well
aligned and do all the pages line up?
• Check the watermark. Use transmitted light (hold it up to the light).
• Use Ultra Violet (UV) light. The watermark should not react to it.
• Check the quality of the print.
• Check for spelling mistakes.
• Check the stamps and visas in the document as visas are secure documents in themselves and will have
security features to the same standard as the passport.
Checking for evidence of forgery
• Check to see have any of the pages been substituted.
• Do all the pages align?
• Is the stitching correct?
• Do all the pages react in the same way when exposed to Ultra Violet Light? They should in a genuine
document.
• Do all the pages have the same serial numbers?
• Are all the pages present?
Checking for evidence of photograph/image substitution
• Check the authentication.
• Check that the safeguards in the laminate are present and undamaged.
• Check that there is only one laminate.
• Look for any cut marks around the photograph.
• Check that the person presenting the passport is the person in the photograph.
• Remember that there are thousands of stolen blank documents in circulation.
• Allow for the fact that the passport may have been applied for fraudulently (i.e. it may be a genuine
passport which was obtained by presenting other fraudulent documents).

14.17. Submission of documents for examination at the
Document and Handwriting Examination Section
Where a number of members in a particular investigation receive documents which are to be technically
examined the State Solicitor’s advice is as follows:

Specimens of handwriting or documents taken possession of should be initialled and dated to facilitate their
subsequent identification. If the document is an original this reference should be attached in a label. No
additions or alterations should ever be made to an original even by punching or stapling. When several
documents are in the possession of different members each member should mark his own group. The
documents should then be handed over to one member who will deliver or forward them to the Document
and Handwriting Examination Section, Garda Technical Bureau with a completed Form C56 detailing
every exhibit.
In important and urgent cases such as murder or forgery documents may be handed over personally to the
Document Examiner and subsequently entered as an exhibit on the Forensic Liaison Office (FLO) recording
system. While the report in relation to the matter under investigation and copies of documents should be
forwarded through the Superintendent, original documents should always be sent direct to the Technical
Bureau, thus preserving continuity of possession of exhibits.

14.18. Care of documents
Care of documents which might be of evidential value could prove to be paramount in proving a crime against
an individual. The following precautions should be taken:
• Handle the questioned document as little as possible. Consider the value of having each document
examined for fingerprints after document examination. Handle at corners or edges only.
• When posting documents, protect with paper or cardboard and only post using registered post for
continuity purposes. However best practice is to hand deliver or deliver through the local exhibit officer.
• Keep documents away from excessive heat, light or dampness.
• Retain notes of the history of each document including origin and names of others who have
handled them.
• Take care that additional indentations are not applied to documents through writing inadvertently when
documents to be examined are underneath your own writing paper. Any writing on envelopes should be
done before document is inserted.
• Do not perforate, pin, clip or staple or make new folds in documents.

14.19. Labelling of Documents
In addition to initials and date for subsequent identification, the following labelling instructions should be
followed:
(i)

Questioned Documents should be suitably packaged and labelled A1, A2, A3, etc.

(ii) Incidental Specimens should be marked B1, B2, B3, etc.
(iii) Request Specimens should be marked C1, C2, C3, etc., in the order in which they were written.
It is advisable to have the paper to be used for taking handwriting specimens labelled appropriately before
commencing to take any of the specimens.

14.20. Accompanying Documentation
Documents being submitted to the Document and Handwriting Examination Section for examination should
be accompanied by a completed C56 Form with all exhibits listed. A brief report outlining the facts of the case
and the type of examinations required should also accompany the exhibits. All exhibits must be clearly
labelled, indicating whether they are questioned documents, incidental specimens or request specimens and
be appropriately packaged to protect the documents and the evidence which they may contain.

Chapter 15 International Liaison

15.1. Liaison and Protection Section, Crime and Security Branch
(Interpol/Europol/Sirene)
The Liaison and Protection Section, Crime and
Security Branch is the primary section within An
Garda Siochana which provides international law
enforcement liaison. It also deals with national
policy direction and implementation on the
protection of resident and visiting dignitaries, the
security of key installations and buildings and the
witness security programme.
Liaison and Protection Section encompasses a
number of sections under the control of Detective
Chief Superintendent, Liaison and Protection.

15.2. Interpol
Formed in 1923, the International Criminal Police Organisation - Interpol is a worldwide organisation whose
aim is to promote and facilitate enquiries into criminal activities. One of its primary objectives is to ensure that
major criminals who operate at international level are arrested and successfully prosecuted. In simple terms,
Interpol coordinates action between police forces of member states.
According to Article 2 of the Interpol constitution its aims are to:
• “Ensure and promote the widest possible mutual assistance between all criminal police authorities,
within the limits of the laws existing in the different countries and within the spirit of the Universal
Declaration of Human Rights
• Establish and develop all institutions likely to contribute effectively to the prevention and suppression
of ordinary law crimes.”
The limits of Interpol’s action are laid down in Article 3:

“It is strictly forbidden for the organisation to undertake any intervention of a
political, military, religious or racial character.”
Interpol has 186 member countries and through its General Secretariat in Lyon, France is the principal channel
of communications between the police forces of member countries in connection with crime enquiries. The
five priority crime areas are fugitives, public safety and terrorism, drugs and organised crime, trafficking in
human beings, and financial and high tech crime.
Interpol has recently undertaken a vigorous regional modernisation programme which now electronically links
all of the 186 National Central Bureaus (NCBs) on a highly encrypted e-mail communications network. This
system, known as I-24/7, allows large files, including photographs and fingerprints, to be exchanged rapidly
and it also allows instant remote access to Interpol’s databases.

1

chapter 15 final:Layout 1

Chapter 15 International Liaison

Interpol databases include:
• Nominals (hundreds of thousands of records on wanted, suspect and missing persons)
• Stolen and lost travel documents (millions of records)
• Stolen motor vehicles (millions of records)
Other databases include fingerprints, DNA profiles and fraudulent payment cards.

Interpol Notices
There are seven Interpol Notices which are colour coded as follows:
• Red gives details of persons who are wanted for arrest with a view to extradition. (This has no legal
basis in Ireland where original arrest warrants must be processed by the Department of Justice, Equality
and Law Reform.)
• Blue seeks to locate or trace a person prior to a decision being made about whether to request arrest
with a view to extradition. It is also used to highlight individuals travelling abroad who are suspected of
major criminal activity. In effect it is an international flagging system.
• Green provides warnings and criminal intelligence about persons who have committed criminal offences
and are likely to repeat those crimes in other countries.
• Yellow gives details of missing persons.
• Black seeks information about unidentified dead bodies.
• Orange warns of possible threats from hidden weapons, parcel bombs and other dangerous materials.
The Interpol-United Nations Security Council Special Notice alerts, police to groups and individuals who
are the targets of UN sanctions against Al Qaeda and the Taliban.
One traditional barrier to international law enforcement cooperation has been language differences. Interpol
overcomes this problem by working in four official languages; English, French, Spanish and Arabic. Interpol
Dublin has officers who are fluent in French and Spanish.

Interpol Assistance
Assistance is provided in the following ways:
• Transmit police investigation files or reports with requests for enquiries in other countries
• Seek permission from other NCBs when members of An Garda Síochána need to travel abroad to pursue
investigations (10 days notice is required)
• Publish international notices about criminals wanted on warrant or for the purpose of seeking further
information on suspects
• Circulate details of missing persons
• Carry out real-time checks on the various Interpol databases
• Obtain previous conviction information on foreign nationals (fingerprints are required for proof of ID)
• Assist with the identification of criminals with false identities

• Circulate/seek information about the activities of international criminals
• Circulate details of stolen valuable works of art
• Circulate details of interesting modus operandi
• Circulate particulars to assist the identification of dead bodies
• Transmit compassionate messages
Communication with Interpol, Dublin should take place via Garda email to interpol@garda.ie. Where such
email facilities are not available members should seek assistance from Interpol in writing via the usual channels.
An Interpol reference number, if known, should be quoted on all communications.
It should be noted that in certain cases where material is required as evidence a Mutual Assistance Request
will be necessary, as such material cannot be obtained on a police to police basis (see below at 15.5).
Where a foreign national is arrested or is suspected of being involved in a criminal matter contact should be
made with the Interpol office at the earliest opportunity. Interpol, Dublin will then begin the process of
checking Interpol databases to verify identity, status (wanted or suspected), authenticity of identity documents,
status of vehicles, and so on. Contact is then made with the country of origin or residence to obtain details of
criminal history if any.

Requests to Interpol
Requests to Interpol should include the following:
• Reason for enquiry (must be crime related)
• Full name of subject (family name and forenames)
• Date of birth (In DD/MM/YYYY format)
• Passport number (include copy)
• Identity document details (include copy)
• Fingerprints (essential for previous convictions. NB Faxed copies are not suitable)
• Contact details of requesting member
• The Member State(s) to which the request should be sent
When making a request of Interpol members should be aware of the time factor and be prompt.
The Irish National Central Bureau (NCB) is based at Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park under Detective Chief
Superintendent, Liaison & Protection.
The full address is:
Interpol, Liaison & Protection, Crime & Security, Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park, Dublin 8. Fax: 01
6662891. www.interpol.int.

15.3. Europol
Europol is the European Union law enforcement organisation that handles criminal intelligence. Its aim is to
improve the effectiveness and co operation between the competent authorities of the Member States in
preventing and combating serious international organised crime.

Europol Method of Operation
Each EU Member State has liaison officers attached to Europol
in The Hague. Requests for information/intelligence to/from
both Europol and the law enforcement intelligence services
within the EU are sent from the Europol National Unit to the
liaison officer at Europol who communicates directly with
Europol or with the other Liaison Officers of the intelligence
services of the Member States as the case may be.
Ireland has two Europol Liaison Officers based at the Irish
Liaison Bureau at Europol HQ, The Hague. All communication
is via a secure encrypted e-mail link.
Europol supports Member States by:
• Facilitating the exchange of information, in accordance with national law, between Europol and the EU
Member States. The Liaison Officers interact with Europol and their counterparts from the different
Member States to facilitate the flow of intelligence and assist investigations at national level
• Providing operational analysis in support of Member States’ operations
• Generating strategic reports (threat assessments) and crime analysis on the basis of information and
intelligence supplied by Member States, generated by Europol or gathered from other sources
• Providing expertise and technical support for investigations and operations carried out within the EU,
under the supervision and the legal responsibility of the Member States concerned
Europol is also active in promoting crime analysis and harmonisation of investigative techniques within
Member States.
Europol’s mandate currently extends only to the European Union but is being extended at political level. It can
only deal with matters where there are factual indications that an organised criminal structure is involved and
two or more Member States are affected. If in any doubt The Europol National Unit will assist in this area.

Europol National Unit
Each Member State develops a Europol National Unit (ENU) which is the only liaison body between Europol
and the competent national authorities. In Ireland, the competent national authorities are An Garda Siochana
and the Customs service.
The Irish Europol National Unit is based within Liaison and Protection, Crime and Security at Garda
Headquarters, Phoenix Park. It comes under the control of Detective Superintendent, Liaison, with Detective
Chief Superintendent Liaison and Protection, the appointed Head of the National Unit.

4

chapter 15 final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:40

Page 5

Chapter 15 International Liaison

It is the task of the ENU to:
• Supply Europol on its own initiative with the information and intelligence necessary for it to carry out
its tasks
• Respond to Europol's requests for information, intelligence and advice
• Keep information and intelligence up to date
• Evaluate information and intelligence in accordance with national law for the competent authorities
and transmit this material to them
• Issue requests for advice, information, intelligence and analysis to Europol
• Supply Europol with information for storage in its computerised system
• Ensure compliance with the law in every exchange of information between it and Europol

A national unit is not obliged in a particular case to supply the information and
intelligence if this would mean:
• Harming essential national security interests
• Jeopardising the success of a current investigation or the safety of individuals
• Divulging information pertaining to organisations or specific intelligence activities in the field
of State security.

Data Protection
The Data Protection Act, 1988 (as amended) applies and has effect with any necessary modifications to the
collection, processing, keeping, use or disclosure of certain information relating to an individual that is
processed electronically.
The Data Protection Commissioner is designated as the national supervisory body for the purposes of the
Europol Act and the Europol Convention.

Guidelines for submitting requests to Europol
Requests for information should be submitted to Detective Superintendent, Europol National Unit, Liaison and
Protection, Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park. Details relating to the request should be submitted
electronically where possible. The request should contain the following:
• Background of investigation/reason for request
• Details of person/vehicle/modus operandi/organisation etc. on which information is required (as much
detail as possible should be given)
• Photographs and fingerprints should accompany the request where available (electronically if possible faxed copies are not suitable)
• Specify the Member State(s) to which the request should be sent if possible
Requests for general searches with all Member States are also possible. In urgent cases, members may contact
the ENU by telephone.

5

Page 6

Chapter 15 International Liaison

15.4. Sirene Bureau (Schengen Project Office)
Ireland has been a member of Schengen since 2000. The Schengen Agreement and Convention provide for a
framework of law enforcement measures to counter any possible increase in criminality as a consequence of
the removal of internal border controls within the Schengen area, which includes all 27 EU Member States,
along with Iceland and Norway.
Operating through the Sirene Bureau, each Member State is directly linked to the Schengen Information
System, which allows for the searching of certain information on each of the Member State’s national police
computer systems on a 24/7 basis.
While An Garda Siochana has yet to connect to the Schengen Information System, all matters relating to
Ireland’s membership of Schengen are managed at the Sirene Bureau/Schengen Project Office at Garda HQ,
Phoenix Park.

15.5. Mutual Assistance Requests
Increasingly, foreign jurisdictions require receipt of a formal letter of request for mutual assistance before
undertaking investigative measures at the request of another jurisdiction. Such measures may include:
• the search of premises
• the questioning of suspects or witnesses
• the uplifting information by way of Court Order (e.g. bank account details or telephone records)
Ireland is a signatory to various international conventions which govern the provision of mutual legal assistance
between jurisdictions. The fact that a country is not a signatory to a particular convention may not be a bar to
the provision of mutual legal assistance in any given case.

The Irish Central Authority
All requests to and from Ireland must be transmitted via The Central Authority for Mutual Assistance at
the Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform. The Mutual Assistance Section at Crime, Policy and
Administration Section, Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park deals with such requests on behalf of An Garda
Siochana. The Central Authority works closely with the Attorney General’s Office, the Director of Public
Prosecutions, the Chief State Solicitor and An Garda Siochana in the performance of its role.
The Central Authority will consider the legal aspects of each request and obtain specific legal advice as
necessary to deal with the request. On occasions, requested material such as Irish bank account details will be
uplifted by way of Court Order in Ireland. Similar procedures will arise in the case where the Gardai are
requested to conduct searches under warrant by the requesting state.

Relevant Irish Law
The law which allows Ireland to provide mutual assistance to, and to ask for mutual assistance from other
countries is contained in Part VII of the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as amended. The law includes provisions
which allow Ireland to:
• Take evidence in connection with criminal investigations or proceedings in another country
• Search for and seize material on behalf of another country
• Provide mutual assistance in relation to revenue offences

6

chapter 15 final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:40

Page 7

Chapter 15 International Liaison

• Serve a summons or any other court process on a person in Ireland to appear as a defendant or as a
witness in another country
• Serve a document recording a court decision on a person in Ireland
• Enforce, in Ireland, confiscation and forfeiture orders made in another country
• Transfer a person imprisoned in Ireland to another country to give evidence in criminal proceedings
there, to be identified there, or to assist proceedings there.

The Garda Mutual Assistance Section
The Mutual Assistance Section at Crime Policy and Administration Section, Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park
processes all such requests on behalf of An Garda Siochana in accordance with the relevant legal procedures
as set out in each case.

Police to Police Requests
Some requests for assistance in investigations can be executed exclusively on the basis of police to police cooperation. The forms of co-operation which can be obtained through police channels include interviewing
witnesses or suspects in criminal investigations where the person to be interviewed is willing to co-operate and
provide an unsworn statement. This procedure may also be used in the following cases:
• sharing information concerning investigations into offences which have been committed in Ireland,
where circumstances permit
• providing details of previous convictions
• providing details of motor vehicles registered in Ireland
• providing details of driving licences issued in Ireland
• obtaining medical or dental statements or records where the patient has given written consent
In cases where it is proposed to use the requested material in evidence in a criminal prosecution, the relevant
material should generally be obtained through the Mutual Assistance procedure. The police-to-police process
is usually more appropriate for intelligence purposes subject to the details above. The advice/directions of the
Central Authority/Director of Public Prosecutions should of course be complied with in any given case.

Incoming Requests
Any aspects of requests received which can be dealt with on a police to police basis should be forwarded to
Chief Superintendent, Mutual Assistance Section, Crime Policy and Administration for attention. The section
will liaise with the relevant Garda Unit concerned to ensure that the request is dealt with.

Outgoing Mutual Assistance Requests
Where investigating Gardai require the issue of a formal Mutual Assistance Request to a foreign jurisdiction,
they should forward a request to Chief Superintendent, Crime Policy and Administration, Garda Headquarters,
Phoenix Park in accordance with the contents of HQ directive 81/04. In general, this procedure is invoked
where it is proposed to uplift evidence abroad for use in criminal proceedings in Ireland.
This directive contains a template request and it should be completed under the headings as set out by the
investigating member. It should be noted that the request is made out in the name of the Director of Public
Prosecutions as the Irish Judicial Authority for the purposes of Article 24 of the Council of Europe Convention
on Mutual Assistance in Criminal Matters (1959) and its additional protocols. The draft request when

Chapter 15 International Liaison

completed should be forwarded through the usual channels for the attention of Mutual Assistance Section at
Garda Headquarters. The staff at Mutual Assistance Section will then liaise with the Director of Public
Prosecutions with a view to having the request issued. Once approved by the Director of Public Prosecutions,
the request will be forwarded to the Central Authority at the Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform
for transmission to the competent authorities of the relevant State.
A copy of this draft template is set out at Appendix A at the end of this chapter.
Translation of the request will be arranged as necessary. The request should always be forwarded to a state
translated into the native language of that state where this language is not English.

15.6. Service of Summons and Judicial Documents Inside and
Outside the State
All summonses and judicial documents being served in the state at the request of foreign jurisdictions and
outside the jurisdiction on behalf of the state must be served in accordance with International Mutual
Assistance Conventions. All such documents, whether incoming or outgoing, are transmitted through the
Central Authority at the Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform and ultimately are either generated
or served by An Garda Siochana. The Mutual Assistance Section, Garda HQ organises the service of incoming
requests and the transmission to the Central Authority of outgoing requests.

15.7. Visits by Foreign Police Forces to this Jurisdiction
All requests by foreign police forces to visit this jurisdiction for the purpose of carrying out investigations must
be received by Interpol Section, Garda HQ. Currently, the following procedures are in operation. Once a
request is received at Interpol it is forwarded to the Mutual Assistance Section. Generally these requests involve
interviews with witnesses or suspects and involve the taking of statements. The Mutual Assistance Section
ensures that the witness/suspect concerned is agreeable to the interview. Once this is established a suitable
liaison officer is nominated to liaise with the visiting police. Permission is then sought from Assistant
Commissioner, Crime and Security for the visit to proceed and the response forwarded to Interpol Section in
order that the requesting police force may be informed.

15.8. Joint Investigation Teams
Joint investigation teams may be set up for a specific purpose and limited period by mutual agreement of the
competent authorities of two or more Member States in order to carry out criminal investigations with a cross
border dimension in one or more of the Member States setting up the team.
The Commissioner of An Garda Síochána has been designated as a Competent Authority in Ireland for the
purposes of issuing and receiving requests for the establishment of Joint Investigation Teams. The
administration of all incoming and outgoing requests for the establishment of Joint Investigation Teams has
been delegated by the Commissioner to the Mutual Assistance Section.
