Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

16.1. Introduction
The Forensic Science Laboratory (FSL) is an office of the Department of Justice, Equality and Law Reform with
responsibility for the provision of impartial scientific evidence following examination of items from crime
scenes. It was established in 1975 and has been located in Garda Headquarters since 1979. The majority of
the staff have qualifications in the relevant branches of biology and chemistry. The work is carried out in four
functional areas; biology, chemistry, DNA and drugs.

Biology
This area examines biological samples and is mainly
involved in offences against the person such as
assaults, murder and sexual assaults.

Chemistry
This area deals with physical examinations and
chemical analysis and is mainly involved in offences
against property including burglaries and armed
robberies.

DNA
This involves DNA analysis of reference samples
from suspects and victims as well as samples from
scenes.

Drugs
This area deals with the analysis of material
suspected of contravening the Misuse of Drugs
Acts.
Members of An Garda Síochána submit items for examination to the Laboratory. The scientific staff examine
the items, perform relevant tests and issue a statement or certificate and return the items to the Gardai.
Samples are prioritised on receipt largely on the basis of the seriousness of the offence being investigated
and/or urgency and whether a scientific examination can help advance the case.

16.2. Role of Forensic Science Laboratory in Criminal
Investigations
The FSL helps most investigations either by the identification of substances such as illegal drugs, accelerants or
firearm residues or by finding evidence of a link between people, between a person and a scene or between
items. Substances identified in the laboratory are listed (Table 1). Evidence of links is usually found through
traces of substances such as body fluids, fibres, paint and glass. When a scientist finds evidence that supports
the suggestion of a link, they will offer an interpretation of the strength of the evidence.
Additional analytical services provided by the FSL include a toxicological screen for drugs and alcohol in blood
and urine of living persons, analysis of mobile phone records and identification of vehicles from fragments left
at the scene of a hit and run. The FSL also supplies dyes and markers to help identify culprits where there is on
going pilfering of money, fuel or other items.

The investigator needs to consider what examinations will advance his case and submit the relevant items.
The FSL will help an investigator to do this in complex cases. The items need to be seized or sampled and
packaged according to the FSL guidelines (Table 2). Correct procedures to avoid contamination must be used.

16.3. Selection of cases where the Forensic Science Laboratory
can assist
Drug cases form approximately 80% of the cases
submitted to the FSL. The majority of these are
Section 3 cases and will only be processed if a
summons has been issued. The remaining cases
consist of sexual assaults, burglaries, assault,
murder, road traffic accidents and arsons amongst
others. Contrary to popular opinion, forensic
science can assist in only a relatively small
proportion of these investigations.
The value that forensic science can add depends
on the case circumstances. Thus a laboratory
examination may not be necessary for a street
fight with lots of witnesses, good CCTV footage,
statement of admissions or combinations of this
type of corroborative evidence. However, the FSL may be able to assist if a stranger has assaulted a victim and
Gardaí have picked up a suspect in the proximity of the scene.
At the time of submission, the Gardaí need to provide details of the case circumstances, the witness accounts
and a suspect’s account if available. This information enables the reception staff and the scientists to assess
whether a forensic examination will advance the query and which items are relevant for the examination. The
Garda making the submission will be asked to complete a brief questionnaire designed to capture the relevant
information. Should this information turn out to be incorrect or if additional relevant information is revealed,
it is important that this is passed onto the FSL.

2

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 3

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

It is important that members of An Garda Síochána do not submit cases that cannot be advanced through a
forensic examination as this allows the FSL to provide a faster service in the cases where it can help. Similarly,
Gardaí should inform the FSL when an examination is no longer required following a plea or a decision not
to prosecute.

16.4. Quality
Most of the scientific procedures carried out by the FSL have been accredited by the Irish National
Accreditation Board to ISO 17025, the agreed international standard for forensic science laboratories. To
maintain this standard, the FSL regularly audits its procedures to ensure compliance and constantly monitors
the quality of the statements or certificates. The FSL also participates in international proficiency trials where
the same test samples are sent to different institutions to check if all participating organisations are getting
the same result. Staff undertake internal and external training and have to be formally assessed and declared
competent in a technique before they can use it for casework. The FSL makes every effort to keep up to date
with trends in the forensic scientific field. Staff are encouraged to keep abreast of the scientific literature and
to attend relevant scientific conferences.

16.5. Scenes
The types of scene most frequently attended are those which involve fatalities but scientists also go to
explosive cases and suspect drug factories. Examples of cases where the attendance at a scene by a scientist is
unlikely to be beneficial would be shootings where there has been no contact between victim and assailant.
Another example would be deaths following street violence.
The FSL assists in training Garda Scene of Crime examiners and provides hands-on practice at mock scenes.

16.6. Additional services provided by the Forensic Science
Laboratory
Forensic scientists attend scenes where there is mutual
agreement that a scientist can add value to the investigation.
Gardaí may request a scientist’s attendance at a scene either by
contacting Command and Control, telephoning the FSL
management during office hours or the On Call Coordinator
(OCC) 086 8393705. See 8.9 for further information on the
role of the FSL in relation to scenes.
The FSL provides advice to Gardaí who ring with queries during
office hours. Queries outside of office hours should be directed
to the Help Line 086 8106513.
Scientists are available for consultation about complex or
difficult cases and will attend case conferences if necessary.
They also participate with AGS in cold case reviews to identify
where recently developed technology may assist.
The FSL advises An Garda Síochána on the contents of kits for forensic examinations which are distributed by
the Forensic Liaison Office. These include kits for the medical examination of complainants and suspects in
sexual assault cases, for swabbing suspects for firearm residues and explosives and for taking hair samples.

16.7. Examinations carried out by Forensic Science Laboratory
Some of the FSL analyses are included in other chapters and can be located through Table 1. Details of the
following examinations are provided in the rest of this chapter.
• Blood
• Body fluids other than blood and semen including
faeces, saliva, urine and vomit
• DNA
• Drugs
• Dyes and markers
• Explosives
• Fibres
• Hair
• Miscellaneous materials including:
• Adhesives and glues
• Plastic bags
• Tinfoil, paper and plastic film,
• Tape (sellotape, duct, insulating etc.)
• Mobile phones
• Paint
• Semen and sexual assaults
• Soil
• Tachograph

This not an exhaustive list as the FSL has the capability to address unusual or non-routine requirements.
Services not provided by the FSL include HIV testing which is done by the Virus Reference Laboratory in
University College Dublin. Beaumont Hospital or the State Laboratory in Backweston provides toxicological
analyses of samples from post-mortems.

16.8. Precautions Against Contamination
From the forensic science perspective, contamination occurs when any trace evidence (fibres, paint, blood and
so on) transfers to exhibits or the scene after the crime was committed and prior to the forensic analysis.
There are four main sources of contamination:
• The crime scene examiner, any person at the scene or any person close to an exhibit may inadvertently
transfer his or her own DNA, hair, or clothing fibres.
• An exhibit or person may be contaminated through contact with an unclean surface. For example,
foreign DNA could transfer to the deceased from the mortuary table.
• Primary transfer of trace evidence through direct contact between items from different aspects of
the case or between victim and suspect after the incident.

4

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 5

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

• Secondary transfer of evidence caused, for example, by the same Garda handling items from different
aspects of a case or packaging items from different persons or scenes in the same room.

Precautions to Avoid Contamination
• The crime scene examiner or exhibit handler should wear clean, disposable over-garments, gloves and
mask until the items are packed and sealed.
• Never talk over an exposed item. It is very easy to transfer one’s own DNA via droplets of saliva.
• Examination surfaces such as tables or mortuary slabs should be thoroughly cleaned before use and
clean plastic sheeting or paper used as a protective barrier for the item or body.
• Items should be packed, sealed and labelled prior to removal from the scene and not re-opened prior
to submission to the FSL.
• Ideally, a different Garda should deal with different sides of the case. For instance, the Garda who
arrests a suspect for stealing a car should not examine or have contact with the car unless they have
changed their outer clothing and gloves.
• Victim and suspect should not be transported in the same patrol car or interviewed in the same room.
• The Garda selected to take samples from suspects should not have recently handled bulk amounts of
the suspect material, e.g. bulk drugs, explosives or firearms. The same applies to the Garda who seizes
items for trace analysis of these substances.
• Samples of liquid accelerants should be packed and stored separately from samples of fire debris.
Such samples should be packed in suitable, non-plastic containers.
In addition, members involved in either firearms training or test firing weapons should not enter clean (i.e.
firearms residue free) areas of the Forensic Science Laboratory or submit items to the Laboratory for
examination for firearms residue traces unless they have taken appropriate measures to eliminate the
possibility of firearms residue contamination. These measures include, but are not limited to, wearing suitable
protective clothing when engaged in activity involving firearms, showering and changing clothes, or leaving
sufficient time (24 hours or more) before visiting the laboratory.
Members involved in shooting on the firing range should also take account of any special instructions, either
oral or written, which may be displayed or given from time to time in relation to whatever anti-contamination
precautions are currently in force.
This is not an exhaustive list of anti-contamination precautions. Whenever samples are being taken for
forensic analysis, the member-in-charge needs to consider the correct measures to avoid contamination. The
history of the handling and packaging, including the names of the Gardaí, location and date are needed when
submitting items for trace analysis to the FSL. Any concerns or doubts in relation to possible contamination
should be communicated at the time of submission or as soon as they become apparent.

16.9. Packaging and Labelling
A guide for the appropriate packaging of different types of evidence is provided (Table 2). All items should be
packed, sealed and labelled at the time and place of seizure. The top of paper bags should be folded twice
and sealed along its length with Sellotape. Knives and other sharp items should be packed in telescopic tubes.
All small items should be backed in tamper evident bags prior to their delivery to the FSL. The unique number
needs to be recorded as it is an integral part of the bag’s security.

16.10. Blood
Blood is the most frequently encountered body fluid in criminal investigations. Its forensically friendly bright
red colour comes from the heme molecule which binds to oxygen and carbon dioxide to facilitate
transportation around the body. To increase their capacity for carrying gases, red blood cells have no nucleus.
Therefore DNA analysis in blood is dependent on the nuclei present in the white blood cells. Fortunately, a
bloodstain smaller than the letter ‘O’ in this text has enough white blood cells to yield a DNA profile.

Potential of Blood as Evidence
There are two main objectives in analysing a bloodstain. The first is to determine the source of the blood by
comparing its DNA profile with reference samples from victims and suspects. The second objective is to see if
Blood Pattern Analysis (BPA) will yield information about the incident.
The potential of the evidence from blood
depends on the case circumstances. It can
help to reconstruct the events which occurred
during the crime, link the suspect to the
bleeding victim, link the bleeding suspect to
the victim or scene, link the victim or suspect
or both to weapons, vehicles and so on.
Blood analysis is limited by the inability to
determine with any precision the age of a
bloodstain. There are also significant
limitations on whether BPA can assist an
investigation. For example, it is often
impossible to distinguish whether the
observed blood pattern on a garment has
resulted from the wearer being the assailant
or as result of the wearer attempting to stop
the assault.
An investigator needs to be aware that there are many circumstances in which blood does not transfer to the
assailant. Transfer of victim’s blood to the assailant is unlikely in any case where the assailant left immediately
after the blow or stab injury that caused bleeding. A victim lying in a pool of blood may have started to bleed
from a head injury sustained from falling during the assault. If the assailant did not touch the injured party
after the fall, they will not have blood on their clothes or person. Little or no blood may transfer back to the
assailant who repeatedly beats a bleeding person with a weapon.
The species of origin of the blood can be identified. This can be useful for poaching cases or malicious injury
to animals.

Examination of Blood at Scenes
When a person bleeds, blood transfers to other people or items by direct contact or through drops that travel
through the air. The pattern of the stains may give information on where an assault started, how much the
victim moved during the assault and what the victim did after the assault if he or she survived. Sometimes,
patterns may indicate if more than one person bled at a scene.
Only competent and experienced examiners should interpret blood patterns. It is very easy to jump to an
incorrect conclusion that could lead an investigation astray or destroy the prosecution case. An inexperienced
examiner may not recognise critical stains such as those left by a bleeding assailant. Competent blood pattern
analysts will have received formal training in combination with reconstruction experiments, followed by
mentoring and continuous peer review of their work. The analyst needs to participate successfully in
proficiency tests to demonstrate their competence.

7

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Selection of Stains at Scenes
It is not possible to analyse all bloodstains from the scene. Instead bloodstains are selected on the basis of
those which are most likely to advance the investigation. There are three main reasons for analysing
bloodstains from scenes:
Finding bloodstains left by the perpetrator:
Where there is a possibility that the perpetrator bled at the scene the scene examiner will look for stains that
are the least likely to have come from the victim. Information from the surviving victim or witnesses may assist
in the selection. In the case where the victim has not survived, the scene examiner searches for drops from
injuries or contact stains from small cuts that are more likely to have originated from the suspect rather than
the victim. Information on bleeding injuries on the suspect should be relayed to the scene examiner.
Reconstructing events:
To reconstruct what happened during and after the incident the scene examiner takes samples from blood
stained areas that will yield information about who bled in different parts of the scene and their position
(standing/sitting/lying) during the assault.
Finding bloodstains left by the victim:
Where there is a denial that the victim bled at the scene the scene examiner takes samples from bloodstains
that are most likely to have been left by the victim.

Searching for Blood at Scenes
Good light is vital for finding blood at scenes, particularly when the scene has been cleaned. Scene of crime
examiners need appropriate torches or mobile lighting for searching purposes. Bloodstains are not enhanced
by fluorescent or any other type of lighting apart from regular white light.
A presumptive test will indicate whether a stain is blood. Presumptive tests work on the principle of
identifying the peroxidase enzyme in blood which reacts with certain chemicals to produce a colour change.
As this enzyme is present in other organic materials the test is not specific for blood, but in practice a false
positive result is unusual. Blood test kits are available from commercial companies. It is important that the
scene examiner is properly trained to do this test.
It is usually very difficult to clean up all the
blood spilled at a scene. However, if it is
suspected that the scene has been cleaned
and little or no blood has been found, then
spraying the scene with Luminol could be
considered as a last resort. This chemical
reacts with diluted blood to give a strong blue
luminescence when viewed in the dark. It is
not specific for blood. There are also health
and safety issues involved with using Luminol
and the scene needs to be thoroughly
cleaned after use and before its release back
to its owners or occupants. The FSL should
be contacted if Luminol testing is required.

Taking Blood Samples at Scenes
Bloodstains should be submitted intact if possible as direct laboratory sampling yields the best results. This
involves, for example, removing door knobs or sometimes the whole door, cutting stains from wallpaper,
carpets and lino and shaving off blood stained paint flakes.

8

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Another method for taking samples is to cover the stain with a strip of Sellotape and break up the stain by
pressing on the tape to improve adhesion. The Sellotape can then be placed on a cobex sheet and packed
into an envelope or plastic bag. This method is not suitable for a painted surface where the paint comes away
with the stain.
Alternatively, the bloodstain can be scraped into a piece of paper which is folded and placed in an envelope.
Flakes of blood should not be placed directly in an envelope.
If the bloodstain cannot be removed intact or is not suitable for Sellotape lifting or scraping, the scene
examiner can try to take the stain by swabbing with a sterile swab dampened with water. This method is only
used as a last resort as wetting the blood may cause degradation of the DNA. If blood is still damp, ideally it
should be dried at room temperature before packing. Liquid blood at a scene can be sampled by soaking onto
a sterile swab. The swab should be frozen or brought to the FSL for freezing.

Blood Stained Clothes
These should be packed in sealed paper bags for transport to the FSL. If clothes are packed while the
bloodstains are wet, the transfer of blood is likely to interfere with the blood pattern or the possibility of
finding blood from another source. Drying clothes involves laying them out on a flat, clean surface and leaving
them at room temperature. Damp bloodstains will deteriorate. Upon arrival in the laboratory, the FSL
reception staff should be informed if items are still damp and they will organise drying. When there is a lot of
wet blood on the deceased at a scene, removing the clothes may be necessary to prevent additional staining
during transportation to the post mortem.
Note: The FSL cannot locate blood on washed clothes.

Reference Samples
Reference samples are the same as those required for DNA analysis (16.12).

16.11. Body Fluids other than Blood and Semen
Faeces
It is usually sufficient for the scientist to comment that a material has the appearance and odour of faeces. If
more information is required, the scientist can carry out a test to indicate the presence of urobilinogen in the
faeces. This test requires a piece of faecal material and is unlikely to yield a result with light stains. DNA
profiling of faeces is usually unsuccessful. A swab of the surface of the formed stool is required to attempt a
DNA analysis.

Saliva
Saliva can be a useful source of DNA in some criminal investigations. DNA is present in the epithelial cells that
transfer from the lining of the mouth cavity to the liquid secreted from the salivary glands into the mouth.
Saliva can be transferred by spitting or by the mouth coming into direct contact with objects such as
cigarettes, drinking straws, drinking utensils, food or balaclavas. It may also be transferred in the course of
kissing, sucking, licking or biting.
In sexual assault cases where ejaculation has not taken place, saliva may yield the DNA profile of the
perpetrator. As saliva stains are invisible the Forensic Medical Examiner should ask the complainant to indicate
where the assailant’s mouth may have made contact with the body. The Medical Examiner will then swab the
indicated areas on the body. If fellatio is alleged, the Medical Examiner should swab the penis of the suspect if
examined within seven days of the alleged sexual assault.

9

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Urine
Occasionally, the identification of urine is useful in an investigation. For example, there have been allegations
of urination on a victim in the course of a sexual assault or a suggestion that a culprit may have urinated at a
scene. Urine stains are often not visible. Locating an area of clothing or furnishing that may contain urine
may depend on odour or a witness observation. Tests for urine are relatively insensitive due to the dilution of
its characteristic constituents; urea and creatinine.
Urine stains are unlikely to yield a DNA profile because of the low level of cells present in the fluid. There is
some chance of getting DNA from liquid samples as it is may be possible to concentrate enough cells from a
large volume of urine.

Vomit
There is no specific test to identify vomit. Characteristics such as odour and appearance of the sample
generally allow the scientist to comment on the likelihood of it being vomit. DNA profiling is usually successful
on both bulk vomit and dry stains. The scene examiner takes a sample of wet vomit in a screw top container
and transfers it immediately to the laboratory or stores in a freezer

16.12. DNA
DNA profiling was introduced as a technique in forensic science in the mid 1980s and, to date, it has proved to
be the greatest advance towards the individualisation of biological material.
The profiling utilised in the Forensic Science Laboratory is based on DNA in cell nuclei; this is present at
different levels in the commonly encountered body samples. Nuclear DNA is the genetic material inherited
from both parents.
In the field of forensic science, small sections of
the (very large) DNA molecule are examined. The
system employed in the FSL looks at twenty
possible areas of difference. In combination these
are highly discriminating thus allowing for
differentiation between people. Because DNA is
inherited, close blood relatives are more likely to
share profiles. To date, no two people, related or
unrelated, have been reported to show the same
twenty elements i.e. to have matching profiles.
An exception to this is identical twins.
The technique currently employed allows the profiling of very small amounts of DNA. This is one of its
strengths but is also a potential weakness as it renders the process more vulnerable to contamination (the
presence of extraneous DNA possibly from a Garda or laboratory operator or DNA transferred within a case
through improper sampling, handling or packing).
When DNA profiling is carried out and profiles are compared with reference samples, the laboratory report
states whether there is a match or a non-match; the latter excludes a person as a source of the DNA.
In addition, the report gives an indication of how rare or how common the profile is in the population. The
chance that an unrelated person would have the same profile is less than one in a billion. The chance that a
close relative such as a brother would have the same profile is much greater, in the order of one in ten to
thirty thousand.
If the laboratory is dealing with very small amounts of DNA or DNA that is degraded due to age or storage
conditions, incomplete profiles may be generated i.e. not all twenty characteristics detected. In these

10

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 11

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

instances, it may be the case that the incomplete profile is shared between brothers or sisters for example.
The ‘brothers defence’ is a common one. If a suspect’s close relatives are in any way possible suspects as well,
efforts should be made to take samples for elimination purposes.
A DNA profile from a stain does not tell anything about the person who left it except their sex. This means
that a profile in isolation is of limited use. Comparative samples from suspects and injured parties are
required. (Note: DNA database legislation may, in time, change this situation. If, for example, a database of
convicted persons were available, a profile from a crime scene could be searched against profiles stored on
this database).

When should DNA be considered?
DNA profiling should be considered in all types of crime, from burglary to murder. DNA is most obviously
deposited if, for example, a burglar cuts themselves at a scene or semen is transferred in a sexual assault.
Other samples that routinely yield profiles are masks, gloves, cigarette ends, chewing gum and drinking
vessels. Thus, physical contact may leave enough DNA for profiling purposes. However, the absence of a DNA
profile does not equate to absence of contact. Insufficient DNA may transfer initially or it may be lost through
subsequent actions.
Selection of items for DNA profiling is very important and items submitted to the laboratory should be
correlated to witness reports if these are relevant. A typical example is samples from a stolen car used in a
robbery where it should be possible to establish what is ‘foreign’ to the car by checking with the owner. The
finding of DNA profiles on items that are not of significance may add confusion as these will be reported as
coming from an unknown source.
Other factors to be considered are common to other types of evidence. Has a suspect legitimate access to a
vehicle, place or person? Is the profile found on a moveable item? In addition, the presence of a DNA profile
is not necessarily indicative of recent contact as it does not tell the time since deposit. Objects with multiple
users often yield un-interpretable mixtures; no conclusion can be drawn from these profiles.
If an object potentially holds DNA and fingerprints, discussion should ideally take place between both experts
to decide the best course of action.

Identification of Bodies
In addition to its use in criminal investigations, DNA profiling can be used as an aid in the identification of
bodies. In many cases, it is not necessary as other means of identification such as dental records may be
available. Details on samples required for DNA identification of bodies are available at 6.27.

DNA Samples
Reference samples from living individuals (in criminal or identification cases) should be either blood samples,
plucked hair samples or buccal swabs. If buccal swabs are taken, note that these must be rubbed very firmly
on gums and cheeks i.e. a swab saturated in saliva is not sufficient. With regard to hair samples it should be
noted that plucked hair is required for DNA profiling and a hair combing is required for other evidence such as
fibres from balaclavas.

16.13. Drugs
The Drugs Section of the Forensic Science Laboratory examines items seized under the Misuse of Drugs Acts,
1977 and 1984. The results of the examination are sent to An Garda Síochána in the form of a Certificate of
Analysis issued under Section 10 of the Misuse of Drugs Act, 1984. This is an important legal document and it
is this original document that should be produced in Court. In cases before the District Court the presence of
the scientist is normally not required, the certificate being sufficient. In cases before higher Courts the scientist
may be required to attend.

11

Page 12

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Prosecutions under the Misuse of Drug Act, 1977 typically involve:
• Possession of a controlled drug - Section 3
• Possession of a controlled drug with intent to supply - Section 15 (or Section 15A where the value of the
drugs is greater than €12,700 - see the Criminal Justice Act, 1999)
• Cultivation, generally of plants of the genus Cannabis - Section 17
• Allowing a premises or vehicle to be used for drug related activities - Section 19
• Importation, sale, manufacture etc. of controlled drugs - Section 5
Items submitted to the laboratory should be sealed in uniquely numbered, tamper evident, plastic bags. The
unique number should be recorded and used in Statements of Evidence. Exceptions to this are very large items
which should be packaged in Garda Evidence bags and vegetative or fungal material which should be
packaged in paper bags to reduce the likelihood of the material decomposing.
Sharp items such as knives and blades should be packaged safely. Special containers are available for this task.
Care should be taken that evidence bags do not become too heavy to be easily managed and carried.
Important: The FSL does not normally analyse syringes or currency notes.

General Points
At all times care should be taken to avoid
contamination especially where trace evidence is
important. Transferred trace evidence may also be
sought. It may be relevant to provide evidence to
support the allegation that a suspect carried a
package strapped to their body, or that an item
was carried in a car or stored in a house. On
occasion, wrapping materials are examined to see
if any link exists between items found on a suspect
and items found at a scene.
As with other types of cases submitted to the
laboratory, the possible need for fingerprints or
DNA should be considered. Consultations may be
necessary between FSL staff, the Garda Fingerprint
Section and Garda in charge to effect the best
approach to individual scenarios.

Cannabis Resin, Cannabis and
Cannabis Plants
Cannabis resin is the drug most frequently
encountered by the FSL. It is a brown substance
and when imported in bulk is usually in slabs
typically weighing approximately 250 grams.
Street deals tend to be comprised of 1 to 2 grams
of the drug wrapped in foil, plastic or paper. The
drug is usually smoked.

12

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 13

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Important: Prior to submission to the FSL, a summons must be applied for in respect of cannabis resin cases
for prosecution under Section 3 of the Misuse of Drugs Act, 1977 (See HQ Directive 91/05).
Cannabis plant material may be green or brown in colour and is often imported as bales containing packages
weighing up to 1 kilogram. Cannabis plants are seized and examined in relation to cultivation charges.
Normally the entire plant is not submitted for analysis, a sample of the leaves of the plants being sufficient. If
a large seizure of plants is made, the FSL should be contacted to ascertain the number of such samples that
are required. The plants will decompose rapidly if packed in plastic.
Paraphernalia such as pipes and hand rolled cigarettes used to smoke cannabis and cannabis resin, together
with knives, scales and wrapping materials are also submitted for analysis.

Powdered Drugs
Drugs in powder form include diamorphine (heroin), cocaine, amphetamine and methylamphetamine. In bulk,
these drugs are usually imported in plastic wrapped slabs of up to1 kilogram in weight. For selling on the
street these drugs are often packaged in knotted plastic bag ends or folded paper packs.
Diamorphine is usually encountered as a brown powder which is either smoked or injected. Cocaine is usually
a white powder which is snorted while ‘crack’ cocaine is typically smoked. Amphetamine is usually an off
white powder which is taken orally or snorted. Methylamphetamine is known as ‘ice’ when encountered in
crystalline form and is typically smoked.
Powders, used as cutting agents, containing non-controlled drugs such as caffeine or lignocaine are
often encountered.

Tablets and Capsules
A large number of illicit tablets are encountered by the laboratory. Many of these contain
methylenedioxymethylamphetamine (MDMA) or other related compounds. These often have a design or logo
impressed into the tablet. The laboratory also analyses medical preparations in tablet or capsule form in
circumstances where it is alleged that they were unlawfully obtained or sold.

Hallucinogenic Mushrooms and Khat
A number of mushrooms contain controlled drugs. The most commonly encountered is the psilocybe
mushroom. Commercially produced plastic packages of a number of varieties of these mushrooms have been
seized. It is important that these are transferred without delay to the laboratory for analysis. They should not
be frozen, but dried or wrapped in paper in the case of any delay.
Khat is a vegetative material which is encountered usually in transit from Africa to the USA. It is typically seen
as a bundle of leafy stems wrapped in a banana leaf. The leaf material is chewed. One or two bundles of
such material are sufficient for analysis and the bulk of the material should not be brought to the laboratory.
In the case of any delay in delivering the material to the laboratory it should be frozen as the active drug is
rapidly lost as the material decays.
The analysis times for both mushrooms and khat are longer than for other drugs and it may take several days
before results are available, even in urgent cases.

Lysergide (LSD)
Lysergide is usually presented in cardboard divided by perforations into small squares. It has also been
encountered in small pellets known as microdots. It should not be handled as the drug can be absorbed
through the skin.

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Illegal Manufacture
If illegal manufacture of drugs is suspected at a premises then it is advisable to obtain expert help to handle or
dismantle items. Such scenes can pose safety hazards as the manufacture of drugs can involve the use of
poisonous, inflammable, intoxicating or explosive chemicals.

Alcohol analysis and toxicology
Alcohol analysis is performed in the laboratory in relation to illegal distillation, breaches of the licensing laws
and in body fluid specimens from fatal accidents and sexual assaults. A drug screening service of blood and
urine specimens in sexual assault cases is also provided. This screening targets the main drugs of abuse. It is
necessary to complete a Toxicology Request Form if either an alcohol analysis or a drug screen is required.

16.14. Dyes and Markers
The usual purpose of a marker is to identify either a specific item of property or an individual who has handled
a specific item of property. There are a selection of markers and dyes that the Forensic Science Laboratory can
analyse; some are supplied by the laboratory and some are commercially available. These include the following:

Malachite Green
Malachite green powder is the dyestuff most commonly used for marking purposes. Anthracene is usually
added to the powder as an additional marker. For outdoor use, Vaseline can be added. They are supplied in kit
form by the laboratory.

Liquid Markers
An invisible liquid marker can be added to diesel or petrol. This does not affect the fuel but the laboratory can
detect its presence.

Powders
Dyes, which are only visible under ultra-violet lighting, are also available if it is desirable that the suspect is not
made immediately aware of a preset trap. In some instances it may be possible to make up a special marker
with unique properties.

Commercially Available Products
Commercially available ultra-violet pens provide a suitable method of marking a wide variety of materials
including money.

Exploding Dyes
The main purpose of banks and other institutions using exploding dyes is to cover stolen money in dye (usually
red smoke dyes) and therefore make it worthless.

Potential of the Evidence
Markers and dyes can be a useful aid in theft cases to help link the suspect to the scene and/or to identify the
items found in a suspect’s possession as the stolen property.
Some financial institutions use slightly different formulations of dye so that the stolen money can be traced to
their bank and so on. This can prove useful in the laboratory as dye found on the suspect’s clothing can then
be linked back to a specific institution such as the Bank of Ireland rather than Allied Irish Bank.

Limitations
Malachite green is mainly used in petty theft, and stains whatever it is applied to. Visible transferred dyes such
as malachite green can be removed by washing. The dye when transferred is in a fine powdered form but

14

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 15

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

becomes more visible when in contact with sweat. Therefore the lapse of time between marked articles being
stolen and examination of suspects’ hands should be as short as possible. The dyes present in smoke dye
devices and in dyes such as malachite green are very fine and light so extreme caution has to be taken to avoid
contamination. Ultra-violet light is needed to detect ultra-violet powders.
Liquid dyes need to be added when the container tank is nearly empty and being refilled. The liquid dyes must
be made up fresh and the size of tank must be known when requesting the marker dye from the laboratory.

When Should Dye and Marker Evidence be Considered?
It is worth consulting with laboratory personnel in relation to the most suitable marker for any particular
situation. The principal use of Malachite green is in instances where the number of suspects is limited. Liquid
dyes are available for situations where it is suspected that fuels such as petrol or diesel are being stolen.
Liquid markers
• Sample from source of supply, known to be free from marker, i.e. pure petrol or diesel
• Take samples from the fuel tank after addition of marker
• Take samples from suspected stolen fuel thought to contain marker
• Take a sample of the marker used
Transferred dyes e.g. malachite green, ultra-violet powders
• Swabs from hands of suspect(s). Use cotton buds slightly moistened with alcohol (methanol)
• Suspect’s clothes
Exploding dyes
Seize as evidence any suspected dyed money notes from the suspect or the suspect’s premises, in accordance
with the Law. Swab the hands and face of the suspect(s) using cotton buds slightly moistened with alcohol
(methanol) and take the clothes. Also seize the device or container that the device was in and any discarded
stained money notes to be used as a reference. Consider other miscellaneous materials which may prove
useful as sources of recoverable evidence.

16.15. Explosives
The FSL examines substances to verify their legal classification as explosives (Bulk Analysis) and objects for the
presence of traces of explosive substances (Trace Analysis).

Bulk Explosives
There are many materials with explosive properties available. The most commonly encountered are
listed below.
Commercial Explosives
These have legitimate uses in quarrying, blasting and so on but may also be used for illicit purposes.
Home Made Explosive (HME) Mixtures
The mixture which has most frequently been encountered here in recent years is ammonium nitrate-based
fertiliser ground to a fine powder and mixed with icing sugar.
Black Powder (Gunpowder)
A black material containing potassium nitrate, sulphur and charcoal.

15

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 16

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Semtex
An orange mixture, similar in consistency to plasticine, containing the explosives PETN and RDX, or RDX only.
Peroxide explosives
These are sensitive and unstable white powders which can be made from readily available ingredients. To
date, they have been extremely rare in this country but, if their presence is suspected, the utmost caution is
advised.
Pipe bombs
These may contain propellant powder from cartridge cases, pyrotechnic materials from fireworks, Black
Powder, or some combination of the above.
Illegal fireworks
It may be necessary to identify active material.
For the analysis of bulk explosives, a few grams of the material are sufficient. Suspect explosive materials
should always be packed in knotted/sealed nylon bags (similar to those used in accelerants analysis) as organic
explosives are volatile and may contaminate other items.

Trace Explosives
The following are the situations in which analysis for trace explosives may be of assistance.
To identify the explosives used to cause an explosion or in an explosive device which has detonated
(e.g. Pipe Bomb):
Take samples of debris from the seat of the explosion. Fragments of the device if found (e.g. pipe bomb
fragments) are likely to be the most fruitful source of trace explosives. Ideally, the fragments of the device
should be submitted to the FSL for examination for trace explosives. If fingerprint/DNA examination is also
required, a joint examination can be arranged. Otherwise, if suspect explosive residues such as traces of
powder are visible on the fragments, these should be removed and submitted. The fragments may also be
swabbed using explosive residue kits which are available from the FSL. These kits contain detailed instructions
on how they should be used.
To link individuals, motor-vehicles and so on to explosive finds:
Explosive Residue Kits, which are available from the Laboratory, can be used to swab the hands of individuals
suspected of having had recent contact with bulk organic explosives. Outer clothing, car mats and so on may
also be submitted.
Items submitted for trace explosives analysis should always be packed in knotted/sealed nylon bags. When
handling and transporting items for trace explosives analysis, the utmost care should be taken to avoid
bringing the items near sources of bulk explosives.

16.16. Fibres
Textile items examined in the Laboratory are made mainly of woven and knitted fibres and include items such
as clothes, bedclothes and household textiles, house and vehicle upholstery, carpets, ropes and so on.
When a textile item comes into physical contact with an object there is the potential for individual fibres of the
textile item to be transferred to the object. The number of fibres transferred will depend on factors such as
the number of fibres on the surface or shedding from the textile, the size of the area of the surfaces in contact
and the pressure involved in the contact. For example, more fibres will be transferred during a struggle due to
increased pressure. The fibres transferred are generally in the form of individual fibres which are usually not
visible to the naked eye.

16

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 17

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

The physical contact involved in the case could be a struggle between people, a person sitting on a car seat,
contact between a person’s clothes and the edges at a point of entry or a person wearing a balaclava. The
first two examples could result in the two-way transfer of fibres, firstly between two sets of clothes and
secondly between clothes and the upholstery of the car seat. The latter two examples could result in the oneway transfer of fibre trace evidence to the edges of a broken window at the point of entry, or to the head hair
of a person. In such instances, the potential for the transfer of other evidence types exists. This includes glass
from the point of entry and DNA on the balaclava.
Physical contact between a victim and a vehicle in a traffic accident can result in the transfer of fibres to the
area of impact on the vehicle, with the transfer of materials such as paint and plastics to the victim’s clothes.
In some instances the impact can cause the transferred fibres to become fused, increasing their persistence.
Some textiles are not suitable as sources of fibres for various reasons. These include a non-shedding fabric,
pale colours or extremely common fibres such as denim.
The examinations carried out in the FSL involve the searching for transferred ‘foreign’ fibres and comparing
these to the control or reference fibres of the textile items such as seat cover fibres on clothes or balaclava
fibres in hair combings. The fibres are examined with regard to the colour, type and matching characteristics
based on microscopic and other examinations.
Although textile items are mass produced, the finding of numbers of transferred fibres, especially if these
involve more than one type of fibre or are part of a two-way transfer of material, can provide strong evidence
of recent contact.
Transferred fibres are rapidly lost during normal activity after the initial transfer. Within the first two hours
after contact as much as 80% of the transferred fibres can be lost. Therefore, items from victims and suspects
should be taken as soon as possible. Similarly, if the scene is out of doors and exposed to the weather, fibres
may be quickly lost and such scenes therefore require early attention.
The size of the fibres and the ability for them to transfer and be redistributed mean they are particularly
susceptible to cross contamination. Great care must be exercised at all times to avoid contamination. To help
avoid the inadvertent cross-contamination of items, the FSL gives the following recommendations to Gardai
dealing with cases involving potential fibres transfer:
Gardai
• Different sources (suspect, victim and scenes) should be dealt with by separate Garda personnel
• Where, due to case circumstances, the same officer must deal with multiple scenes, precautions such as
wearing different barrier clothing, gloves, anti-contamination suits, and so on between scenes must be
used and their use noted.
• Leave an appropriate length of time between examining different aspects of a case to eliminate the
possibility of cross-contamination.
Suspects and victims
• Suspect and victim should be kept apart and should not be transported in the same motor vehicle or
interviewed in the same interview room.

17

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 18

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Handling items
• Control or reference materials should be kept separate from any surfaces or items with which contact
could be of significant evidential value.
• Items should be taken and appropriately packaged and labelled as soon as possible.
• Once sealed in packaging, items should not be removed and repackaged.
• If items need to be displayed for interview purposes they should be sealed in a paper sack with a clear
window.
• Where practical, the whole item for fibre examination should be submitted to the Laboratory. Where this
is impractical, adhesive tape lifts are recommended for the recovery of transferred fibres. These should be
taken as quickly as possible, packaged and labelled.
• Items should be packaged in paper sacks, the top of which should be folded over and sealed, and the
sack appropriately labelled. Exceptions to this are where items are to be examined for the presence of
accelerants, when sealed nylon bags should be used.
• Hair combing kits should be used to recover fibres and extraneous material from the hair of suspects.
The most difficult task facing scene of crime examiners is to initially locate fibre evidence since fibres are
usually not visible to the naked eye. All surfaces with which it is thought a suspect may have had contact
should be sampled.
When it is impractical to submit an item such as the point of entry or car seat the FSL recommends that
adhesive tape lifts be used to recover the transferred fibres. In such situations, a control or reference sample of
the textile items, such as vehicle upholstery, should be taken. It is recommended that a sample of the fabric
taken from the likely contact area be submitted and that it should be representative of the pattern, i.e. take a
piece of sufficient size to ensure that each colour and kind of fibre has been sampled. Samples should be
placed in a self-seal plastic bag, sealed and labelled.
As well as cases involving trace fibre evidence, the FSL examines cases involving textiles such as torn or cut
pieces of fabric, residual threads and so on.
Pieces of fabric may be torn and left at a point of entry or cut from garments such as hats and sleeves to
create eyeholes to make a facemask. Garda personnel involved in subsequent searches should be made aware
of the general appearance of the textile item. Torn or cut edges of the pieces of fabric may fit together with
the possibility of physical fit evidence.
Items such as buttons may be torn from garments during a struggle and the residual thread and button can be
compared with potential matching garments.

16.17. Hair
Although hair features prominently in fiction as a means of solving crime, in reality its usefulness in forensic
science is limited. This is because it is not possible to determine that a hair originated from a particular person
on the basis of an examination of its physical appearance.
People shed hair continuously and sometimes the hair transfers to another person or is left at a scene. The
absence of hair matching a suspect does not mean contact did not occur. Even in sexual assault cases where

18

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 19

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

there is considerable contact, hairs may not transfer. Studies show that transferred head hair is found on the
outer clothing of victims or suspects in approximately 13% of sexual assault cases. Hairs matching suspect’s
pubic or head hair were found on the underwear of 3-4% of victims of sexual assault. The combination of the
poor discriminatory power of hair evidence and the relatively low rate of hair transfer means that hair
examinations are less likely to yield successful results than other kinds of trace evidence.

When Should Hair Evidence be Considered?
Hair should be considered when the investigator needs evidence to show contact between people or between
a person and a scene or relevant item. Hair analysis is usually attempted by the FSL when there is no other
trace evidence or when the trace evidence available is weak and hair analysis might strengthen the evidence.
For example, if a balaclava was left at a scene, the FSL would first try to get a DNA profile from skin cells or
saliva and compare it with the suspect(s). Only if this is unsuccessful will the scientist compare hairs in the
balaclava with suspect’s samples. Hair evidence is so weak that there is no point carrying out this examination
unless there is other significant corroborative evidence.

Other Information from Hair
It is usually possible to distinguish scalp hair, pubic hair and beard hair. Examination of the roots with a
microscope often establishes whether the hair fell out or was pulled. Human hair can be distinguished from
other animal hair and the animal hair can be identified. Generally it is not possible to determine if animal hairs
came from a particular animal.

DNA Analysis of Hair
Usually, hair lacks the discriminating type of DNA normally used in forensic investigations. The exception is
pulled hair when scalp cells come away with the root. Hair that falls out naturally has shrivelled roots without
adhering cells.
A less discriminating form of DNA, known as mitochondrial DNA, is present in the hair shaft. This is a rarely
used analysis because approximately one person in a hundred has the same mitochondrial DNA.

Samples for Hair Analysis
Items submitted to the laboratory on which hair may be found include clothing, balaclavas and motorcycle
helmets. The Gardaí may take control samples for comparative work from the head or beard using a hair
combing kit supplied by the laboratory. The whole area of the head or beard needs to be combed to ensure a
representative sample. If the combing does not appear to have 15 to 20 hairs, the sample should be
supplemented by cut or pulled hairs. Cut samples must be taken close to the skin so that the full length is
available for examination. Only a medical examiner should take pubic hair samples.

16.18. Miscellaneous Materials
Adhesives and Glues
These materials can be identified and linked to a possible source by chemical analysis.

Plastic Bags
Plastic bags have been used in many ways in the course of committing crimes including the transport of drugs
or stolen property and covering bodies in murder cases.
A comparison of a crime scene bag with similar bags from suspect’s or victim’s homes or vehicles involves its
chemical composition, dimensions, manufacturing finish and random manufacturing marks.
The most conclusive outcome is a direct linkage where the scientist can say the crime bag was directly linked
to a known source bag. Depending on the extent of the matching properties other lesser degrees of evidential
value are possible.

19

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 20

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Plastic Film/Tinfoil/Paper
Materials such as plastic film, tinfoil or paper are frequently used in packaging drug deals and can arise in
other circumstances such as pages torn from diaries and weapons wrapped in material. For examination
purposes, the FSL requires the crime sample and a known source for comparison. The most conclusive
outcome is a direct linkage through a physical fit between the two samples. Depending on the outcome of
analytical comparison, other lesser degrees of evidential value are possible.
Tapes (duct tape, insulating tape, Sellotape and so on)
Tape is a material that arises in a range of case types from hoax incendiary/explosive devices to crimes in which
victims are bound up with tape. The same approach and outcomes as with plastic films should apply.
Note: Tapes can be a source of other types of trace evidence such as fibres, DNA, fingerprints and so on.

Other Less Frequently Encountered Material
Cosmetics may be encountered in sexual assault cases or on vehicle airbags after an accident. Lubricant
materials such as Vaseline and KY jelly may also be encountered in relation to sexual assault cases as can glitter
particles shed from clothing or belts.
As with other types of trace evidence, a crime sample and a known source sample as a control material are
needed for comparative purposes. These samples are subjected to comparative analysis such as microscopic
analysis and chemical analysis and depending on the outcome they may be found to match and ascribed an
evidential value.

16.19. Forensic Mobile Phone Analysis
The FSL can retrieve standard data from phones such as text messages, photos and videos and also has the
facility to capture deleted data. If DNA is required to link a person to the phone, the laboratory will sample
for DNA prior to examining the digital data.
Trawling through the mass of information retrieved from mobile phones can be very time consuming. The
FSL has developed a powerful software tool that shows linkages between the examined phone and other
numbers. This has provided significant information about phones in communication with the examined
phone around the time of a crime such as the hijacking of a security van carrying cash.

16.20. Paint
Paint is widely encountered in forensic
casework from burglaries, traffic accidents,
armed robberies, criminal damage and
other types of crime.
Most paint cases submitted to the FSL
require the comparison of transferred or
recovered paint with paint from a known
source, called a ‘control’ sample. Some
cases may require the identification of a
paint type or the determination of the
source of a particular paint flake (e.g. from
a particular type of car).

20

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 21

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

The FSL examination of paint flakes includes microscopic comparison of colour and layer structure and
chemical analysis of one or more layers. The laboratory can work with very small quantities of material, so
suitable precautions must be taken to ensure that one sample (e.g. from a suspect car) does not contaminate
another sample (e.g. from a victim of a hit and run).

Transfer Mechanisms
Paint can be transferred by impact (in a hit and run accident), by flaking (from a window in a burglary), by
pressure (smearing from a jemmy onto another surface), by contact with a wet painted surface (onto clothing)
or by spraying (onto surfaces or nearby clothing in graffiti cases).

Significance
The significance of a match between two paint flakes will increase with the number of paint layers in the
flakes: multiple layers matching are more valuable than single layers of paint. The ability to discriminate paints
will also vary with the colour of the paint: black or white paints are more difficult to discriminate than other
colours like red, green or blue.
It is worth remembering that with larger paint flakes, or paint on materials like car body filler, there is the
possibility of a physical fit to link two flakes of paint. This would make further laboratory analysis unnecessary
and can provide conclusive proof of an association.
Generally, wet paint that has been smeared or spilled will be of less significance than multi-layered paint. .
However, in cases involving, for example, paint spraying or graffiti, the presence of tiny paint droplets or
splashes on a suspect's clothes or shoes may be critically important. The droplets caused by aerosol spraying
may be invisible to the naked eye.

Precautions
Small flakes of paint should be treated the same way as all particulate trace evidence with care being taken to
avoid cross-contamination. Paint is best sampled by removing with a forceps and/or scalpel blade and
packaging in self-seal plastic bags or folded into a paper sheet in a paper envelope. When sampling, the
entire layer structure should be taken right down to the underlying material
Sellotape lifting is not a suitable means of recovering paint flakes from surfaces.
It is wise to exercise caution when interpreting paint smears because they are thinner than normally applied
paint. Paint smears can give a misleading impression of the original colour. For example, a smear of a dark
blue paint on a surface can look like a light blue colour. Similarly, a clear topcoat or lacquer can appear white
when it is smeared, due to surface abrasion.

Case Examples
A white-painted metal bar was recovered from a suspect who was believed to have broken into several cars.
The bar was rusting and flakes of paint were missing from parts of the surface. A flake of white paint
recovered from one of the damaged cars fitted back precisely onto where some of the paint was missing from
the bar, providing conclusive proof of its origin.
An estate car belonging to a painter and decorator was stolen and crashed. Paint cans in the back of the
vehicle burst open and spilled several different colours of paint inside. Paint splashes and smears matching this
spilled paint were recovered from the clothing of two suspects found nearby, providing strong evidence of
contact with the inside of the stolen car.
In the ‘Green River’ murder case in Washington State in the US, paint recovered from some of the victims led
investigators, 20 years later, to a truck manufacturing plant where highly distinctive paint types were in use.
One of the employees subsequently pleaded guilty to the murder of 48 women.

16.21. Semen and Sexual Assaults
The role of the forensic scientist in sexual assaults varies with the case circumstances. Most sexual assaults fall
into one of four categories:
• Stranger sexual assaults where the identity of the assailant is unknown
• Cases where the accused alleges that the other party consented to the sexual act
• Cases where the complainant has an unclear recollection of the event
• Sexual abuse of children or vulnerable adults

Stranger Sexual Assaults
In these cases, the primary objective of the forensic scientist is to find a body fluid stain on samples from the
victim’s body, clothes or scene that would yield the DNA profile of the offender. The profile is then compared
to laboratory databases or reference samples from suspects. If the complainant had intercourse within seven
days of the incident, it is important that the sexual companion of the complainant is eliminated by DNA
profiling.
If a male body fluid is not found on the complainant and there is a suspect, the scientist can look for female
DNA on swabs from the penis or on the underpants. If this is unsuccessful, the forensic scientist will look for
trace evidence such as hair or fibres that may have transferred between the parties.

Sexual Assaults where the Accused Alleges that the Other Party Consented
These are the most common type of sexual assault and constitute more than 80% of the cases received by the
FSL. Once the accused agrees that sexual activity occurred, the useful role of the forensic scientist is limited to
looking at any discrepancy between the accounts of the accused and the complainant. For example, the
female may allege that full sexual intercourse occurred whereas the male says only fellatio took place. The
scientist will also examine the complainant’s clothes to determine if there is damage beyond what would be
expected from normal wear.

Sexual Abuse of Children and Vulnerable Adults
The accused in these cases usually denies that any sexual activity took place. The scientist’s role is to look for
evidence of the alleged activity. The examination results often have to be interpreted cautiously as the alleged
offender may have legitimate access to the complainant and there could be innocent explanations for some or
all of the findings. Frequently, complaints of this type of abuse occur some time after the alleged incident(s).
In these situations, any relevant clothes or bedclothes that have not been washed in the interval should be
submitted for a semen search.

Sexual Assaults with Limited Complainant Recollection
These cases are frequently associated with alcohol and drug consumption or the complainant alleges
interference with their drink. The role of the scientist is usually to establish if sexual activity took place.
Toxicological analysis of the complainant’s urine and blood samples may indicate drug or alcohol consumption
(see section 16.13). A prosecution is unusual in these circumstances, as the issue of whether the complainant
consented cannot be established. On occasion, the complainant may withdraw their allegation. For this
reason, the FSL usually waits until the DPP advises on prosecution before commencing a scientific examination.

Medical Examination
There are Sexual Assault Treatment Units in Dublin, Cork, Waterford, Letterkenny, Galway and Mullingar.
Trained medical staff take all body samples from complainants using Sexual Assault Forensic Examination Kits
supplied by Garda Headquarters. The kits, designed by the FSL, contain everything necessary for the medical

22

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 23

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

examination of a complainant or suspect. They include sufficient detailed instructions to facilitate a forensic
medical examination by a General Practitioner outside the geographical areas served by the Sexual Assault
Treatment Units. A toxicology module is provided in the kit to facilitate taking urine and blood when there is a
possibility that alcohol or drugs were consumed.
The medical examiner will also record information in relation to the nature of the sexual activity during the
incident and the complainant’s previous sexual history within the last week. This information is necessary to
facilitate the scientist in the interpretation of the findings.
Garda Headquarters supplies Early Evidence Kits for Gardai to use in situations where there may be a delay
between the incident and the medical examination. These are primarily used when there is an allegation of
fellatio or where a toxicological analysis is needed. Semen may only survive for a few hours in the mouth so
swabs are provided to take mouth samples. If the complainant wishes to urinate prior to the medical
examination, a container is provided to collect a urine sample for toxicology.

Samples for the FSL
The investigating Garda submits the Sexual Assault Examination Kit and the complainant’s clothes worn at the
time of the incident. Lower clothing worn immediately after the incident should also be submitted as these
may be stained with semen arising from vaginal drainage. Similarly, if there was ejaculation onto any other
part of the body, the garment worn next to the skin after the incident should be submitted.
A reference sample (blood or mouth swab) is needed
from the suspect if DNA analysis is required. If the
suspect denies intercourse or fellatio, the medical
examiner should also swab the penis (if within seven
days of the alleged incident). The clothes worn by
the suspect at the time of the alleged incident should
also be taken if they deny involvement in the sexual
activity or being in the company of the complainant.
A reference sample may also be required from any
sexual partner of the complainant within the
previous week.
The selection of samples from the scene depends on the case circumstances. The most frequent scenario is
that the suspect agrees that both he and the complainant were at the scene. Samples are generally not
required in these situations unless there is some discrepancy between the description of the events and a
scientific examination of scene items may help to support one version over another.
If the suspect denies being at the scene, then the scene examiner has to consider what items are most likely to
yield trace evidence that could be linked to the suspect. If the suspect denies that the complainant was with
them in their home or car, then the scene examiner will take samples of or from the surfaces with which the
complainant may have been in contact.

Persistence of Semen
The survival of spermatozoa in a living person is affected by the location on the body and the activities of the
individual after the sexual activity. After ejaculation within the vagina, spermatozoa should be found after 24
hours, are likely to be found up to 48 hours and have been found by the FSL up to four days later. There is a
shorter persistence time for semen deposited in the course of anal intercourse. Spermatozoa may survive in
the mouth up to 24 hours after oral ejaculation. Identification of semen is more difficult if the man has a low
sperm count or has had a vasectomy.
Spermatozoa may survive for considerably longer in deceased bodies, especially in a cool environment. It may
persist indefinitely on clothing that has not been washed and has been kept in a dry environment.

23

chapter 16 final final:Layout 1

11/12/2007

11:42

Page 24

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

The absence of semen in a sexual assault case does not mean that sexual activity did not take place, as
intercourse without ejaculation is not unusual. The FSL finds semen on approximately one third of the vaginal
swabs examined.

Other Examinations in Sexual Assault Cases
The FSL examines any damage found on the complainant’s clothes and evaluates whether it was likely to have
occurred in the course of normal wear or if a greater force was involved. False allegations of sexual assault
have been identified infrequently when the complainant described tearing of her or his clothes and the
scientist finds that a scissors caused the damage.
Depending on the case circumstances, the scientist may also look for blood, saliva, urine, faeces, fibres or head
and pubic hairs.
For further information on the role of the FSL and AGS in the investigation of sexual assaults see “Rape/Sexual
Assault: National Guidelines on Referral and Forensic Clinical Examination in Ireland”, published by the
Department of Health and Children in 2006 and available through the Government Publications Sales Office or
at the Irish College of General Practitioners website (www.icgp.ie).

16.22. Soil
Soil recovered from the clothing and footwear of persons or a vehicle suspected of having been at a crime
scene location can be compared with a reference sample of soil taken from the scene. The reference soil
sample is usually taken from the surface as this is the layer most likely to have been in contact with the victim,
suspect or vehicle.
To undertake a case involving soil requires the following samples to be taken:
• Soil stained clothing/footwear from suspect/victim
• Soil collected from a vehicle suspected of being at the scene
• Reference samples, approximately 100 grams mainly from the surface area of the scene; several
samples may be taken.

Packaging of Samples
Paper sacks with clear window are appropriate for clothing and footwear. Plastic or glass containers are
appropriate for reference samples. All samples taken should be sealed in their packaging and the packaging
appropriately labelled.

Evidential Significance
The evidential significance of finding a match between soil on an item and a reference sample of soil from a
scene is generally low, unless extraneous material such as paint flakes, glass fragments, fertilizer material, and
so on are also present and lend a degree of uniqueness to the soil.
The presence of identifiable pollen material can also add a degree of uniqueness to soils. Identification of
pollens or a detailed geological examination of soils is not a service currently provided by the FSL.

16.23. Tachograph chart examination
Background
Tachograph units are fitted to most commercial vehicles and
buses. They record the speed of the vehicle, the distance
travelled and the type of activity of the driver (driving, resting,
non-driving work). The current type of tachograph unit is fitted in
place of the speedometer and typically takes an electronic signal
from the gearbox which it transforms into a speed recording.
From 2006, digital tachographs are being fitted: these have no
chart and an examination requires specialised equipment (contact
the FSL for more details).
The original purpose of the unit was to record the time the driver
spent driving and resting, for reasons of safety and to prevent
drivers being over-worked and exploited. The forensic
applications are a by-product of this. The FSL does not normally
analyse charts for adherence to the driver’s hours regulations,
unless there is a specific reason such as the suggestion that driver
fatigue may be a factor in an accident.
Tachograph charts have been examined at the Forensic Science Laboratory since 1987. Most of these cases
involve accident investigation with a small number every year requiring route tracing.

Potential of the Evidence
From a forensic viewpoint the charts can provide information about the behaviour of a vehicle in the moments
before an accident, or about the route a vehicle took at a particular time. For example, chart examination can
reveal if a lorry driver braked before an impact or whether he was still accelerating; whether a lorry was
exceeding the relevant speed limit; where a cargo of stolen goods was unloaded; where smuggled drugs or
other goods were loaded or transferred, or when a driver witnessed an incident at a known location. The
surface of the chart is also a good source of finger marks.
Note: Global Positioning Systems, if fitted, can provide a lot of information about a vehicle's movements.
This may give a quicker and more precise result than a tachograph chart examination where route tracing is
required. Even if the GPS system is not fully activated, the movements of the vehicle may still be stored on an
onboard computer and can be recovered and analysed. If this is required, contact the Forensic Science
Laboratory to discuss it.

Limitations
The tachograph does not normally record speeds below about 10 km/h, so low speed driving rarely provides
any useful information. If an impact occurs, there will often be a disturbance on the chart which allows the
speed at impact to be determined. If the point of impact is remote from the siting of the tachograph unit (e.g.
at the back of an articulated lorry) or if the impact is not with a hard object (e.g. a pedestrian accident) there
may be no disturbance recorded at all.
The accuracy of the tachograph recordings depends on the accurate calibration of the vehicle when the
tachograph is installed. To verify the calibration, the FSL requires a map showing the exact location of an
accident or where a vehicle was recovered. The time on the tachograph clock is set by the driver and should
agree with the time in the country of registration of the vehicle.
Digital tachographs, which replace the old style tachograph chart with a smart card inserted by the driver were
introduced in 2005. The amount of information which can be recovered from a smart card in relation to
vehicle movement or accident reconstruction is limited. It will, however, be many years before these completely
replace the tachograph chart.

25

Page 26

Chapter 16 Forensic Science Laboratory

Precautions
Tachograph charts have a pressure-sensitive, wax-coated surface which can easily be damaged by folding or by
abrasion. For this reason they should be placed in a paper or card envelope or folder to protect them.

Legislation
The principal regulations governing tachographs are EC Regulations 3820/85 (Drivers' Hours) and 3821/85
(Installation and Use).

Examples
Tachograph evidence provided information on the times when a range of commercial vehicles passed Jack
White's pub near Wicklow on the night the owner, Tom Nevin, was murdered. Drivers described seeing
different things but the tachograph charts allowed them to be put in sequence to construct a chronology.
Tachograph evidence has provided information on truck drivers' movements in drug importation cases. Charts
can be analysed and compared with statements to see if the statements can be verified. A more detailed
information sheet is available from the FSL.

16.24. Traffic Accidents
Flakes of paint at traffic accident scenes or on a hit and run victim's clothing can sometimes be recovered and
may provide information about the make and model of the vehicle involved. Many vehicles with either solid or
metallic/pearlescent paint have a colourless clear coat on top of the other layers of paint. If only this layer is
transferred it will give almost no information about its origin. The identification of vehicles depends on several
layers of the original (not re-spray) paint being transferred.
Broken headlight and indicator lenses found at a scene can sometimes reveal the vehicle make, model and
year of manufacture. Broken glass, lenses, mirrors or plastic cowlings have the potential to be physically fitted
to that remaining on the suspect vehicle thus providing identification evidence of that vehicle’s presence and
its part in the incident. Broken fragments of glass may be linked to a suspect vehicle by its refractive index.
Note: Vehicle identifications can also be achieved by searching databases of headlight and sidelight lenses and
of door mirrors. Further details are available from the FSL.

Limitations
The database of vehicle paints is largely limited to passenger cars as many commercial vehicles are repainted in
colours specified by the owner.
