Appendix I Human Rights

Appendix I
European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003
The following summary is intended as a general overview of the European Convention of Human
Rights Act, 2003. It is not intended as a precise legal definition and members are referred to the
provisions of the Act or to the relevant publications as distributed by the Garda Human Rights
Office at the Garda College for further information. This summary will assist members in
understanding the various Principles, Articles and Protocols of the Convention which are discussed
in Appendix II.
The European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003 came into force in Irish law on December 31st 2003.
The Act provides that “every organ of the State shall perform its functions in a manner compatible
with the State’s obligations under the Convention provisions.”
The relevant provisions of the European Convention for the Protection of Human Rights and Fundamental
Freedoms (including additional articles known as ‘protocols’ that apply to Ireland) are set out in Schedules 1 to
5 of the 2003 Act.
An Garda Siochana is an organ of the State for the purposes of the Act and every member must
perform his or her functions in a manner that is compatible with the European Convention on
Human Rights.

Key Principles of the European Convention on Human Rights
Gardai are in the front line of ensuring that human rights are protected. How members act and respond to
situations can sometimes also lead to an abuse of human rights.
Where a member of the Garda Siochana invokes a power that limits a person’s rights such as an arrest this is
permitted so long as that power is lawfully invoked. All such limitations to a person’s human rights are
governed by five key principles:
• Legality
• Necessity
• Proportionality
• Accountability
• Non-discrimination
Legality
All actions by the Gardai must have a clear basis in law.
Necessity
The action by the Gardai should be strictly necessary in order to resolve a particular problem or deal with
a threat.
Proportionality
It must be capable of being demonstrated that the action taken by Gardai is proportionate to the threat or
problem that they are seeking to prevent.

1

appendix 1 art 2:Layout 1

02/12/2007

13:19

Page 2

Appendix I Human Rights

Accountability
It must be capable of being shown that Garda action is sufficiently open to scrutiny by the public and other
authorities (such as the Garda Ombudsman Commission and the Garda Inspectorate) in order to ensure that
the Gardai are accountable in practice as well as in theory.
Non-discrimination
All reasonable steps must be taken to ensure that Garda action will avoid discrimination on any ground such as
sex, colour, language, religion, political or other opinion, national or social origin, association with a national
minority, property, birth or other status.
These principals are central to the assessment of any action or proposed action in terms of its conformity with
the Convention. Therefore, before proceeding with any action that has the potential to affect the rights of
another person a member must always take the principles into account.

Article 2 - Right to Life
Article 2 of the Convention bans unlawful killing by the agents of the State, including the Gardai. The only
exceptions are where the State can show that the force which caused death was ‘no more than absolutely
necessary’. The onus is on the State, not on the victim or their next of kin to prove that this was the case.
Article 2 also places the Gardai under a duty to take positive steps to protect life if they know or ought to
know that a real or immediate risk to the life of an identified person is posed by the criminal acts or
another person.

Article 3 - Prohibition of Torture
Ill-treatment is absolutely prohibited by Article 3. The European Convention on Human Rights allows no
exceptions to this rule, even in times of war or public emergency and regardless of the way in which a victim
or potential victim may have behaved. This is particularly relevant in relation to persons who are suspected or
convicted of offences related to terrorism or who are believed to constitute a threat to national security.
Nothing that such persons may have done can be used to justify ill-treating them or exposing them to the risk
of being ill-treated.

Article 4 - Prohibition of Slavery and Forced Labour
Article 4 is an absolute prohibition of slavery and forced labour. This prohibition applies even in times of war or
public emergency.

Article 5 - Right to Liberty and Security
Article 5 lists the six reasons for which people can lawfully be deprived of their liberty by agents of the State,
including the Gardai. This is an exhaustive list and therefore depriving a person of their liberty for any other
reason is unlawful. Article 5 also outlines the rights that must be made available to persons detained by
the Gardai.
The six reasons for lawful deprivation of liberty are as follows:
• Lawful detention following conviction by a competent court
• Lawful arrest or detention for non-compliance with the lawful order of a court or in order to secure the
fulfilment of any obligation prescribed by law
• Lawful arrest or detention for the purpose of bringing a person before a competent legal authority on
reasonable suspicion of having committed an offence or when it is considered reasonably necessary to
prevent a person committing an offence or fleeing after having done so
• Detention of a minor by lawful order for the purpose of educational supervision or their detention for the
purpose of bringing them before a competent legal authority

2

appendix 1 art 2:Layout 1

02/12/2007

13:19

Page 3

Appendix I Human Rights

• Lawful detention for the prevention of the spreading of infectious diseases, of persons of unsound mind,
alcoholics or drug addicts or vagrants
• Lawful arrest or detention of a person to prevent their effecting an unauthorised entry into the country or
of a person against whom action is being taken with a view to deportation or extradition
The rights that must be made available to persons detained by the Gardai can be summarised as follows:
• They must be informed promptly, in a language they understand, of the reasons for their arrest and of any
charges against them
• They must be brought promptly before a judge or other officer authorised by law to exercise judicial power
and shall be entitled to trial within a reasonable time or to release pending trial
• They shall be entitled to take proceedings by which the lawfulness of their detention can be decided
speedily by a court and their release ordered if their detention is not lawful
• Anyone who has been the victim of arrest or detention in contravention of Article 5 has a right to
compensation

Article 6 - Right to a Fair Trial
Article 6 sets out the minimum rights of persons charged with a criminal offence, including the rights to a
fair trial, to be presumed innocent until proven guilty and to have adequate time and facilities to prepare
a defence.

Article 7 - No Punishment Without Law
Article 7 prohibits punishment as a result of retrospective criminal laws. In other words, a person should not be
convicted of a criminal offence for engaging in behaviour that was not illegal when it took place, nor
sentenced to a more severe penalty than applied at the time of their act.

Article 8 - Right to Respect for Private and Family Life
Article 8 recognises that people have a right to privacy as regards their private life, family life, home and
correspondence. The Gardai may interfere with these privacy rights, such as through surveillance activities, but
only if it can be demonstrated that the interference is necessary, lawful and proportionate and falls within one
of the following exceptions, always providing that it is in accordance with the law and is necessary in a
democratic society:
• In the interests of national security, public safety and economic well-being
• For the prevention of disorder or crime
• For the protection of health or morals
• For the protection of the rights and freedoms of others

Article 9 - Freedom of Thought, Conscience and Religion
Article 9 protects freedom of thought, conscience and religion and provides that people should be able to
practice their religion or otherwise demonstrate and celebrate their beliefs. The Gardai can only restrict these
freedoms if they can demonstrate that the restriction is lawful, necessary and proportionate and meets the
terms of one of the exceptions set out under Article 8.

3

Appendix I Human Rights

Article 10 - Freedom of Expression
Article 10 covers the right to freedom of expression. Because this is one of the basic foundations of a
democratic society the Article protects freedom of expression not only where it concerns information and ideas
generally thought to be inoffensive, but also information and ideas that offend, shock or disturb the State or
any sector of the population.
It also recognises that freedom of expression should be exercised in a responsible manner and lists a number of
reasons for which the State may place restrictions on the exercise of this right. Again these reasons are broadly
similar to those set out under Article 8 as well as the protection of the reputation or rights of others, the
prevention of the disclosure of information received in confidence or for maintaining the authority and
impartiality of the judiciary.

Article 11 - Freedom of Assembly and Association
Article 11 is concerned with the right to associate with others (including through membership of a trade
union) and to assemble peacefully. It includes a right to demonstrate and the mere fact that a demonstration
may result in disorder is not a sufficient reason for it to be banned.
Again, Gardai may place restrictions on demonstrations and other meetings for one of the reasons set out
under Article 8 above. In addition, the Article specifically provides that it “shall not prevent the imposition of
lawful restrictions on the exercise of these rights by members of the armed forces, of the police or of the
administration of the State.”

Article 12 - Right to Marry
Article 12 provides that men and women of marriageable age have the right to marry and to found a family, in
accordance with the laws of the State governing the exercise of this right.

Article 13 - Right to an Effective Remedy
This article makes it absolutely clear that the State must provide an effective remedy to every person whose
rights have been violated, even if their rights have been violated by a member of An Garda Siochana acting in
their official capacity.

Article 14 - Prohibition of Discrimination
This Article imposes an overarching obligation on the Gardai to uphold the rights and freedoms in the
Convention in a non-discriminatory way. This does not mean that everyone with whom the Gardai come into
contact must be treated in exactly the same way, but rather that any difference in treatment must have an
objective and reasonable justification, otherwise it will be considered discriminatory.

Protocols
The following are the Protocols which affect the Gardai in the course of their operations:

Protocol 1, Article 1 - Protection of Property
This provision recognises that people have a right not to be deprived of their property. However, Gardai may
seize property (e.g. if it is thought to be evidence of the commission of a criminal offence or the proceeds of
crime) provided that they can show that the seizure is lawful, necessary and proportionate.

Protocol 4, Article 2 - Freedom of Movement
This deals with the right to freedom of movement within a country and the right to leave a country. These
rights are available to everyone present in Ireland no matter what their own nationality. The Gardai may place
restrictions on the exercise of these rights, but only if those restrictions can be shown to be lawful, necessary
and proportionate.

4

Appendix I Human Rights

Protocol 4, Article 3 - Prohibition of Expulsion of Nationals
This provides that a State cannot expel or refuse to admit its own nationals. Therefore, in Ireland these rights
apply only to people of Irish nationality. With the exception of lawful extradition, there are no circumstances
in which it is permissible to expel an Irish national from Ireland or to refuse to allow an Irish national to
enter Ireland.

Protocol 4, Article 4 - Prohibition of Collective Expulsion of Aliens
Collective expulsion is where a group of persons is expelled without each of them having their cases
individually examined. This practice is prohibited.

Protocol 7, Article 1 - Procedural Safeguards Relating to Expulsion of Aliens
This provides that a person shall not be expelled from the State without being allowed to submit reasons
against their expulsion, having their case reviewed and being represented for these purposes. However, they
may be expelled before the exercise of these rights when the expulsion is necessary in the interests of public
order or is grounded on reasons of national security.

Protocol 7, Article 2 - Right of Appeal in Criminal Matters
This confers the right of appeal to higher courts of all persons convicted of criminal offences save in the case
of very minor offences.

Protocol 7, Article 3 - Compensation for Wrongful Conviction
This provides for the compensation of those wrongly convicted of crimes save where the conviction is due to
the victim’s part in not disclosing a key fact.

Protocol 7, Article 4 - Right not to be Tried or Punished Twice
This mirrors the ‘double jeopardy’ provisions under Common Law and provides that no person who has been
finally acquitted or convicted or an offence in accordance with the law and penal procedure of the State shall
be liable to be tried or punished again for it in the same State.

Protocol 7, Article 5 - Equality Between Spouses
This provides for the equality of spouses in terms of their relations with their children, their marriage and
its dissolution.

Protocol 13, Article 1 - Abolition of the Death Penalty
This Protocol prohibits the death penalty and states that no one shall be condemned to such penalty
or executed.

Appendix II Procedures

Introduction
Overview
In this Appendix, a general overview is attempted of Garda practice and procedure as enshrined in
current Irish legislation and case law. These procedures are considered briefly in the context of Irish
Constitutional Law and decisions of The European Court of Human Rights in the interpretation of
The European Convention on Human Rights, a brief overview of which has been provided at
Appendix I.
This summary is intended as a general guideline for members to consult in the course of conducting
criminal investigations. The guidance given is not presented as a precise legal interpretation. In this
context, members should refer to the relevant legislation and the judgements cited.
The materials and assistance provided by the Dean of the Law School at the Honorable Society of
Kings Inns, Dublin is gratefully acknowledged in the compilation of this summary.

National and International Articles and Rules of Particular Relevance
The Irish Government is a signatory to international conventions on human and civil rights. The Gardai should
act in accordance with both the Irish Constitution and these international conventions. Articles of particular
relevance are:

Irish Constitution
Article 38(1):
No person shall be tried on any criminal charge save in due course of law.
Article 40(4.1):
No person shall be deprived of his personal liberty save in accordance with law.

The United Nations International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights
Article 9(1):
Everyone has the right to liberty and security of person. No one shall be subjected to arbitrary arrest or
detention. No one shall be deprived of their liberty except on such grounds and in accordance with such
procedures as are established by law.
Article 10(1):
All persons deprived of their liberty shall be treated with humanity and with respect for the inherent dignity of
the human person.
The European Convention on Human Rights (ECHR)
(See appendix I and publications as circulated by the Garda Human Rights Office, Garda College)

1

Appendix II Procedures

Section One Arrest
1. Power of arrest
Arrests may be made either with a warrant, or, if there is a common law or statutory power to do so, without
a warrant.

1.1. Arrest under warrant
To effect an arrest under warrant the arresting Garda must have secured prior permission (a warrant) from the
appropriate authority. Most arrests are, however, effected without warrant (also known as summary arrest).
The powers of arrest without warrant are identified below.

1.2. Arrest at Common Law
Common law powers of arrest have largely been superseded by statute. However, the common law power of
summary arrest for the common law offence of breach of the peace remains. Both a citizen and a member of
Garda Síochána enjoy a common law power of arrest for breach of the peace. Either may arrest a person who
has committed or is committing such an offence in their presence or someone who they reasonably believe is
going to commit a breach of the peace in the immediate future.

1.3. Arrest without warrant pursuant to statute
Section 4 of the Criminal Law Act, 1997 and section 30 of the Offences Against the State Act, 1939 create
powers of arrest for a range of offences. Numerous other statutes create powers of arrest in relation to
individual offences.
1.3.1. Section 4 of the Criminal Law Act 1997
The section confers powers of arrest on any person and on members of An Garda Síochána in relation to
arrestable offences. An arrestable offence is defined in section 2(1) of the Act (as amended by the Criminal
Justice Act 2006) as:
“an offence for which a person of full capacity and not previously convicted may, under or by
virtue of any enactment or the common law, be punished by imprisonment for a term of five
years or by a more severe penalty and includes an attempt to commit any such offence”
The section creates a power of arrest in relation to arrestable offences in the following situations:
Where an arrestable offence is being committed or there is reasonable cause to suspect it is
being committed
Any person may arrest anyone without warrant who is or whom he or she, with reasonable cause, suspects to
be in the act of committing an arrestable offence
Where an arrestable offence has been committed
Where an arrestable offence has been committed any person may arrest without warrant anyone who is or
whom he or she, with reasonable cause, suspects to be guilty of the offence.
Where there is reasonable cause to suspect that an arrestable offence has been committed
Where a member of the Garda Síochána, with reasonable cause, suspects that an arrestable offence has
been committed, he or she may arrest without warrant anyone whom the member, with reasonable cause,
suspects to be guilty of the offence.
Reasonable cause/suspicion
Reasonable cause/suspicion is not defined by the Criminal Law Act 1997 but the test for reasonable suspicion
has been described in Dallison v Caffrey [1964] 2 ALL ER 610 as “whether a reasonable man, assumed to
know the law and possessed of the information which in fact was possessed by [?] would believe
that there was reasonable and probable cause.”1
1: Dallison v Caffrey [1964] 2 A11 ER 610

2

appendix2 final final 2:Layout 1

10:20

Page 3

Appendix II Procedures

The fact that the arrested person is released without being charged2 or, having been charged, is later acquitted
of the crime does not necessarily mean that the suspicion which formed the basis for the arrest was
unreasonable. Reasonable suspicion may constitute facts and information which may not be admissible in
evidence or, even if admissible, may not form part of a prima facie case. For example, the furnishing of a false
alibi may form grounds for reasonable suspicion but such an alibi may not be admissible in evidence.3
The test for reasonable suspicion relates to what was in the mind of the arresting Garda when the power was
exercised.4
Formulating reasonable suspicion should be determined by three factors:
• the amount of information available;
• the sources of the information; and
• the credibility of the sources.
Reasonable suspicion may be based on hearsay evidence provided to the Garda if it provides reasonable
grounds for suspicion and the Garda actually believes it.5
A Garda can only act on the basis of hearsay evidence when forming a reasonable suspicion if they have taken
steps to satisfy themselves of its probable truth.
It is also permissible for a Garda to take into account the criminal record of an accused when forming a
reasonable suspicion.6

1.4. Detention Short of Arrest
There is no legal power to detain an individual, short of arrest, for the purpose of conducting an investigation
into a criminal offence. The most important statement of the law in this context is to be found in the
judgement of Hanna J in Dunne v Clinton7 where he stated;
“The first question that arises is whether this detention is something different from arrest or
imprisonment. In law there can be no half-way house between the liberty of the subject,
unfettered by restraint, and an arrest. If a person under suspicion voluntarily agrees to go to a
police station to be questioned, his liberty is not interfered with, as he can change his mind at
any time. If, having been examined, he is asked and voluntarily agrees to remain in the barracks
until some investigation is made, he is still a free subject, and can leave at any time. But the
practice has grown up of “detention” as distinct from arrest. It is, in effect, keeping a suspect in
custody…without making a definite charge against him…there [can] be no such thing as
notional liberty, this so-called detention amounts to arrest, and the suspect has in law been
arrested and in custody during the period of his detention…”

1.5. Section 30 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 (as amended by
Offences Against the State Amendment Act 1998)
The section confers a number of powers, including the power of arrest, on members of An Garda Síochána in
relation to offences under any section of the Act and offences scheduled under the Act (commonly referred to
as ‘Scheduled Offences’). Section 30(1) provides as follows:
“A member of the Garda Síochána (if he is not in uniform on production of his identification card
if demanded) may without warrant stop, search, interrogate, and arrest any person, or do any
one or more of those things in respect of any person, whom he suspects of having committed or
being about to commit or being or having been concerned in the commission of an offence
under any section or sub-section of this Act or an offence which is for the time being a scheduled
offence for the purposes of Part V of this Act or whom he suspects of carrying a document
relating to the commission or intended commission of any such offence as aforesaid or whom he

5: O’Hara v. Chief Constable for Northern Ireland [1997] 1 ALL ER 129
6: McArdle v. Egan [1933] 150 LT 412

Section 30(2) of the Act empowers a member of An Garda Síochána to search vehicles and vessels which they
suspect to contain persons whom they are entitled to arrest by virtue of section 30(1) - see further under
Section Two ‘Search’.

1.6. Other Statutes conferring a power of arrest
Numerous other statutes create a power of arrest in relation to specific offences – these include (note that this
is by no means an exhaustive list):
• Misuse of Drugs Act 1977, s 25
• Criminal Justice (Public Order) Act 1994, s 24
• Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act 1990, s 14
• Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006, s 3(8)
• Criminal Damage Act 1991, s12
• Numerous sections of the following road traffic acts: Road Traffic Act 1961, Road Traffic (Amendment) Act
1968, Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 1978

1.7. Effecting an Arrest
An arrest has been described as the “seizure or touching of a person’s body accompanied by a form of
words which indicate to that person that he is under restraint”.8 Physical contact is not necessary,
however. Words alone are enough to effect an arrest where “they are calculated to bring, and do bring, to
the person’s notice that he is under restraint and he submitted to the compulsion.”9
The person being arrested must be informed of the factual basis for the arrest and the legal authority for it.10
Only reasonable and necessary force may be used to effect an arrest. At common law a member of the Garda
Síochána, and any citizen, can use such force as is reasonably necessary to effect or maintain a lawful arrest or
to prevent crime.11 In addition, section 19(1) of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 provides:
“The use of force by a person in effecting or assisting in a lawful arrest, if only such as is
reasonable in the circumstances as he or she believes them to be, does not constitute an offence.”
Neither statute nor common law provide for a definition of what amounts to ‘reasonable force’. What is
reasonable will depend on the individual circumstances under which the arrest is taking place, including the
offence for which the person is being arrested. There must be a reasonable proportion between the level of
force used and the offence in question12 and only moderate and proportional force will be compatible with the
European Convention on Human Rights.13
On arrest a person must be conveyed to a Garda station with reasonable dispatch14 although detention
occurring during a temporary delay before the suspect is conveyed to a Garda station may be permissible if for
a ‘reasonable’ period of time.
Section 4(5) of the Criminal Law Act 1997 explicitly states that where a person is arrested by someone other
than a member of the Garda Síochána, they must be transferred to the custody of the Garda Síochána as soon
as is practicable.
Best practice when effecting an arrest is for the Garda to act as follows:
• show the suspect their identification if not in uniform
• place a hand on the suspect’s shoulder
• tell the suspect in plain language that they are under arrest and for what offence (also a legal requirement)
• caution the suspect (also a legal requirement) using the words: “You are not obliged to say anything unless
you wish to do so but what ever you say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”

9: DPP v. McCreesh [1992] 2 IR 239

Appendix II Procedures

• Any reply (or silence) to the caution must be recorded exactly in the Garda’s notebook and the suspect
should be asked to sign it as a true record. Any refusal to sign the note book should be recorded by the
arresting Garda.

1.8 Reasonable Force
There must be a reasonable proportion between the amount of force used and the offence in question15 and
only force that is strictly proportionate and no more than is absolutely necessary will be compatible with the
European Convention on Human Rights.16
The following two cases taken under the ECHR Convention illustrate this point.
McCann
In McCann and others v. United Kingdom17 three members of the IRA were killed by British security forces in
Gibraltar. A car bomb had been planted and the soldiers believed they were likely to have carried a detonator.
The British Government claimed that the killing was justified under Article 2 paragraph 2(a) as the use of force
that was no more than reasonably necessary to defend innocent bystanders from unlawful violence.
The court said that the belief as to the use of force had not merely to be honest but reasonable and although
the soldiers entertained this belief the identities of these people were known to British authorities and it would
have been possible to arrest them prior to entering Gibraltar and before any placing of the car bomb. The use
of lethal force was thus more than was absolutely necessary and there was a violation of Article 2 of the
Convention. The court also expressed the view that in assessing actions under examination, it must consider
not only the actions themselves but also such matters as planning and control of those actions.
Makaratzis
In Makaratzis v. Greece18 the court expanded on the planning and control issue. The court found a violation of
Article 2 where the applicant was shot at but not killed after a police chase. Since the police believed the
applicant to be armed and dangerous the use of force was reasonable. However, the court was “struck with
the chaotic way in which the firearms were actually used” with a large number of officers acting
erratically and uncontrollably, without a clear chain of command and that domestic law provided insufficient
guidance as to the use of firearms by the police.
This case is authority for the proposition that even when the use of force is reasonable the manner in which it is
used invites consideration under the Convention and in particular the planning and control of a police operation.

1.9. Other Principles Emerging from the ECHR Convention in Relation to
Reasonable Force
The Court has also established that the protection of civilians and bystanders must be an operational priority
and has extended that protection to not only intentional killings but the killings of civilians and others not
central but collateral to the operation.
The court has also microscopically examined the nature of weapons used and in particular whether a more powerful
weapon than that which is absolutely necessary to use has been availed of. The court will examine operational
assertions and in several cases has examined whether despite state assertion there was a terrorist threat.
The court has in fairness to authorities accepted an on the spot honest and reasonable belief and
has been reluctant to second guess such immediate operational decisions.
The court has also indicated that it will only examine a killing when there is evidence that the domestic judicial
system is inadequate or unable to uncover the truth.20

Appendix II Procedures

1.10. The Constitution and Reasonable Force
It might also be noted that under the Constitution in McGee v. AG,21 Walsh J specifically recognised the State
had a positive obligation to ensure by its laws as far as practicable that life would not be endangered.
Further, Attorney General v X,22 establishes that a real and substantial risk to life invokes the right to life clause
under the Constitution and that the state cannot engage in an administrative act which creates a real and
substantial risk to life.

1.11. The Obligation to Inform of the Reasons for Arrest
Under the European Convention of Human Rights and in particular under Article 5 of the Convention a person
must be informed in simple non-technical language of the legal and factual grounds for arrest23. The individual
must be informed promptly though the information need not be conveyed in its entirety at the moment of
arrest. Indeed, it is sufficient that if the suspect finds out the precise reasons for arrest during questioning or
interrogation.24
Convention case law also establishes that arrest must be based on both honest and reasonable suspicion
(assessed objectively) and that uncorroborated hearsay is insufficient.25

Appendix II Procedures

Section Two Search and Powers of Entry
Under the constitution every person enjoys the right to the inviolability of their dwelling house and the right to
bodily integrity and therefore no person can be subjected to a search of themselves or their premises unless it
is on the basis of one of the following:
• the person’s consent
• a specific statutory power
• on foot of a lawful arrest
• on foot of a valid search warrant
Any search should comply with Article 3 of the European Convention which contains the prohibition against
inhumane and degrading treatment and Article 8 (privacy considerations).

2.1. Stop and search of persons prior to arrest
In the absence of consent or a valid warrant, persons can only be detained and searched prior to arrest if there
is a statutory power to do so. The legal authority and reason for the search must be communicated to a
suspect before any search is carried out.26
Examples of statutes which confer such powers are:
• Section 30 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 allows a member of An Garda Síochána to
“stop… [and]…search…any person whom he suspects of having committed or being about to commit or
being or having been concerned in the commission of…” any offence in the 1939 Act or any offence
scheduled for the purpose of that Act. The section also empowers a member to stop and search (by
reasonable force if necessary) any vehicle, ship, boat or other vessel which they suspect to contain a person
whom they are empowered to arrest without warrant by virtue of the section.
• Section 8 of the Criminal Law Act 1976 - confers a power of search in respect of a vehicle which has
been stopped in certain circumstances. A member of An Garda Síochána may exercise the power where
they suspect with reasonable cause that an offence within the scope of the section has been or is about to
be committed.
• At borders and ports - A member of the Garda Síochána does not enjoy a general power to stop and
search people as they enter or leave the State. However, there are a number of disparate powers which
collectively might be considered to produce the same result.
• Section 23 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 enables a member of An Garda Síochána to search any
person whom they reasonably cause to suspect is in possession of a controlled drug in breach of the Act.
Where a member entertains a reasonable suspicion with respect to a person they may also stop and search
any vehicle, vessel or aircraft in which they suspect such drugs may be found.
In order to effect the search the Gardai may detain the person for a reasonable period, even though they have
not been arrested,27 but if incriminating objects are found on the person the Garda is obliged to arrest, charge
and to take the person into custody.

2.2. Power to enter and search premises to effect an arrest
The Constitution in Article 40(5) provides that:
“The dwelling of every citizen is inviolable and shall not be forcibly entered save in accordance with
law.” A dwelling therefore enjoys a special constitutional protection which other premises do not.

Appendix II Procedures

2.2.1. Entry by invitation
A Garda may enter private premises with the consent of the occupier but, in such circumstances, must leave
when asked to do so unless they have effected an arrest in the premises (in which case it is permissible to
search the immediate vicinity pursuant to the common law power to search an arrested person and his or her
immediate vicinity - see 2.3 below).
2.2.2. Entry on foot of an arrest warrant
Section 6(1) of the Criminal Law Act 1997 provides that a Garda in possession of an arrest warrant may enter
any premises in which the Garda has reasonable cause to believe the wanted person to be and effect an arrest
(by reasonable force if need be).
2.2.3. Entry without an arrest warrant
Section 6(2) of the Criminal Law Act 1997 provides that where no warrant of arrest exists and the Gardai wish
to arrest an offender for an arrestable offence, they may enter (if need be, by use of reasonable force) and
search any premises (including a dwelling) where they have reasonable cause to suspect that person to be.
Where the premises concerned are a dwelling, a Garda may not enter the premises without the consent of the
occupier or person in charge of the premises unless:
• a Garda has observed the offender enter the premises
• the Garda has reasonable cause to suspect that to delay whilst a warrant is obtained would obstruct the
course of justice; or the offender will abscond; or the offender will commit an arrestable offence
• the offender resides at that address.

2.3. Search of a person and their immediate environment on foot of a valid arrest
A Garda has the power at Common Law to search a person who they have arrested.28
When effecting an arrest, the Garda may seize anything in possession of the arrested person when they
believe it necessary to do so to avoid the abstraction or destruction of that property and when that property is:
• evidence in support of the charge on foot of which the arrest was made; or
• evidence in support of any other criminal charge against the person arrested then being contemplated; or
• reasonably believed to be stolen property or property otherwise unlawfully in the possession of the
arrested person.29
The powers of search and seizure outlined above also extend to the immediate area where the subject
was arrested, including any parts of a premises where a person was arrested to which that person had
immediate access.30
The powers of seizure of property conferred by section 7 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006 and section 9 of the
Criminal Law Act 1976 also apply in this situation (see section 2.5.4 below).
Searches made on foot of an arrest should, where possible, be conducted out of the public view. The suspect
should be asked to account for property found and their replies noted and cautioned if appropriate.

2.4. Search of detained persons
Section 6 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984
This section confers a power to search persons detained under section 4 of the CJA Act 1984 (which relates to
arrestable offences - see 1.3 above):
• Where a person is detained pursuant to section 4, a member of the Garda Síochána may ... search him or
cause him to be searched ...AND ...seize and retain for testing anything that he has in his possession
• Subsection (1) (b) does not empower a member of the Garda Síochána to require a person to remove his
underclothing, except where such member, with reasonable cause, suspects that he has concealed on his
person a controlled drug (within the meaning of section 2 of the Misuse Of Drugs Act, 1977) or an explosive
substance and a member of the Garda Síochána not below the rank of superintendent so authorises.

Appendix II Procedures

Section 30(5) of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 (as amended by Offences Against the State
Amendment Act 1998)
A member of the Garda Síochána may search a person detained under the section or cause him to be
searched. See below under (Section 3 para 1.2) for detention under this section.
2.4.1. Procedure for search of persons in custody
Regulation 17 of the Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Síochána Stations Regulations 1987 states:
17. (1) A member conducting a search of a person in custody shall ensure, so far as practicable, that the
person understands the reason for the search and that it is conducted with due respect for the person
being searched.
(2) A person in custody shall not be searched by a person (other than a doctor) of the opposite sex.
(3) Where a search of a person in custody involves removal of clothing, other than headgear or a coat,
jacket, glove or similar article of clothing, no person of the opposite sex shall be present unless either
that person is a doctor or the member in charge considers that the presence of that person is
necessary by reason of the violent conduct of the person to be searched.
(4) A search of a person in custody involving removal of underclothing shall, where practicable, be carried
out by a doctor.
(5) Where clothing or footwear of a person is retained, replacements of a reasonable standard shall be
provided.
(6) A record shall be made of a search of a person in custody including the name of the person
conducting the search and the names of those present.
(7) Particulars of any property taken from or handed over by a person in custody shall be recorded. The
person shall be asked to sign the record of such property as being correct. If he refuses to do so, the
refusal shall be recorded at the time of refusal.
2.4.2. Precautions advised to be taken by those searching detained persons
It is advised that those searching arrested persons take great care and in particular:
• The Garda should tell the person in custody that they are going to search them.
• Having told the person in custody, the Garda should ask if they have anything in their possession which
might cause injury. Most persons in custody will indicate, when asked, if they have anything sharp in their
possession.
• Arresting and searching members must be mindful of the need to wear protective gloves to avoid infection
from bodily fluids and pricks from needles used by drug addicts.
• Where possible a visual search should be made first.

2.5. Powers to search premises
As stated earlier a dwelling enjoys a special constitutional protection which other premises do not.
2.5.1. Without a search warrant
Where a valid arrest is made on private property, the Gardai have the power to search those parts of the
property in the possession or control of the suspect at the time of arrest (see 2.2 and 2.3 above).
Statutory Powers - the legislature has provided the Gardaí with a number of specific powers of entry and
search without a warrant. These can be divided into the following broad categories:
Where suspicion of an offence is not necessary
Powers here mostly concern premises which are being used for purposes which present the potential for antisocial behaviour, criminal or subversive activity, public health and environmental risks and danger to person or
property or customers or employees. Statutes which confer such power include the Betting Act 1931, s26,
Abattoirs Act 1988, s54, and the Air Navigation and Transport Act 1988, s18. In some cases the entry and
search of a dwelling is specifically excluded.

Appendix II Procedures

On reasonable suspicion
A member of An Garda Síochána enjoys a range of powers to enter and search premises without a warrant
where there are reasonable grounds to believe that a relevant offence is being committed.
Statutes which confer such power include the Chemical Weapons Act 1997 s 8, and the Child Care Act
1991 s12.
In addition, a number of other statutes also confer powers of search and entry. One such statute is the Mental
Health Act 2001 s 13(4) which confers a power to enter premises for the purpose of taking a person into
custody for the purposes of receiving mental health treatment.
2.5.2. With a Search Warrant
A search warrant can only be obtained where there is a statutory provision authorising the issue of such a
warrant. It is important that the statutory requirements are complied with fully when issuing warrants (see DPP
v Dunne [1994] 2 IR 537) and the content of a warrant should be fully checked.
Search warrants in relation to arrestable offences
The Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act 1997, s10(1) (as amended by s.6 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006)
provides:
If a judge of the District Court is satisfied by information on oath of a member not below the
rank of sergeant that there are reasonable grounds for suspecting that evidence of, or relating to,
the commission of an arrestable offence is to be found in any place, the judge may issue a
warrant for the search of that place and any persons found at that place.
“Place” is defined as a physical location and includes:
(a) a dwelling, residence, building or abode,
(b) a vehicle whether mechanically propelled or not,
(c) a vessel, whether sea-going or not,
(d) an aircraft, whether capable of operation or not, and
(e) a hovercraft
A warrant issued under this section permits a named Garda, accompanied by such other members or persons
as the member thinks necessary, to enter the place named in the warrant at any time or times within one week
of the date of issue of the warrant. The warrant must be produced if requested at the time of entry.
Reasonable force may be used if necessary.
Search warrants in relation to offences which are not arrestable offences
In any given instance it is necessary to check the legislation for the following:
• Whether there is a power to issue a search warrant in relation to the particular offence
• Who may apply for the warrant
• Who may issue the warrant
• The grounds for issuing a warrant
• The length of validity of the warrant
• Whether there is a power (other than the power to search on foot of an arrest) to search persons on the
premises – there is no power to do so unless expressly conferred by the relevant statute.
Application for warrants to search to District Court Judge
An application for a warrant to search premises is made on sworn information to a Judge of the District Court
under the relevant Act. The warrant is valid until it is executed or until its time limit expires per the relevant Act
- for example a drugs search warrant remains valid in force for one month and the Criminal Justice (Theft and
Fraud Offences) Act 2001 limits validity to only seven days. Any error in the warrant must be corrected by the
Judge not the Garda.

Appendix II Procedures

It is best practice before any application is made for a warrant, to ensure that the details of any person or
premises are accurate and must be checked and verified. It may also be advisable to make any application for a
warrant, especially to search for evidence, in the privacy of the Judge’s Chambers but this will depend on the
judge.
Statutes authorising a member of the Garda Síochána to issue search warrants
An officer of the rank indicated below (or above) may issue an authority to search premises without sworn
information being required under the following Acts:
• Section 29 of the Offences Against the State Act 1939 - Superintendent
• Section 24 of the Firearms Act 1925 - Superintendent
• Section 73 of the Explosives Act 1875 - Superintendent
• Section 16 of the Official Secrets Act 1963 - Chief Superintendent
• Section 26 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977 (as amended by section 8 of the Criminal Justice Drug
Trafficking Act 1996) - Superintendent
• Prevention of Corruption Act 1906 (as amended by the PCA 2001 & Section 191 CJA 2006) Superintendent
It is for the member authorising to justify, when called upon, that they reasonably believed there was a need to
issue the authority to search.
Execution of the warrant
When executing the warrant the searching Garda should have it in their possession and be ready to read it to the
occupier if required. Where access is refused, only sufficient force as is necessary may be used to gain entry.
It is good practice to make clear to the occupier at the outset of the search what is being sought and ask if
such items are within the premises and where. The question posed and the answer elicited should be recorded
and the occupier cautioned if appropriate. Upon gaining access, the property outlined in the warrant should
be searched for and, if found, seized and removed.
2.5.3. Termination of search
No unnecessary damage should be caused to the premises and where possible the premises should be restored
to their original state. The owner of the property should be asked at the conclusion of the search if they have
any complaint about the condition of the premises and where possible the complaint should be attended to
and rectified. An inventory of all property seized should be prepared by the investigating Gardai and this
inventory agreed with the occupier/person in charge/suspect.

2.6. Seizing of property
Section 9 of the Criminal law Act 1976
Where in the course of exercising any powers under CLA’ 76 or in the course of a search carried out under any
other power, a member of the Garda Síochána, a Prison Officer or a member of the Defence Forces finds or
comes into possession of anything which they believe to be evidence of any offence or suspected offence, it
may be seized and retained for use as evidence in any criminal proceedings
Note that it does not matter that the power of search was confined to an offence totally unrelated to that
associated with the suspected offence for which the goods were seized or that the warrant specifically
prescribed the goods to be seized.
However, the section only applies to property found during a lawful search and therefore does not appear to
apply if a search was wider than that lawfully allowed. For instance, a warrant to search for a stolen grand
piano would not permit the seizing of a small amount of cannabis found behind a couch.
Section 7 of the Criminal Justice Act 2006
Where a member of the Garda Síochána who is in a public place, or any other place under a power of entry
authorised by law or to which they were expressly or implicitly invited or permitted to be, finds or comes into

Appendix II Procedures

possession of any thing, and they have reasonable grounds for believing that it is evidence of, or relating to,
the commission of an arrestable offence, they may seize and retain the thing for use in any criminal
proceedings.
Note that this power may be used in relation to a public place or any other place to which the Garda has
lawfully gained entry.
Any property seized pursuant to either of the above enactments will be dealt with in accordance with the
Police Property Act 1897 regarding storage and later disposal.
It is good practice to carefully record precisely what property was found, when and where the item was found
and by whom. The occupier can be asked questions regarding any property found to facilitate the
effectiveness of the search. Any answers or silences from the occupier should be recorded. Should the occupier
wish to volunteer any statement or admission, the usual caution should be administered and the terms of the
Judges Rules complied with.

2.7. Designation of Place as a Crime Scene
The Criminal Justice Act 2006 s5 makes detailed provision for the designation of a place as a crime scene (both
in relation to public places and any other places where a member is present under a power of entry authorised
by law) where the member has reasonable grounds for believing:
• An arrestable offence was, is being, or may have been committed; or
• There is, or may be, in the place evidence of, or relating to, the commission of an arrestable offence that
was or may have been committed elsewhere.
Section 5(3) provides that a Garda not below the rank of Superintendent, where they have reasonable grounds
for doing so, may give a direction designating a place as a crime scene. Subsection (4) authorises members to
take certain steps as they reasonably consider necessary to preserve, search for and collect evidence at the
crime scene to which the direction under subsection (3) relates.
Subsection (18) provides that nothing in Section 5 of the Act prevents the designation of a place as a crime
scene, or a Garda from taking any of the steps referred to in subsection (4), if the owner or occupier of the
place consents.
Designation of a place as a crime scene (CJA 2006 s5): Constitutional and ECHR Convention
Considerations
It is worth noting that certain constitutional and European Convention implications arise from this provision.
First, Gardai are given the power to direct and remove people from the crime scene. The exercise of such
powers must not infringe on bodily integrity (Article 40.3 of the Constitution) or constitute inhuman and
degrading treatment (Article 3 of the Convention). Second, there are implications arising from the utilisation of
the crime scene as a dwelling which impact on privacy (Article 40.3 of the constitution, Article 8 of the
Convention), inviolability of the dwelling (Article 40.4 of the Constitution) and family rights (Article 41 of the
Constitution, Article 8 of the Convention). The section also authorises Gardai to permit people to come onto
the crime scene and this again could have implications in regard to the issues identified above.
Superintendents in deciding whether or not to designate a scene under Section 5 of the Criminal Justice Act,
2006 should consider the above issues in the event that any consent given by an owner/occupier is
subsequently withdrawn.
Where members are complying with the terms of statutory authority, their actions in preserving and uplifting
evidence from crime scenes are likely to be upheld by the courts in the event of any subsequent dispute by the
owner - for example, where incriminating evidence was found implicating the owner/occupier in the
commission of a criminal offence.

Appendix II Procedures

Where there is any doubt about the owner/occupier’s consent in any given case, it will always be safer to issue
a direction under Section 5(3) and it will always be good practice to record any consent given by the
owner/occupier in accordance with section 5 (18) in writing.

2.8. The Irish Constitution and Searches: Article 38 and Article 40.5
2.8.1. Obtaining Real Evidence/Search Warrants: Article 38 and Article 40.5
Article 40.5 (the protection of the dwelling) commonly comes into play when the Gardai have procured a
search warrant for a premises or where they are conducting a search without a warrant. The Constitutional
rules in this respect are dealt with under the heading of “unconstitutionally obtained evidence” which is also
an emanation of Article 38 and Due Process on which, a brief discussion follows;
O’Brien
In People (Attorney General) v O’Brien31 it was found that evidence obtained as a result of a deliberate breach
of a constitutional right should be excluded, unless there are extraordinary excusing circumstances, which
justify its admission. If the evidence has been obtained as the result of a mere illegality not amounting to the
breach of a constitutional right, then such evidence is admissible subject to the discretion of the trial judge to
exclude where for example the judge is satisfied the illegal act was the result of a settled or deliberate policy.
In O’Brien the Gardai had proceeded to search the accused person’s home on foot of a warrant, which mistakenly
recorded his address. The resulting evidence was admissible because the error was purely formal and accidental.
Kenny
O’ Brien was amplified upon and clarified in The People (DPP) v Kenny32 where the applicant challenged the
constitutionality of a search conducted on the basis of a warrant issued by a Peace Commissioner. The search
warrant was invalid by reason of the fact that no evidence was tendered on which the PC had satisfied himself
there were reasonable grounds for suspecting the existence of controlled drugs on the premises and he had
relied entirely on the written information sworn by the Garda. Finlay CJ emphasised a PC cannot act as a mere
rubber stamp. In considering the element of consciousness necessary (in respect of the Garda who conducted
a search based on a warrant with an inherent defect), Finlay CJ said:
“The correct principle is that evidence obtained by invasion of the constitutional personal rights
of a citizen must be excluded unless a court is satisfied that either the act constituting the
breach...was committed unintentionally or accidentally, or is satisfied that there are extraordinary
excusing circumstances. Detection of crime and conviction no matter how important cannot
outweigh the unambiguously expressed constitutional obligation as far as practicable to
vindicate the personal rights of the citizen.”
Finlay CJ pointed out that to exclude only evidence obtained by a person who knows he is violating a
constitutional right would be to impose a negative deterrent only; an absolute protection rule, however, also
incorporates a positive encouragement to acquaint oneself with the personal rights of the citizen. In these
circumstances it was immaterial whether the police officer was or was not aware that what they were doing
was in breach of the constitutional rights of the accused.
Healy
In The People (DPP) v. Healy33 McCarthy J. rejected the good faith submission, an emanation of American
Constitutional Law, in that:
“a violation of constitutional rights is not to be excused by the ignorance of the violator, no more
than ignorance of the law can ensure to the benefit of a person who is presumed to have
intended the natural and probable consequences of his conduct. If it were otherwise, there
would be a premium on ignorance.”

31: [1965] IR 142

32: [1990] 2 IR 110

33: [1990] 2 IR 73

Appendix II Procedures

Balfe
In DPP v Balfe34 Murphy J distinguished O’Brien and Kenny in terms of the fact that a search warrant that
innocently but vitally misdescribes premises which may be searched on foot of it is not without operative
effect. Property seized in innocent reliance on such a warrant may be admissible, but where a warrant is made
without authority it has no value in law however innocent the mistake.
Kelly in his work “The Irish Constitution” observes that:
“Perhaps Balfe and, indeed, O’Brien can be regarded as examples of where the errors were de
minimus which did not ultimately affect the validity of the search warrant. In Balfe, however,
Murphy J described the error as a serious one, but concluded that because the defect was patent
(rather than latent as in Kenny), the exclusionary rule did not come into play.”
Extraordinary excusing circumstances
In O’Brien, the case that coined the phrase Walsh J gave examples of what might amount to extraordinary
excusing circumstances, rendering admissible evidence, which would normally be inadmissible as obtained in
breach of the Constitution. These were the need to rescue a victim in peril or to prevent the imminent
destruction of vital evidence.
In The People (DPP) v. Shaw,35 the chance of finding a victim alive was such an extraordinary excusing
circumstance to justify the lengthy detention of the accused.
In The People (DPP) v. Lawless36 the police found seventeen packets of heroin in a manhole beside a flat. These
packets had apparently been flushed down the lavatory as the Gardai were entering the house. The court
considered that this fell into the category of the need to prevent the imminent destruction of vital evidence.
Mere eagerness on the part of the gardai to extend their investigations into offences other than that in
connection with which a person originally submitted to questioning does not amount to an extraordinary
excusing circumstance sufficient to justify detention. The People (DPP) v. O’ Loughlin.37
An extension of the principle is found in Freeman v. DPP38 where the gardai, having chased subjects, followed
them into a private dwelling where they found stolen property. Carney J held this was an extraordinary
excusing circumstance and opined:
“It is hard to think of circumstances more apt to come within Walsh J’s exception than the facts
of this case; there was simply not enough time for the gardai to obtain the necessary search
warrant.”
Recent Potential Changes in the ambit of the Exclusionary Rule
In The DPP v. Cash,39 Charleton J. ruled that the position of the court is that unlawfully obtained evidence may
be excluded and will be excluded if it constitutes an unfair trial.
The judge argued that a balance be struck and the remorseless exclusion of mistakes is not consonant with the
proper ordering of society which is the purpose of the criminal law. The judge also argued that Kenny
undermines O’Brien in that the principle that extraordinary excusing circumstances can allow for the admission
of evidence obtained in breach of a constitutional right can no longer be applied. On the facts and in
answering the case stated Charleton J found that Kenny should not be extended as to its effects to require the
prosecution to prove that every element of an investigation was entirely proper and in accordance with
statutory powers.
2.8.2. Search and Other Convention Considerations
As far as personal non dwelling searches are concerned the relevant constitutional and Convention
considerations would be the protection of privacy and the prohibition against inhumane and degrading
treatment. Such considerations would undoubtedly impact in relation to intimate and forcible searches. It
might also be noted that any search would have to be effected with reasonable force and the Convention
would frown on the disproportionate use of force.

Appendix II Procedures

In Wainwright v. United Kingdom,40 one of a number of recent cases concerning strip and intimate searches,
the court indicated generally that with respect to searches that:
“A search carried out in an appropriate manner with due respect for human dignity and for a
legitimate purpose may be compatible with Article 3. However, where the manner in which a
search is carried out has debasing elements which significantly aggravate the inevitable
humiliation of the procedure, Article 3 has been engaged: for example, where a prisoner was
obliged to strip in the presence of a female officer, his sexual organs and food touched with bare
hands and where a search was conducted before four guards who derided and verbally abused
the prisoner (Iwa czuk v. Poland, no. 25196/94, § 59, 15 November 2001). Similarly, where the
search has no established connection with the preservation of prison security and prevention of
crime or disorder, issues may arise (see, for example, Iwa czuk, cited above, §§ 58-59 where the
search of the applicant, a model remand prisoner, was conducted on him when he wished to
exercise his right to vote; Van der Ven v. the Netherlands, no. 50901/99, §§ 61-62, ECHR 2003-II,
where the strip-searching was systematic and long term without convincing security needs).”
Thus the court has set out the parameters for any such search that the police or prison authorities might
undertake.
The court also noted in Wainwright pointing out the interaction between Article 3 and the privacy right in
Article 8 that:
“There is no doubt that the requirement to submit to a strip-search will generally constitute an
interference under the first paragraph of Article 8 and require to be justified in terms of the
second paragraph, namely as being ‘in accordance with the law’ and ‘necessary in a democratic
society’ for one or more of the legitimate aims listed therein. According to settled case-law, the
notion of necessity implies that the interference corresponds to a pressing social need and, in
particular that it is proportionate to the legitimate aim pursued (see e.g. Olsson v. Sweden,
judgment of 24 March 1988, Series A no. 130, § 67).”
It might be noted that Wainwright found a violation of these rights in the context of a sloppy search of relative
visitors to a prison.

Section Three Detention and Treatment of Persons in Custody
This section does not address the detention of persons under 18. The Children Act 2001 contains detailed
provisions regarding the detention of such persons.

3.1. Powers of Detention and periods of detention
A number of statutes confer both general and specific powers on the Gardai to detain suspects. Some of
these statutory powers of detention are outlined below:
3.1.1. Section 4, Criminal Justice Act, 1984
This section provides a power of detention in relation to persons arrested for arrestable offences. That is
offences which are punishable by five years imprisonment or more and attempts to commit such offences.
Maximum periods of detention permitted
The detention periods provided for under this section for such offences are:
(I) 6 hours from time of arrest
(II) A further 6 hours where directed by an officer not below the rank of Superintendent
(III) A further 12 hours (additional to those at (ii) above) where directed by an officer not below the rank of
Chief Superintendent
(IV) A direction under (ii) or (iii) above may be given orally or in writing but where it is given orally it must be
recorded in writing as soon as is practicable).
The clock will stop running in relation to the detention periods outlined above in the following situations:
If the Member In Charge (MIC) authorises suspension of questioning for a specified period between midnight
and 8am to allow the detainee to rest, then that rest period will be excluded when reckoning the period of
detention. (s4 (6)(a)) – note that this only applies where questioning is suspended pursuant to this section.
If the detainee is in need of medical attention and is taken to hospital the period that the detainee is absent
from the station will be excluded when reckoning the period of detention (s4(8))
Where detainee is taken to a court in connection with an application relating to the lawfulness of his
detention, the time during which he is absent from the station for that purpose will be excluded when
reckoning the period of detention (s4(8)(a)).
Grounds on which detention is permitted
The member/officer authorising detention must have reasonable grounds for believing that such detention is
necessary for the proper investigation of the offence for which arrested.
Procedure where there are no longer reasonable grounds for suspecting the person has committed
an offence to which the section applies
Where there are no longer reasonable grounds for suspecting that the person has committed an offence to
which the section applies, they must be released immediately unless another power of detention applies.
3.1.2. Section 30, Offences Against the State Act 1939 (as amended by Offences Against the State
Amendment Act 1998)
This section provides a power of detention for offences under any section of the act and offences scheduled
under the Act.
The maximum detention periods provided for under this section are:
a)
b)
c)

24 hours from time of arrest
A further 24 hours where directed by an officer not below the rank of Chief Superintendent
A further 24 hours (additional to those at (b) above) where authorised by a warrant obtained from a
District Judge on application by an officer not below the rank of Superintendent:

• An application for such a warrant may only be made if the officer applying for the warrant has reasonable

• At an application for such a warrant the detained person must be produced and the judge must hear any
submissions made and consider any evidence adduced by or on behalf of the person and the officer of the
Garda Síochána making the application.
Charge of a person detained under this section
A person detained under this section may, at any time during such detention, be charged before the District
Court or a Special Criminal Court with an offence.
Release of a person detained under this section
A person detained under this section:
• May at any time be released by direction of an officer of the Garda Síochána.
• Must, if not charged or already released, be released at the expiry of the authorised detention period.
Powers of Gardai in relation to persons detained under this section
A member of the Garda Síochána may do all or any of the following things in respect of a person detained
under this section:
• demand of such person his name and address;
• search such person or cause him to be searched;
• photograph such person or cause him to be photographed;
• take, or cause to be taken, the fingerprints and palm prints of such person.
• seize and retain for testing anything that he has in his possession.
3.1.3. Section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996
The section provides a power of arrest in relation to persons arrested for a drug trafficking offence – the

the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 which provides as follows:
Drug trafficking offence means any of the following:
“(a) an offence under any regulations made under section 5 of the Misuse of Drugs Act, 1977, involving
the manufacture, production, preparation, importation, exportation, supply, offering to supply,

(b) an offence under section 15 of that Act of possession of a controlled drug for unlawful sale or supply,
(bb) an offence under section 15A of that Act
(bbb) an offence under section 15B of that Act
(c)
an offence under section 20 of that Act (assisting in or inducing the commission outside the State of

(e) an offence under section 31 of the Criminal Justice Act 94 in relation to the proceeds of drug
trafficking, (Money Laundering).
(f)
an offence under section 33 or 34 of the Criminal Justice Act 94, or (Drug Trafficking on Irish Ships,
Ships used for Drug Trafficking).
(g) an offence of aiding, abetting, counselling or procuring the commission of any of the offences
mentioned in paragraphs ( a ) to (f) of this definition or of attempting or conspiring to commit any
such offence or inciting another person to do so”

3.2. Treatment of Persons in custody at a Garda Síochána Station
The following is a summary of the regulations and is intended to be a quick reference guide for
members. Members are advised to make themselves aware of the full text of these Regulations as
they are of critical importance in dealing with prisoners in custody.
Criminal Justice Act 1984 (Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Síochána Stations) Regulations
1987 (“the regulations”)
These regulations apply to all persons detained in Garda Stations under whatever legal power, not only those
detained under the 1984 Act.
They do not apply to the period from arrest to arrival at the station nor to any period spent in a Garda vehicle
while under arrest. The regulations have no application to a person held in Garda custody in a place other
than a Garda station.
It is good practice to keep a custody record even if a person is attending a Garda Station voluntarily and is not
under arrest.
A failure on the part of any member of the Garda Síochána to observe any provision of the regulations shall
not of itself render that person liable to any criminal or civil proceedings or affect the lawfulness of the custody
of the detained person or the admissibility in evidence of any statement made by them. (Criminal Justice Act
1984 s7(3))
A failure on the part of any member of the Garda Síochána to observe any provision of the regulations shall
render them liable to disciplinary proceedings. (Criminal Justice Act 1984 s7(4))
3.2.1. Regulation 3 provides as follows:
3.(1) In carrying out their functions under these Regulations members shall act with due respect for the
personal rights of persons in custody and their dignity as human persons, and shall have regard for the
special needs of any of them who may be under a physical or mental disability, while complying with
the obligation to prevent escapes from custody and continuing to act with diligence and determination
in the investigation of crime and the protection and vindication of the personal rights of other persons.
3.(2) There shall be no unnecessary delay in dealing with persons in custody.
A copy of the regulations must be kept in each Garda station.

Appendix II Procedures

3.2.2. Member in Charge (MIC)
The regulations create the role of MIC and provide for a number of duties to be carried out by the MIC in
relation to persons in custody including an over-riding duty to oversee the application of the regulations to
persons in custody. The member who is designated the MIC in any particular case is the member who is in
charge of the station at the time these duties are to be carried out and as far as is practicable should not be a
member who was involved in the arrest of the person in custody or in the investigation of the offence for
which they have been arrested.
The Superintendent should issue instructions in writing as to who is to be the MIC in each station in the
District and ensure that a record is maintained in each station recording the name and rank of the MIC at any
time. (see regulations 4 and 5)
3.2.3. Custody Record
A ‘custody record’ must be kept for each person in custody. The MIC must ensure that all the required
information is kept in this record and each entry must be signed or initialled by the member making it. The
overall responsibility for the accuracy and completeness of the custody record lies with the MIC. (see
regulation 6)
Custody records must be kept for a period of:
• at least12 months; or
• until the final determination of any proceedings in respect of which the record is relevant; or
• until the final determination of any complaint against a member while the person was in custody,
whichever is the later. (Regulation 12)
A copy of the record must be supplied as soon as is practicable if requested by the person who was in custody or
their legal representative at any time between their leaving custody and 12 months after that. (regulation 24)
3.2.4. On arrival at the Garda station
The arresting Garda should take the person in custody directly to the public office where the arresting Garda
will state clearly to the MIC:
• their name, rank and any identifying warrant number and/or details of any other person effecting the arrest
• the name of the person in custody
• the fact that the person in custody is under arrest
• the reason for the arrest
• the time, date and place of arrest
• any information regarding the condition of the person in custody (for example, if they are drunk, injured or
have medication needs)
• a request for detention of the person in custody to allow for the proper investigation of the offence (for
example interview the person in custody or further enquiries)
The MIC will record carefully all the information in a custody record and will consider the request for
detention. If detention is granted, the MIC will say so and proceed accordingly.
It is an essential requirement of a valid detention that the Member in Charge, separately and
independently of the arresting member, considers the information available to them and on that
basis reaches a conclusion that there are reasonable grounds for believing that the detention is
necessary for the proper investigation of the offence in question.
Directions authorising the extended detention of a prisoner must also be recorded in the custody record (see
regulation 7 and the relevant provisions authorising the extended detention).

3.2.5. Information to be given to persons in custody
The MIC must ensure that arrested persons are given the following information:
• Details of the offence or other matter for which they have been arrested
• That they are entitled to consult a solicitor (see also under legal advice at 3.3 below)
• That they are entitled to have another person notified of their being in custody, or, if under 17 that a
parent, guardian or other responsible adult has been told of the reason for their arrest and requested to
attend the station without delay
• The MIC is required to give the person in custody a notice outlining their rights and request the prisoner
to acknowledge receipt in writing. The time of this action must be recorded.
(Regulation 8)
3.2.6. Enquiries about the presence of a person in custody at a Garda Station
Regulation 10 requires that, with the consent of the person in custody, information as to the station where the
arrested person is in custody shall be given in answer to any enquiry from a solicitor who has not been
requested by the person in custody. Such information may also be given in response to an enquiry from any
other person if the person in custody consents and if the MIC is satisfied that giving the information will not
hinder or delay the investigation of crime.
3.2.7. Conditions of custody
Regulation 19 contains detailed rules governing the care and treatment of a person in custody. It states:
• A person shall be kept in custody only in a station which has facilities to enable them to be treated in
accordance with the Regulations for the period during which they are expected to be in custody in that station.
• A person in custody shall be allowed such reasonable time for rest as is necessary.
• A person in custody shall be provided with such meals as are necessary and, in any case, at least two light
meals and one main meal in any twenty-four hour period. They may have meals supplied at their own
expense where it is practicable for the member in charge to arrange this.
• Access to toilet facilities shall be provided for a person in custody.
• Where it is necessary to place persons in custody in cells, as far as practicable not more than one person
shall be kept in each cell.
• Persons of the opposite sex shall not be placed in a cell together.
• A violent person shall not be placed in a cell with other persons if this can be avoided.
• Where a person is kept in a cell, a member shall visit him at intervals of approximately half an hour. A
drunken person or a person under the influence of drugs shall be visited and spoken to and if necessary
roused for this purpose at intervals of approximately a quarter of an hour for a period of two hours or
longer if their condition warrants it.
• A member shall be accompanied when visiting a person in custody of the opposite sex who is alone in a cell.
• A person in custody under the age of seventeen years shall not be kept in a cell unless there is no other
secure accommodation available and where practicable shall not be placed in a cell with an adult other
than an adult relative.
3.2.8. Prohibition on use of force except reasonable force as is necessary in certain situations
Regulation 20(1) and (2) provide as follows:
20. (1) No member shall subject a person in custody to ill-treatment of any kind or the threat of ill-treatment
(whether against the person themselves, their family or any other person connected with them) or
permit any other person to do so.
(2) No member shall use force against a person in custody except such reasonable force as is necessary (a) in self-defence,
(b) to secure compliance with lawful directions,
(c) to prevent escape, or
(d) to restrain them from injuring themselves or others, damaging property or destroying or
interfering with evidence.
