Appendix II Procedures

The regulation also sets out detailed reporting requirements in the following situations:
• where a member uses force against and injures a person in custody, or
• where it comes to the attention of a member that another member has contravened the provisions of
regulation 20, or
• where a person in custody makes a complaint concerning the conduct of a member
3.2.9. Medical Treatment of Persons in Custody
Where a person in custody:
• is injured,
• is under the influence of intoxicating liquor or drugs and cannot be roused,
• fails to respond normally to questions or conversation (otherwise than owing to the influence of
intoxicating liquor alone),
• appears to the member in charge to be suffering from a mental illness, or
• otherwise appears to the member in charge to need medical attention.
The MIC should summon a doctor or cause one to be summoned or ensure the removal of the person to
hospital, whichever is appropriate. In addition, medical advice should be sought in relation to persons who
claim they need medication for or have in their possession medication for a heart condition, diabetes, epilepsy
or other potentially serious condition (see Regulation 21).
3.2.10. Telephone calls and visits of friends and relatives and provision of writing materials
Any telephone calls to friends and relatives or visits allowed by the MIC will be monitored. Where it is
considered that continuance of a call or visits would interfere with the investigation it may be terminated. The
person in custody must be warned before the call or visit that anything said in the call or visit may be given in
evidence. Any request for writing materials by the person in custody will be attended to and met (See
Regulation 11).

3.3. Right to Legal advice
The Supreme Court decision in DPP v Healy41 established a detainee’s right of access to a lawyer as a
constitutional right:
“…. I am driven to the conclusion that such an important and fundamental standard of fairness
in the administration of justice as the right of access to a lawyer must be deemed to be
constitutional in its origin, and that to classify it as merely legal would be to undermine its
importance and the completeness of the protection of it which the courts are obliged to give.”
3.3.1. Right under Section 5 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984
This establishes a right of persons detained under section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act,1984 to be informed of the
right of access to a solicitor and the right to have the solicitor informed of a request to exercise the right of access.
Requirements of the regulations in relation to access to legal advice
• An arrested person must have reasonable access to a solicitor of their choice and be able to communicate
with them privately. (See regulation 11 (1))
• The MIC must inform the person in custody that they are entitled to legal advice. (See regulation 8)
• Where an arrested person has asked for a solicitor the MIC must inform the solicitor as soon as practicable
and if the solicitor cannot be contacted within a reasonable time or if the solicitor is unable or unwilling to
attend the person must be given an opportunity to ask for another solicitor and the MIC should then
inform the other solicitor as soon as is practicable. (See regulation 9)
• Where an arrested person asks for a solicitor, he shall not be asked to make a written statement in relation
to an offence until a reasonable time for the attendance of the solicitor has elapsed. (Regulation 12(6))

Appendix II Procedures

• Where an arrested person has not had access to a solicitor and a solicitor who he or she has not requested
arrives at the station and requests a consultation with the arrested person the arrested person must be
allowed to consult that solicitor if they wish to do so. (See regulation 11(2))
• A consultation with a solicitor may take place within sight but out of hearing of a member. (Regulation 11 (3))
The solicitor may attend the Garda station or the consultation may take place by telephone (but in either case
it must take place out of hearing of a member).
The length of consultation to be allowed is suggested as 30 minutes however this has not been tested in the
courts.
Legal Aid can cover advice given in the Garda station. When the solicitor arrives they will ask the detainee to
complete a means-tested application form to ensure they are eligible.
A solicitor may be represented by a legal executive and granted the same facilities by the Garda as if he were a
solicitor.
Parameters of the Right
The right is one of reasonable access and not an absolute right. An MIC must make all reasonable and bona
fide attempts to contact a solicitor but where there is a considerable delay in the arrival or securing of a
solicitor, it will not necessarily breach the constitutional right of the accused (or the regulations) if questioning
commences before the solicitor arrives42. However, this practice is contrary to the jurisprudence of the ECHR
and the decision in Averill v United Kingdom43
There is no provision for stopping the clock in relation to detention periods while a solicitor is being obtained.
The right to access to legal advice does not extend to the right to have a solicitor present during interview.44

3.4. Powers to search, photograph, fingerprint and take forensic samples from a
person in custody
For searches see Section 2 above.
Where a person has been detained under section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 the MIC may photograph
them if the person consents or, in the absence of such consent, where authority has been given by a member
not below the rank of Inspector. A person who obstructs a member who is carrying out this power is guilty of
an offence. (Section 6 of the 1984 Act)
An arresting Garda, on the authority of a sergeant or above, may photograph an arrested person or cause
them to be photographed at a Garda Station for the purpose of assisting in the identification of the person in
connection with any proceedings which may be instituted in respect of the offence for which he or she is
arrested. It is an offence for a person to refuse to allow themselves to be photographed pursuant to this
provision. (Criminal Justice Act 2006 s12).
No video recording of an interview may be used to make a photograph of a person except with the consent of
that person (Criminal Justice Act 1984 (Electronic Recording of Interviews) Regulations 1997 regulation 17)
Fingerprinting of persons in detention
Persons detained under the following provisions may be fingerprinted, and to obstruct or impede a garda
exercising this power is an offence:
• Criminal Justice Act 1984 s4 (see section 6)
• Offences Against the State Act 1939 s30 (see s30(5)(6))
• Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 (see s5 of that act)
• Criminal Justice Act 2007 section 50

Appendix II Procedures

As with the provisions pertaining to photographing, a person detained under a relevant statutory provision
may have their fingerprints taken, either by consent,45 or in the absence of such consent on the authority of an
Inspector46 or higher ranking officer.
Use of Force
Under the provisions of section 6A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 (as inserted by Section 48 Criminal Justice
Act, 2007) if a person fails or refuses to allow their photograph or fingerprints to be taken this can be done by
the use of such force as the member taking the prints or photograph reasonably considers to be necessary. If
prints or photographs are taken by force this must by authorised by a member of at least Superintendent rank
and in the presence of a member of at least Inspector rank.
Destruction of Records
Previously, there was an obligation on State authorities to destroy fingerprints and photographs taken pursuant
to section 6 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 within specified periods if proceedings where not instituted or
convictions obtained. Section 49 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007 substitutes a new section 8 into the Criminal
Justice Act 1984 whereby there is now no automatic duty to destroy prints or photographs.
Instead, a person can after 12 months make a request to the Garda Commissioner for the destruction or to
limit the of use of prints and photographs held by Gardai. A refusal of such request must be reasoned and can
be appealed to the District Court.
The previous obligation to destroy and the new obligation to consider a request to destroy do not apply to
prints or photographs taken by consent rather than pursuant to section 6 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984.

The taking of bodily samples from persons in detention
This is governed by the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 as amended by the Criminal Justice Act
2006. The following discussion is intended as a guide for members resorting to the section and should not be
taken as a precise legal interpretation. Members are referred to the provisions of the Act and the Criminal
Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 Regulations, 1992.
Members should note that the DPP has advised that where a suspect consents to provide or permit the taking
of a sample it is not necessary to invoke the provissions of the Act. In such cases care should be taken to warn
the suspect that they are not oblged to give a sample and that the results of any tests on the sample may be
given evidence in any proceedings. This caution should be video recorded, if possible.
This Act confers the power on a member of An Garda Siochana not below the rank of Superintendent (Section
2(4)(a) to authorise the taking of bodily samples for forensic testing from persons who are in Garda custody
under the provisions of:
• Section 30, Offences Against the State Act, 1939 as amended
• Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984, as amended
• Section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996 as amended
• Section 50 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007.
The power to authorise the taking of samples also extends to persons in prison in circumstances where the
sample is required in connection with an offence other than the offence for which the suspect is in prison and
the investigation concerns one of the following offences:
• An offence under the Offences Against the State Act, 1939 as amended or any scheduled offence under
Part V of the OASA 1939
• An offence to which Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 applies
• A drug Trafficking Offence within the meaning of Section 3 (1) of the Criminal Justice Act, 1994 as
amended (see Section 3.1.3 above)
• An offence to which Section 50 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007 applies

The Superintendent before giving the authorisation under Section 2(4)(a) must have reasonable
grounds for suspecting the involvement of the person from whom the sample is to be taken in:
(1)
(11)

the offence for which the person is in custody in the case where the person is in Garda custody
the case where the person is in prison, in one of the offences listed above in respect of persons in
prison;
(111) and, in either (i) or (ii) above that the sample will tend to confirm or disprove the involvement of the
person from whom the sample is to be taken in the said offence.
When properly authorised by the Superintendent, the following samples may be taken without the
‘appropriate consent’ of the suspect in writing:
• Saliva
• Hair other than pubic hair
• A Nail
• Any material found under a nail
• A swab from any part of the body including the mouth but not from any other body orifice or a genital
region. Mouth in this context also includes inside the mouth
• A footprint or similar impression of any part of the person’s body other than a part of his hand
Where a sample of hair other than pubic hair is taken in accordance with this section (a) the sample may be taken by plucking hairs with their roots and, in so far as it is reasonably practicable,
the hairs shall be plucked singly, and
(b) no more hairs shall be plucked than the person taking the sample reasonably considers to be necessary
to constitute a sufficient sample for the purpose of forensic testing.
The ‘appropriate consent’ of the suspect must be obtained in writing where it is intended to take a sample of:
• Blood
• Pubic hair
• Urine
• A swab from a body orifice, other than the mouth, or a genital region
• A dental impression
Additionally, in the above cases the samples can only be taken by a doctor or a dentist as appropriate.
‘Appropriate consent’ means the consent of:
• The person if they are over 17;
• If the person is between 14 and 17, the person and their parent or guardian
• If the person is under 14, their parent or guardian.
Before a member takes or causes a sample to be taken or seeks the consent of the person from
whom the sample is required for the taking of such a sample, the member must inform the person:
• of the nature of the offence in which it is suspected that the person has been involved
• that the Superintendent has given an authorisation under section 2(4)(a) to take the sample and the
grounds on which this authorisation has been given, and
• that the results of any tests on the sample may be given in evidence in any proceedings
A person who obstructs or attempts to obstruct a member or any other person acting under the powers
conferred by subsection 2(1) is guilty of an offence.

Appendix II Procedures

Where a consent required by section 2 of the Act is refused without good cause, then the court in determining
whether the accused has a case to answer or whether the charge should be dismissed under Part 1A of the
Criminal Procedure Act, 1967, may draw such inferences from the refusal as appear proper. This also applies
to the determination of whether the person is guilty of the offence charged. The refusal may be treated as
corroboration of any evidence on which the refusal is material, but critically, the person cannot be
convicted of an offence solely on an inference drawn from the refusal.
No such inference can be drawn unless the accused was told in ordinary language by a member
of the Garda Siochana when seeking their consent that the sample was required for the purpose
of forensic testing, that their consent was necessary and, if consent was not given, what the
effect of a refusal by them of such consent would be.
Furthermore, the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act, 1990 Regulations, 1992 require that the following
matters are recorded in the custody record or prison record as appropriate;
• The name, rank of the Superintendent (or higher rank) who gave the authorisation under Section 2 (4) (a)
and the date and time when it was given.
• A record shall be made where a member provides information in accordance with Section 2 (6) (nature of
the offence, that Superintendent has authorised the taking of the sample and that results may be given in
evidence and so on) that the information was given to the suspect together with the name and rank of
the member who gave it and the date and time it was given.
• Where a person whose consent is required to the taking of a sample under Section 2 (4) (b) gives or
refuses his consent, a record shall be made in the custody record recording the refusal or consent together
with the date and time of the giving or refusal and where consent is given in a document, such document
shall be attached to the custody record.
• Where a sample has been taken, the following detail must be recorded in the custody record:
• The name of the person from whom the sample was taken
• The offence in respect of which the sample was taken
• The nature of the sample
• The name of the person who took the sample
• The date and time the sample was taken and the manner in which it was taken.
• A sample shall not be taken from a person under 17 nor shall the consent of such person to the taking of
a sample be sought except in the presence of a parent or guardian of that person.
Destruction of records and samples
Section 4 of the Criminal Justice (Forensic Evidence) Act 1990 as amended provides;
4. (1) Subject to subsection (5) of this section , every record identifying the person from whom a sample has
been taken pursuant to section 2 of this Act shall, if not previously destroyed, be destroyed as this
section directs and every sample identified by such record shall be destroyed in like manner.
(2) Where proceedings for any offence in respect of which a person could be detained under section 30
of the Offences against the State Act, 1939, section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 , or section 2
of the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996 or section 50 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007, are
not instituted against the person from whom the sample was taken within twelve months from the
taking of the sample and the failure to institute the proceedings within that period is not due to the
fact that he has absconded or cannot be found, the destruction of the record and the sample
identified by such record shall be carried out on the expiration of that period unless an order has been
made under subsection (5) of this section .
(3) Where proceedings have been so instituted and (a) the person is acquitted,
(b) the charge against the person in respect of the offence concerned is dismissed under section 4E
of the Criminal Procedure Act 1967, or
(c) the proceedings are discontinued, the destruction of the record and the sample identified by such
record shall be carried out on the expiration of twenty-one days after the acquittal, dismissal or
discontinuance unless an order has been made under subsection (5) of this section.

Appendix II Procedures

Section Four Questioning Suspects
4.1. Presence of Solicitor
When a person in custody has been arrested and detained they are not entitled to have a solicitor present at
any interview whilst in detention. However, where a suspected person, not in detention, attends a voluntary
interview they may have a solicitor present and continued legal advice throughout the interview.

4.2. The Judges Rules 1922
The rules were originally four in number and were drawn up by the judges of the King’s Bench Division in 1912
at the request of the Home Secretary for clarification of the circumstances in which police officers could
question a person. By 1922 these had increased to nine in number and these are set out in detail at Chapter 4.

4.3. Requirements of the Custody Regulations
The Criminal Justice (Treatment of Persons in Custody) Regulations, 1987 were made under section 7 of the
Criminal Justice Act, 1984. Section 7 (3) provides that a failure on the part of any member of the Garda
Síochána to observe any provision of the regulations shall not of itself render that person liable to any criminal
or civil proceedings or of itself affect the lawfulness of the custody of the detained person or the admissibility
in evidence of any statement made by him. A member who breaches the Regulations can however be
disciplined.
Notwithstanding section 7(3) the provisions should be followed and a Judge can always exercise their
discretion to deem custody unlawful or exclude evidence as a result of a breach.
Regulation 12 makes the following provisions for interviewing of suspects at a Garda Station:
12. (1) Before an arrested person is interviewed, the member conducting the interview shall identify himself
and any other member present by name and rank to the arrested person.
(2) The interview shall be conducted in a fair and humane manner.
(3) Not more than two members shall question the arrested person at any one time and not more than
four members shall be present at any one time during the interview.
(4) If an interview has lasted for four hours, it shall be either terminated or adjourned for a reasonable time.
(5) As far as practicable interviews shall take place in rooms set aside for that purpose.
(6) Where an arrested person asks for a solicitor, he shall not be asked to make a written statement in
relation to an offence until a reasonable time for the attendance of the solicitor has elapsed.
(7) (a) Except with the authority of the member in charge, an arrested person shall not be questioned
between midnight and 8 a.m. in relation to an offence, which authority shall not be given unless (i) he has been taken to the station during that period,
(ii) in the case of a person detained under section 4 of the Act, he has not consented in writing to
the suspension of questioning in accordance with subsection (6) of that section, or
(iii) the member in charge has reasonable grounds for believing that to delay questioning the
person would involve a risk of injury to persons, serious loss of or damage to property,
destruction of or interference with evidence or escape of accomplices.
(b) Subparagraph (a) (i) is subject to the provisions of Regulation 19 (2). (a person in custody shall be
allowed such reasonable time for rest as is necessary.

(8) (a) Where an arrested person is deaf or there is doubt about his hearing ability, he shall not be
questioned in relation to an offence in the absence of an interpreter, if one is reasonably available,
without his written consent (and, where he is under the age of seventeen years, the written consent
of an appropriate adult) or in the circumstances specified in paragraph (7) (a) (iii).
(b) A consent shall be signed by the arrested person and be recorded in the custody record or a
separate document.
(c) Where an arrested person has requested the presence of an interpreter under subparagraph (a) and
one is not reasonably available, any questions shall be put to him in writing.
(9)

An arrested person who is under the influence of intoxicating liquor or drugs to the extent that he is
unable to appreciate the significance of questions put to him or his answers shall not be questioned

(7) (a) (iii).

(10)

If, while being interviewed, an arrested person makes a complaint to a member in relation to his

is not present at the interview, and record it or cause it to be recorded in the record of the interview.

(11) (a) A record shall be made of each interview either by the member conducting it or by another member
who is present. It shall include particulars of the time the interview began and ended, any breaks in
it, the place of the interview and the names and ranks of the members present.
(b) Where an interview is not recorded by electronic or other similar means, the record shall (i) be made in the notebook of the member concerned or in a separate document and shall be as
complete as practicable,
(ii) if it is practicable to do so and the member concerned is of opinion that it will not interfere with
the conduct of the interview, be made while the interview is in progress or otherwise as soon as
practicable afterwards, and
(iii) be signed by the member making it and include the date and time of signature.
(12) (a) A record shall be made of the times during which an arrested person is interviewed and the
members present at each interview.
(b) Where an authority is given pursuant to this Regulation, the fact that it was given, the name and

(c) The fact that an arrested person has consented in writing under section 4 (6) of the Act to the
suspension of questioning between midnight and 8 a.m. shall be recorded and the consent shall be
attached to and form part of the custody record.
(d) The particulars specified in section 4 (6) (d) of the Act shall be recorded.

4.4. Suspension of Questioning of Person in Custody
Regulation 12(7) states that persons should not be questioned between midnight and 8am except in certain
situations, when detained under section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984. Section 4(6) of the Criminal Justice
Act 1984 (as amended) provides that where persons are detained under section 4 of the Act the MIC may
determine that any questioning of the person in custody should be suspended (for no more than one period)
in order to afford the person in custody a reasonable period of rest.
This is provided that the person in custody consents. Any such break will not be included in determining the
length of detention time but this only applies where questioning is suspended under this section and the
person is detained under section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984.
Where questioning is suspended under this section the MIC may rescind the rest period for a “serious reason”
as specified in Regulation 12.7(c)(iii).

Appendix II Procedures

4.5. Restrictions on a Suspect’s Right to Silence
Situations where a person being questioned is required to give certain information and where a
failure to do so constitutes an offence
In certain specific situations the law requires information to be provided by the suspect and failure to do so will
constitute an offence.
In each situation, however, the requirement does not come into effect unless the person being
asked to give the information has been told in ordinary language by a member of the Garda
Síochána what the effect of his failure or refusal might be.
It is also vital to note that while a person may be obliged to give information under pain of
prosecution, such information cannot be used for the purpose of evidence against them. This is
because such evidence was not given voluntarily and its reliability is therefore inherently
suspect.47
Care should also be exercised in relying on any of the following provisions in terms of questioning the accused
in connection with the crime under investigation. In general, admissions made by an accused person are only
admissible in evidence if made voluntarily and in accordance with the Judge’s rules (see discussion on this topic
at chapter 4.). The following statutory Provisions all impact on the right of silence formerly enjoyed by an
accused person. If the investigating member resorts to the terms of the following provisions at an early stage
in the suspect’s detention or questioning, any admissions made by the accused subsequently could be open to
challenge on the basis that the accused believed that he/she was legally obliged to make the admission or was
confused as to his/her obligations in this regard. This essentially will be an issue for decision by the Senior
Investigating Officer in charge of any investigation, however best practice will dictate in many cases that the
accused should be interviewed under the terms of the traditional caution set out in the Judges rules from the
commencement of questioning/detention and only when no further progress is likely to be achieved in terms
of admissions volunteered by an accused person should reliance be placed on any legal provisions requiring an
accused person to answer questions where failure to answer will result in either the commission of a criminal
offence or alternatively the possibility of an adverse inference being raised against him/her. In these cases the
original caution given will obviously have to be withdrawn and the requirements of the relevant legal provision
being relied on complied with.
4.5.1. Requirement when detained under section 4 Criminal Justice Act 1984 to give name and
address
A person detained under section 4 Criminal Justice Act 1984 has an obligation to give their name and
address when required by a member of the Garda Síochána. (s6(2) of that Act)
4.5.2. Requirement to give information regarding possession of firearms or ammunition
Section 15 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 provides as follows:
15. (1) Where a member of the Garda Síochána (a) finds a person in possession of any firearm or ammunition,
(b) has reasonable grounds for believing that the person is in possession of the firearm or
ammunition in contravention of the criminal law, and
(c) informs that person of his belief, he may require that person to give him any information which is
in his possession, or which he can obtain by taking reasonable steps, as to how he came by the
firearm or ammunition and as to any previous dealings with it, whether by himself or by any
other person.
(2) If that person fails or refuses, without reasonable excuse, to give the information or gives information
that he knows to be false or misleading, he shall be guilty of an offence.

This requirement applies even if the person has not been arrested or is not in Garda custody.
Any information received in compliance with this requirement is not admissible in evidence against that
person or his spouse in any proceedings, civil or criminal, other than proceedings for an offence under
subsection (2). (s15(4))
4.5.3. Requirement to give information about stolen property
Section 19 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 provides as follows:
19. (1) Where a member of the Garda Síochána (a) has reasonable grounds for believing that an offence consisting of stealing property or of handling
stolen property has been committed,
(b) finds any person in possession of any property,
(c) has reasonable grounds for believing that the property referred to in paragraph (b) includes, or
may include, property referred to in paragraph (a) or part of it, or the whole or any part of the
proceeds of that property or part, and
(d) informs the person of his or her belief,
the member may require the person to give an account of how he or she came by the property.
(2) If the person fails or refuses, without reasonable excuse, to give such account or gives information
that the person knows to be false or misleading, he or she is guilty of an offence.
This requirement applies even if the person has not been arrested or is not in Garda custody.
Any information received in compliance with this requirement is not admissible in evidence against that
person or his spouse in any proceedings, civil or criminal, other than proceedings for an offence under
subsection (2). (s19(4))

4.6. Inferences from Silence
Prior to the introduction of section 19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984, by section 30 of the Criminal Justice
Act 2007, the general rule of law was that no inference could be drawn from an accused person’s silence
while being questioned48.
4.6.1. Statutes which allow the court to draw such inferences where a suspect fails to mention a fact
later relied on in his or her defence
Certain statutory provisions – these being s.5 of the Offences Against the State (Amendment) Act, 1998 and
s.7 of the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996 – have, by way of exception to the general rule
provided for the drawing of inferences from silence, in circumstances where a defendant later relies on facts in
their defence in relation to which they were previously silent, for the purpose of corroborating any evidence
material to such facts.
Section 19A of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 (as inserted by section 30 of the 2007 Act) now
provides for such inferences in relation to all arrestable offences (i.e. all offences carrying a prison
sentence of 5 years or more).
• Inferences can be drawn by judge or jury:
• where the defendant is relying on a fact as part of their defence – i.e. if they do not put up any form of
positive defence (even in cross examining prosecution witnesses) the provision does not appear to apply;
• where the circumstances clearly call for an explanation;
• where there is some other prosecution evidence which can be corroborated by the accused’s silence – a
person cannot be convicted on inferences from silence alone and silence does not appear to be
independent corroboration of guilt, but corroboration of prosecution evidence in relation to
which the silence is material.

48: see for e.g. DPP v. Finnerty [1999] 4 IR 364

Section 30 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007 provides as follows;
“…30. – The act of 1984 is amended by the insertion of the following section after section 19:
19A. (1) Where in any proceedings against a person for an arrestable offence evidence is given
that the accused(a) at any time before he or she was charged with the offence, on being questioned by a
member of the Garda Siochana in relation to the offence, or
(b) when being charged with the offence or informed by a member of the Garda
Siochana that he or she might be prosecuted for it,
failed to mention any fact relied on in his or her defence in those proceedings, being a
fact which in the circumstances existing at the time clearly called for an explanation from
him or her when so questioned, charged or informed, as the case may be, then, the court,
in determining whether a charge should be dismissed under Part 1A of the Criminal
Procedure Act 1967 or whether there is a case to answer and the court (or, subject to the
judge’s directions, the jury) in determining whether the accused is guilty of the offence
charged (or of any other offence of which he or she could lawfully be convicted on
that charge) may draw such inferences from the failure as appear proper; and the
failure may, on the basis of such inferences, be treated as, or as capable of amounting
to, corroboration of any evidence in relation to which the failure is material…”

4.6.2. Provisions where inferences can be drawn from failure or refusal to account for objects,
substances or marks, or from failure or refusal to account for presence at a particular place.
Both of the following sections apply in relation to all arrestable offences:
Criminal Justice Act 1984 s18 (as amended by s. 28 CJA 2007) - Failure to account for object,
substance or mark:
The section applies where
(a) in relation to the arrested person there is:
(i) on his person, or
(ii) in or on his clothing or footwear, or
(iii) otherwise in his possession, or
(iv) in any place in which he is at the time of his arrest
any object, substance or mark, or there is any mark on any such object, and the member reasonably believes
that the presence of the object, substance or mark may be attributable to the participation of the person
arrested in the commission of the offence in respect of which he was arrested, and
(b) the member informs the person arrested that he so believes, and requests him to account for the
presence of the object, substance or mark, and
(c) the person fails or refuses to do so.
Criminal Justice Act 1984 s19 (as amended by s.29 CJA 2007) - Failure to account for presence at a
particular place:
This section applies where:
(a) the arrested person was found by the member at a particular place at or about the time the offence in
respect of which he was arrested is alleged to have been committed, and
(b) the member reasonably believes that the presence of the person at that place and at that time may be
attributable to his participation in the commission of the offence, and
(c) the member informs the person that he so believes, and requests him to account for such presence, and
(d) the person fails or refuses to do so.

Appendix II Procedures

Requirements, including Caution, for Inference to be drawn
These inferences cannot be drawn unless:
• the accused was told in ordinary language when being questioned, charged or informed, as the case may
be,
• what the effect of the failure to account for a matter/account for their presence at a particular
place/mention a fact later relied on in their defence might be - i.e.
• that they were properly cautioned in relation to inferences from silence; and
• the accused was afforded a reasonable opportunity to consult a solicitor before such a failure
occurred; and
• the questioning was electronically recorded or the accused consented in writing to it not being
recorded.
Note in this context that section 32 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007 provides for the making of Ministerial
regulations in relation to the administration of cautions by Gardai to detained persons. The regulations made
include provision for the forms of caution to be administered to a person in different circumstances and in
different classes of cases, and the procedures that are to apply where a caution has to be withdrawn and a
different caution administered.49
The above are safeguards built into the legislation to counterbalance the withdrawal of the historic right to
silence from an accused person.

4.7. Electronic Recording of interviews
The electronic recording of interviews is governed by the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 (Electronic Recording of
Interviews) Regulations, 1997 (“ERI Regulations”). The regulations state: “Any failure to comply with a
provision of the regulations shall not by itself render a person liable to civil or criminal proceedings,
and (without prejudice to the power of the court to exclude evidence at its discretion) shall not by
itself render inadmissible in evidence anything said during such questioning.”
4.7.1. Interviews which must be recorded electronically (ERI regulations 3 and 4)
The regulations apply to stations where the equipment has been provided and installed and where the person
being interviewed has been detained under one of the following provisions:
• Criminal Justice Act 1984 (arrestable offences);
• Offences Against the State Act 1939;
• Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996; and
• Criminal Justice Act 2007 s.50.
Such interviews must be electronically recorded unless the MIC certifies that the equipment is not working, or
is not available due to being already in use and to delay the interview would hinder the course of justice.
4.7.2. Written record of interview (ERI regulation 4(5))
Even where an interview is electronically recorded it is still necessary to comply with the provisions of custody
regulation 12(11)(b)(i), (ii) and (iii) which require a written record to be made of the interview
contemporaneously or as soon as practicable thereafter and should be signed and dated.50
4.7.3. Commencement of the interview (ERI Regulation 6)
Before the commencement of the interview the MIC should ensure the person to be interviewed is informed that
the interview will be electronically recorded and that he or she will be entitled to receive a notice as to what is to
happen to the tapes of that interview. They must also ensure they are provided with a copy of this notice.
Before recording commences the investigator breaks the seals on the required number of tapes in front of the
person in custody and inserts them into the recording equipment and starts to record.

49: Note: At time of writing, no such regulations have been made. There appears however to be nothing preventing any relevant sections
operating and appropriate cautions being administered in the abscence of such regulations.
50: Due to the introduction of video recording technology this requirement is being reviewed but is still in force at the time of writing

Appendix II Procedures

4.7.4. Initial interview procedure (ERI Regulation 6)
The investigating garda should caution the person in custody in the following terms:
“You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but whatever you say will be taken
down in writing and may be given in evidence. As you are aware this interview is being taped and
the tape may be used in evidence.”
And then state:
• the name, rank and identification number of the members present
• the name and status of any other people present
• the name of the person being interviewed
• the date, time and place of the interview
• that the person in custody has been given a notice of what will happen to the tape recording of the
interview and its availability later
• the purpose of the interview
4.7.5. Interruption of recorded interviews (ERI Regulation 7)
An interview may be interrupted for any of the following reasons:
• the tape runs out and requires replacement
• the equipment requires attention
• where a break is to be taken in the interview
• the person being interviewed objects to the tape recording after the recording has commenced
4.7.6. Procedure when the tape runs out (ERI Regulation 8)
A buzzer will sound and the interviewing member should:
• tell the person in custody they are coming to the end of the tape
• state the time and reason for interrupting the interview
• switch off the recording equipment
• remove the tapes
• seal one of the three tapes as a master copy in its box with the appropriate seal and give it an
identification number
• sign the seal on the master tape and ask the person in custody to sign it also, if the person in custody
refuses to sign, the MIC is called to the interview room and signs the seal in the presence of the person in
custody and the tape remains in the sight of the person in custody until that is done
• insert a new set of tapes into the machines and repeat the process of commencing the start of a taped
interview by reminding the person in custody that they are still under caution and so on
4.7.7. Procedure when the recording equipment requires attention (ERI Regulation 9)
Only the equipment supplied for interviewing may be used to conduct an interview. (ERI Regulation 4(1))
When the equipment fails, if possible the interviewing member will state on tape what the problem is. The
member will remove the tapes, seal one as a master tape and seal it in its box, signing the seal and asking the
person in custody to sign. A refusal to sign by the person in custody will result in the MIC being asked to come
to the interview room and sign the seal.
If it is possible to restart the interview using the same or other official recording equipment, the member will
explain the failure of the earlier interview at the outset and where possible state why the recording equipment
failed. They will then remind the person being interviewed that they are under caution and repeat the
procedure outlined earlier.

4.7.8. Procedure where a break is to be taken in the interview
This may occur for various reasons including:
• if the interview is over a long period;
• is planned to take place over several interviews; or
• there is an operational requirement or need for a comfort break.
No interview shall last for more than four hours without it being terminated or an appropriate break being
taken. (Custody Regulation 12(4))
The procedure differs depending on whether or not the person being interviewed remains in the interview
room during the break.
a) Procedure where person in custody leaves the interview room during a break (ERI Regulation 10(1))
The member recording the interview will:
• state that there is to be a break and the reason for it
• state the time and switch off the recording equipment and
• remove the tapes
• seal one of the three tapes with a master seal in its box, give it an identification number, sign the seal and
ask the person in custody to sign the seal
• a refusal to sign by the person in custody requires the MIC to be called to the interview room and to sign
the seal of the master tape
• on resumption of the interview, repeat the process of starting a tape interview with a set of fresh tapes
explain that the interview is resuming
b) Procedure where person being interviewed remains in the interview room during a break
(ERI Regulation 10(2))
The interviewing member will state that there is to be a break and the reason for the break. They
will state the time and switch the recording equipment off. There is no requirement to remove
the tape. When the interview resumes, the recording equipment is switched back on. The
interviewing member will remind the person being interviewed that they are under caution and
record the time at which the interview recommenced.
The matter under investigation should not be discussed with the detained person off tape during the break.
Should an issue be alluded to by the detainee the member is advised to ask the person in custody to desist
until the interview resumes. It is good practice for the member to ask the person in custody to confirm that
nothing with regard to the matter has been discussed during the break or if it has been discussed, what was
said by who.
4.7.9. Procedure where the person being interviewed objects to the interview being electronically
recorded (ERI Regulation 11)
Even if the person being interviewed objects, the interviewing member may, as they see fit, continue the
electronic recording of the interview. (11(3))
The DPP advice is that any objection should be recorded electronically.
If the interviewing member decides to discontinue the recording due to the objection of the person being
interviewed they must:
• record the time and reason for the discontinuance
• switch of the equipment and remove the tapes
• seal one of the tapes with a master tape label, give it an identification number, sign the label and ask the
person being interviewed to sign it
• a refusal to sign by the person in custody requires the MIC to be called to the interview room and to sign
the seal of the master tape

35

Appendix II Procedures

4.7.10. Conclusion of the interview (ERI Regulation 12)
Whilst the tape is still running the interviewing member will:
• ask the person in custody if they have anything further they wish to say or clarify
• read back to the person interviewed the notes made of the questions and answers in the interview and
invite them to identify and make any alterations or additions
• record the time, switch off the recording equipment, remove the tapes, seal one of the tapes back in its
box, give it an identification number, sign the seal, invite the person in custody to sign the seal and if they
refuse call the MIC to sign instead
4.7.11. Master tapes
All master tapes must be handed to the MIC as soon as practicable after the conclusion of the interview. The
MIC will ensure that the custody record records that:
• the interview has taken place
• the date of the interview
• the MIC has received the master tapes, the date and time of this receipt and the identification numbers of
these tapes
• It is also good practice to record how the tapes have been stored safely in accordance with the procedure.
4.7.12. Breaking seals on master tapes (ERI Regulation 15)
In the rare event that a master tape seal has to be broken, it will only be done under one of the following
circumstances:
• on written request of the person in custody or their solicitor
• on the order of a court
• on the direction of the Garda Síochána Complaints Board/Ombudsman Commission
• a Chief Superintendent is investigating a disciplinary matter
In any event, the person in custody and/or their legal representative will be notified in writing of the proposal
and allowed to be present for as long as the master tape is unsealed.
If neither the legal representative nor the person interviewed is present when the seal is broken, a member not
below the rank of superintendent will be present to witness the process and the re-sealing of the tape.
The MIC will reseal the tape and the person interviewed and/or their legal representative or a member not
below the rank of superintendent will sign the seal.
4.7.13. Destruction of tapes (ERI Regulation 14)
After six months have expired from the date of the interview, the person interviewed may apply for the
destruction of the master tape and any working copies in the possession of the Garda Síochána.
The tapes will be destroyed if:
• Proceedings have not been instituted and the failure to do so is not because the person interviewed has
absconded or cannot be found; or
• Although proceedings have been instituted the person interviewed has been acquitted or discharged or the
proceedings were discontinued
This will apply unless the Superintendent of the District authorises their preservation for a further six months
on being satisfied that they may be required for the purpose of proceedings, including further proceedings.
Where the tapes are destroyed the person interviewed, his or her legal representative, or a person authorised
to act on his or her behalf will be entitled on request to witness their destruction.

Appendix II Procedures

4.7.14. Requests for copy of tape recorded interview
Under section 56 of the CJA 2007 the accused or their solicitor is entitled to a copy of the taped interview only
if the court before whom the person is charged with an offence directs that they be given a copy. The Court
can attach any conditions to this.

4.8. Voluntary interviews
Where a suspect is not arrested they may be asked to attend an interview on a voluntary basis. This may take
place in an interview room at a Garda station or the offices of the suspect’s solicitor.
Under these circumstances a solicitor may be present during the interview. As far as possible, the interview
should be recorded electronically and the procedures relating to such interviews applied.

4.9. ECHR Convention and Irish Constitutional issues regarding the Questioning of
suspects
The Right to Silence/the Privilege against Self-Incrimination/The Constitution and the Convention
The Irish Courts have accepted that the Right to Silence is either part of due process (Article 38) or protected
as a correlative of Freedom of Expression (Article 40.6)
4.9.1. The Domestic Position on the Right to Silence
In Heaney v Ireland,51 the Supreme Court upheld the constitutionality of section 52 OASA which made failure
to account for one’s movements when requested to do so under that Act a punishable offence. O’Flaherty J
located the right to silence in Article 40 as a corollary to the freedom of expression conferred by that Article.
“Just as the freedom of expression clause in the Constitution is itself qualified, so must the
entitlement to remain silent be qualified…however it is clear that the right to freedom of
expression is not absolute. It is expressly stated in the Constitution to be subject to public order
and morality. The same must hold true of its correlative right -the right to silence”.
Applying a proportionality test O’Flaherty J. concluded the power given to the Gardai by the section was
proportionate to the objects to be achieved by the legislation.
In re National Irish Bank Ltd52 the Supreme Court addressed the question as to whether legislation may validly
require a person to answer questions which tend to incriminate him or her and the answers to which questions
would be admissible in criminal proceedings against the individual. The Supreme Court indicated that such
legislation was not unconstitutional per se, but that it would be for the trial judge to decide whether such
evidence should be excluded in the interests of fairness and justice, particularly in the light of the fact that the
evidence might have been compelled. The conclusion in this case may be doubted in the light of the recent
jurisprudence in the convention.
In People (DPP) v Finnerty53 the Supreme Court held that the right of suspects in custody to remain silent,
recognised by the common law, was also a constitutional right. This case confirms the prominence of the right
to silence in Irish law and as a necessary corollary the requirement that adverse inferences may not be drawn
unless specifically provided for by statute. If the Oireachtas intends to abridge a right it must legislate. So
where a person detained under the Criminal Justice Act 1984 declined to answer Garda questions, a jury may
not be invited to draw inferences adverse to him/her from that silence. This position is now changed by the
insertion of section 19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 by section 30 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007 which
specifically provides for adverse inferences from silence.
4.9.2. The Position under the ECHR Convention
Heaney
It should be noted that Heaney was taken to the European Court of Human Rights54 where the Court found
that rather than a restriction on the right to silence Section 52 constituted an abolition of the right to silence,
which was not justified, by any emergency or consideration of public order.
51: [1996] 1 IR 580
52: [1999] 1 ILRM 321
53: [2000] 1 ILRM 193
54: [2001] 33 EHRR 264
55: No. 38544/97, (8 April 2004)

Appendix II Procedures

Weh
The case of Weh v. Austria55 summarises the present position of the ECHR:
“40.The right not to incriminate oneself is primarily concerned with respecting the will of an accused person
to remain silent.
41. A perusal of the Court's case-law shows that there are two types of cases in which it found violations of
the right to silence and the privilege against self-incrimination.
42. First, there are cases relating to the use of compulsion for the purpose of obtaining information
which might incriminate the person concerned in pending or anticipated criminal proceedings against
him, or - in other words - in respect of an offence with which that person has been “charged” within
the autonomous meaning of Article 6.
43. Second, there are cases concerning the use of incriminating information compulsorily obtained outside
the context of criminal proceedings in a subsequent criminal prosecution.
44. However, it also follows from the Court's case-law that the privilege against self-incrimination does not
per se prohibit the use of compulsory powers to obtain information outside the context of criminal
proceedings against the person concerned.
45. For instance, it has not been suggested in Saunders v UK56 (that the procedure whereby the applicant was
requested to answer questions on his company and financial affairs, with a possible penalty of up to two
years' imprisonment, in itself raised an issue under Article 6 § 1 (Saunders, ibid.;). Moreover, in a recent
case the Court found that a requirement to make a declaration of assets to the tax authorities did not
disclose any issue under Article 6 § 1, although a penalty was attached to a failure to comply and the
applicant was actually fined for making a false declaration. The Court noted that there were no pending
or anticipated criminal proceedings against the applicant and the fact that he may have lied in order to
prevent the revenue authorities from uncovering conduct which might possibly lead to a prosecution did
not suffice to bring the privilege against self-incrimination into play. Indeed, obligations to inform the
authorities are a common feature of the Contracting States' legal orders and may concern a wide range
of issues.
Furthermore, the Court accepts that the right to silence and the right not to incriminate oneself are not
absolute, as for instance the drawing of inferences from an accused's silence may be admissible. Given the
close link between the right not to incriminate oneself and the presumption of innocence, it is also
important to reiterate that Article 6 § 2 does not prohibit, in principle, the use of presumptions in
criminal law.”
John Murray v. United Kingdom57
In relation to the above extract the European Court has considered the drawing of inferences from the silence
of an accused. In John Murray v. United Kingdom the court opined:
“On the one hand, it is self-evident that it is incompatible with the immunities under
consideration to base a conviction solely or mainly on the accused's silence or on a refusal to
answer questions or to give evidence himself. On the other hand, the Court deems it equally
obvious that these immunities cannot and should not prevent that the accused's silence, in
situations, which clearly call for an explanation from him, be taken into account in assessing the
persuasiveness of the evidence adduced by the prosecution.
Wherever the line between these two extremes is to be drawn, it follows from this
understanding of "the right to silence" that the question whether the right is absolute must be
answered in the negative.

56: (1996) 23 E.H.R.R. 313

57: (1996) 22 E.H.R.R. 29

Appendix II Procedures

It cannot be said therefore that an accused's decision to remain silent throughout criminal
proceedings should necessarily have no implications when the trial court seeks to evaluate the
evidence against him. In particular, as the Government have pointed out, established
international standards in this area, while providing for the right to silence and the privilege
against self-incrimination, are silent on this point.
Whether the drawing of adverse inferences from an accused's silence infringes Article 6 (art. 6) is
a matter to be determined in the light of all the circumstances of the case, having particular
regard to the situations where inferences may be drawn, the weight attached to them by the
national courts in their assessment of the evidence and the degree of compulsion inherent in the
situation.”

Shannon
In the recent decision of Shannon v. UK,58 the European Court considered The Proceeds of Crime Act N.I.
Order 1996 and the obligation in that provision that the accused attend interview and give information to
financial investigators and found a violation of the right to silence.
The court was particularly influenced by the fact that the appellant was charged with a criminal offence but
also, it is submitted, by the fact that information at interview, could be channelled to the police conducting the
criminal investigation.
Allan
A useful interaction of the right to silence with privacy considerations is in Allan v. United Kingdom59 where
the extracting of information from conversations in prison by using a long standing informant to structure the
discussion was deemed a violation of the right to silence as it amounted to the functional equivalent of
interrogation without the safeguards of a police interview including the attendance of a solicitor and the issue
of a caution and the applicant was feeling vulnerable and open to pressure.
The court indicated that:
“The right, which as the court has previously observed is at the heart of the notion of a fair
procedure, serves in principle to protect the freedom of a suspect person to choose whether to
speak or to remain silent when questioned by the police. Such freedom of choice is effectively
undermined in a case which, the suspect having elected to remain silent during questioning, the
authorities use subterfuge to elicit, from the suspect, confessions or other statements of an
incriminatory nature.”
4.9.3. Constitutional and Convention Implications
The following implications can be derived from the Convention case law in particular.
• A statute which criminalises the exercise of a right to silence violates the Convention
• A compulsion to give information which may subsequently be used in a criminal investigation violates the
convention.
• The drawing of inferences is acceptable though the degree of compulsion will be monitored by the court
and inferences cannot found solely or mainly a criminal conviction.
• It might be noted that Section 19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 as inserted by section 30 of the 2007
Act is arguably contrary to the convention in that it permits inferences to be drawn from silence where no
solicitor is present.60 However, this is a matter for decision by the Irish Superior courts in any given case
and this should in no way limit the Gardai in resorting to the section at present.

4.9.4. The Constitution and Oppressive Questioning
The Constitution prohibits interrogation that is oppressive as a fundamental violation of due process. Evidence
obtained as a result of such practices will be excluded absent extraordinary excusing circumstances. In
particular the courts have frowned on the practice of extracting a confession out of an accused by lengthy
questioning without a break. In People (DPP) v. McNally61 convictions based on 40 hours of continuous
questioning were quashed by reason of such oppression. In DPP v Lynch,62 the Supreme Court held that the
sustained questioning of the accused over a 22 hour period, coupled with the denial of access to his family or
the opportunity for rest or sleep all amounted to such circumstances of harassment and oppression as to make
it unjust and unfair to admit statements.
Further The People (DPP) v. Shaw63 indicates that a statement that is the product of physical or psychological
pressures threats or inducements or that is made while intoxicated, drugged or hypnotised will not be
admitted into evidence in court.
In Ryan v. AG,64 the SC upheld the judgement of Kenny J that one of the unenumerated rights protected by
Article 40.3 was the right to bodily integrity.
4.9.5. Right to an Interpreter
This principle was first announced in Kamasinski v. Austria65 as deriving from Article 6 though unsuccessful on
the facts. It would seem in principle from Kamasinski that the right to an interpreter applies not just at trial but
in custody and in particular at interview.

Section Five The Obligation to Preserve Evidence, To Conduct
Enquiries and The Duty of Disclosure
These doctrines emanate from recent Supreme Court decisions and directly affect the operational decisions
that may be made in the conduct of an investigation.
The importance of the prosecution’s duty of disclosure was set out by the European Court of Human Rights in
Edwards v UK 15 E.H.H.R. 417 (1992) where it was held that disclosure of all material evidence to an accused
is a requirement of a fair trial under Article 6 of the European Convention on Human Rights. The Irish courts
have held that an individual’s constitutional right to fair procedures in a criminal trial requires that adequate
disclosure is made to an accused person. The following basic principle was laid by McCarthy J. in The People
[DPP] v Tuite 2 Frewen 175 at 180/181
“The Constitutional right to fair procedures demands that the prosecution be conducted fairly; it
is the duty of the prosecution, whether adducing such evidence or not, where possible, to make
available all relevant evidence, parol or otherwise, it its possession, so that if the prosecution
does not adduce such evidence, the defence may, it if wishes, do so.”
Two elements from this passage should be noted:
1. “Relevant evidence” in this context refers to anything that is relevant to the defence case. The
prosecution is not bound to disclose everything just because it has been gathered in the course of the
investigation which led to the preferral of charges. When the prosecution intends to rely on a witness
statement, there is a duty to disclose any factors or circumstances in which the statement was made
which might affect its reliability. In The People [DPP] v Meleady and Grogan [1995] 2 I.R. 517, the Court
of Criminal Appeal quashed a conviction which had been based on identification evidence partly on the
basis that the defence was not made aware of the possibility that a key prosecution witness had been
shown a book of photographs of suspects before making the identification.
2. “Possession of the prosecution”; In Murphy v DPP [1989] I.L.R.M. 71 the accused was charged with a
number of offences while driving a motor car which was crashed and seriously damaged. The case
against the accused was based entirely on identification evidence. A defence request to examine the
damaged car was granted but it was removed from Garda custody by the insurance company which was
interested in its salvage value. The Defence sought an order restraining any further prosecution of the
charges in the High Court on the grounds that the removal of the car had prevented them adducing
possible scientific evidence which might have served to refute the visual identification evidence. The
Defence also claimed that the actions of the Gardai in failing to preserve evidence which might have been
helpful to the defence case amounted to a breach of natural justice. Lynch J. concluded:
“The authorities establish that evidence relevant to guilt or innocence must as far as is necessary
and is practicable be kept until the conclusion of the trial. These authorities also apply to the
preservation of articles which may give rise to the reasonable possibility of securing relevant
evidence.”
This approach was followed by the Supreme Court in the case of Braddish v DPP [2001] 3 I.R.127 where the
accused was charged with robbery of a shop which had been caught on video tape. He was identified from
the video tape by a member of the Garda Siochana and made confessions while in Garda custody
subsequently. He was charged nine months later when the defence requested copies of the video footage. Six
months later, the Defence were informed that the video tapes had been returned to the owner. Hardiman J.
noted that the prosecution were not relying on the video evidence but were content to rely on the confession.
Nevertheless, the Judge considered that this did not relieve the prosecution of their obligation to retain the
video evidence. Hardiman J. noted;

41

Appendix II Procedures

“…More fundamentally, this is a video tape which purports actually to show the robbery in
progress. It is not acceptable, in my view, to excuse the absence of so vital and direct a piece of
evidence simply by saying that the prosecution were not relying on it, but prefer to rely on an
alleged confession. Firstly, the confession is hotly disputed. Secondly, a confession should if
possible be corroborated and relatively recent history both here and in the neighbouring
jurisdiction has unfortunate examples of the risks of excessive reliance on confession evidence.
Thirdly, the video tape has a clear potential to exculpate as well as to inculpate.
This video tape was real evidence and the Gardai were not entitled to dispose of it before the
trial. It is now admitted that they should not have done so. Lest however, the sentence already
quoted from the State Solicitor’s letter (and which can only have been based on his instructions
from the Gardai) can be read to suggest that because the prosecution was based wholly on an
alleged confession, other items of evidence can be destroyed or rendered unavailable, I wish to
state emphatically that this is not so. It is the duty of the Garda, arising from their unique
investigative role, to seek out and preserve all evidence having a bearing or potential bearing on
the issue of guilt or innocence. This is so whether the prosecution proposes to rely on the
evidence or not, and regardless of whether it assists the case for the prosecution or not…”
The Supreme Court accordingly concluded that the failure to preserve such vital evidence violated the
guarantee to fair procedures.
In Dunn v DPP66 the incident in question concerned a petrol station, which had been subject to video
surveillance. Irrespective of whether the Gardai had actually received the video evidence, there was a violation
of the right to a fair trial in that it would have been reasonable for the Gardai to have inquired about the
video. The obligation of the Gardai was to seek out as well as preserve the evidence.
Bowes and McGrath
In Bowes and McGrath v. DPP67 the boot of a car contained a substantial quantity of illegal drugs but at trial it
was learned that the vehicle had been sent for scrappage. The court indicated that the finding against Bowes
was based on finding the drugs in the car and an alleged oral statement, which if admitted and accepted by
the jury might be thought to demonstrate knowledge of the contents of the boot. In those circumstances
there was no unfairness. As far as McGrath was concerned the applicant was charged with dangerous driving
causing death as her vehicle collided with a motorcycle, which the Gardai had parted possession with. Here
the technical examination would have provided a reasonable possibility of rebutting the evidence proffered
against her and thus there was unfairness.
In general terms Hardiman J indicated that the duty to preserve potentially important evidence was not an
open ended one and:
“It cannot be interpreted as requiring the gardai to engage in disproportionate commitment of
manpower and resources in an exhaustive search for every conceivable kind of evidence. The
duty must be interpreted realistically on the facts of each case.”
Scully
In Scully v DPP,68 relief was refused for reasons which, inter alia, included the fact that the three cameras
covering a garage forecourt did not cover the area where a particular vehicle was parked and were, in any
event, totally ineffective in darkness. In other words, the respondent was able to and did demonstrate that no
prejudice in fact arose or could arise on the facts of the particular case.
Hardiman J. in the course of his judgment in Scully v DPP deprecated applications brought on theoretical or
fanciful grounds stating (at 252):

“The fact is that the rationale of this application vanished after the undisputed facts in relation to
the video surveillance on the filling station at the time of the crime were revealed. This did not
happen until quite close to the trial because the applicant did not investigate the position any
earlier. This, in turn, appears to me to indicate that the applicant was more interested in tripping
the investigators than in discovery of evidence: certainly he was constrained to continue his
application on the basis of a theoretical possibility only…. Applications on this basis must be
discountenanced in the interest of the public right to prosecute, but also in the interest of the
integrity of the jurisdiction, in a proper case, to restrain a prosecution on the basis that
significant evidence has been ignored or destroyed”.
McFarlane
In McFarlane v. DPP 7th March 2006 the Supreme Court split in circumstances where fingerprints and
photographs had been taken and then the items on which those prints and photographs were taken were lost.
Hardiman J for the majority indicated that:
“It therefore appears that, whether the fingerprint on the missing items be regarded as a
document, or as an independent item of real evidence, there is ample provision in law for its
proof by way of a duly authenticated photograph.
Viewed in this light, the distinction between the present case and those cited is clear. Quite
obviously the Gardaí have been in breach of their duty to preserve the evidence, but in this case,
unlike the others, this breach has not resulted in the loss of that evidence in an independently
verifiable form. That statement must of course be read subject to what is said below about the
powers and functions of the trial court.”
Kearns J dissented and indicated in a consideration of general principles that:
“Braddish is therefore important for various reasons in the context of the present case. Firstly, it
emphasises the requirement to preserve items, which may be “useful” to the defence. It goes
without saying, in my view, that the original items, which have been lost, in this case can only be
seen as falling into that category. However they are not merely items which are useful or
evidentially relevant. They constitute or contain the primary and central evidence in the case.
………Secondly, the judgment of Hardiman J in Braddish is authority for the proposition that
respondent cannot rely on the existence of an alleged admission by an accused to substitute for
the obligations outlined above, a view also expressed by Hardiman J in Dunne v DPP [2002] 2 IR
305 . Thirdly it shows that reliance by the prosecution on secondary evidence (the stills in the
Braddish case) may create a ‘real problem’ for an accused person who has been deprived of an
opportunity of examining the original exhibit.
This case goes a lot further in my view than a number of the video tape cases, where in some
instances applications were brought to court on the basis of a mere possibility that they might
have contained material either inculpatory or exculpatory of the accused.”
Note: Members are also referred to chapter 9 of the “Guidelines for Prosecuters” as published by the
Director of Public Prosecutions generally on this subject.

Section Six Surveillance and Investigation
6.1. The Constitution and Privacy
There is no express recognition of privacy in The Irish Constitution and it has been developed as an unspecified
right under the rubric of Article 40.3
McGee v. AG/The Right to Marital Privacy
In McGee v. AG,69 the SC (4:1 majority) identified the unenumerated right to marital privacy guaranteed by
Article 40.3.1, which included a right to decide to use contraceptives
Norris v AG/the Recognition of Pure Privacy
In Norris v. AG70 the right to privacy per se was argued as rendering unconstitutional the criminalisation of
certain homosexual conduct between consenting adult males. The argument failed on the facts with a majority
of the court emphasising the Christian nature of the state, the obligation of the state to discourage conduct
that was morally wrong, the effect of marriage and the spread of venereal diseases. Nonetheless, all judges
either explicitly or implicitly recognised the privacy right though emphasising such considerations as State
security, public order or morality, or other essential components of the common good would justify a
restriction of the right. The ECHR subsequently upheld the privacy right of Norris as an expression of his
individual personality and Irish law was ultimately changed.71
Privacy and Surveillance: Kennedy v Ireland
In Kennedy v. Ireland,72 the first two Plaintiffs were journalists (Geraldine Kennedy and Bruce Arnold) whose
phones had been tapped on foot of a warrant issued by the Minister for Justice. Conversations were taped
and transcripts made although it was later admitted that there was no justification for this. The Plaintiffs
claimed damages for breach of their constitutional rights. Hamilton P (as he then was) accepted that the
Constitution guaranteed an unenumerated and fundamental personal right to privacy which flowed from the
Christian and democratic nature of the State. He continued:
“The dignity and freedom of an individual in a democratic society cannot be ensured if his
communications of a private nature [...] are deliberately, consciously and unjustifiably intruded
upon and interfered with. [However,] an individual must accept [...] the fact that in certain
circumstances the exigencies of the common good may require and justify such intrusion and
interference.”
Since it had been admitted that no such circumstances had existed in this case the Plaintiffs recovered £20,000
each for breach of their constitutional right to privacy.
Again it was emphasised that the right to privacy is not absolute and can be restricted by reference to the
constitutional rights of others or the common good/public order and morality.
6.1.1. Statutory Regulation of Telephone Tapping
The interception of communications is not unconstitutional per se and is authorised by statute. Under The
Postal and Telecommunications Services Act 1983 Sections 98 and 110 no offence is committed if a person
acts under the direction of the Minister for Communications and the Minister of Justice has granted an
authorisation for interception under The Postal Packets and Telecommunications Message (Regulation) Act
1993 Section 2.
Such authorisation may only be given for the purposes of investigating serious criminal offences or in the interests
of the security of the state. The tap lasts for three months only and there is provision for review of the latter Act’s
operation by a high court judge or the investigation by that judge of an individual case provided for under Section
8 of the Act. Section 9 of the Act contains a provision whereby a complaint can be investigated by a complaints
referee who may make a recommendation for an award of compensation if they uphold the complaint.

69: [1974] IR 284

70: [1984] IR 36

71: Norris v. Ireland [1989] 13 EHRR

72: [1987] IR 587
