The People (Director of Public Prosecutions) v. Dillon73
In Dillon The Court of Criminal Appeal held that the action of a member of the Gardai in holding a
conversation on a confiscated phone and using a false name amounted to an interception within the meaning
of s. 98 of the 1983 Act. The court was also of the view that such action affected constitutional rights and the
evidence could only be admitted if there were extraordinary excusing circumstances.

6.2. Privacy and the Convention
Privacy, Telephone Tapping and the Convention
Unless properly regulated by law, interception of communications will create a violation of Art. 8 of The
European Convention: Klass v. Germany.74 Although no violation was found in Klass the court laid down
rigorous safeguards which they would utilise in subsequent cases:
In terms of general principle the court opined that:
“Although telephone conversations are not expressly mentioned in paragraph 1 of Article 8 (art.
8-1), the Court considers, as did the Commission, that such conversations are covered by the
notions of "private life" and "correspondence" referred to by this provision”.
The court also cautioned that:
“Powers of secret surveillance of citizens, characterising as they do the police state, are tolerable
under the Convention only in so far as strictly necessary for safeguarding the democratic
institutions.”
The court noted in Klass that there was an act of parliament justifying such measures and that they were
deemed "necessary in a democratic society in the interests of national security" and/or "for the
prevention of disorder or crime". The Court accepted that the existence of some legislation granting
powers of secret surveillance over the mail, post and telecommunications is, under exceptional conditions,
necessary in a democratic society in the interests of national security and/or for the prevention of disorder or
crime but crucially noted the necessity for certain procedural safeguards against abuse.
Crucially the court emphasised the need and importance of judicial control and monitoring:
“The Court considers that, in a field where abuse is potentially so easy in individual cases and
could have such harmful consequences for democratic society as a whole, it is in principle
desirable to entrust supervisory control to a judge.”
Though on the facts the supervisory and other safeguards provided by Germany passed muster they did not do
so in Malone v United Kingdom75 (1985).
Other forms of Surveillance
It would seem that the Irish Legislation governing telephone tapping would pass the Convention test
adumbrated in Klass. However, the crucial problem in this jurisdiction is the absence of regulation of other
forms of electronic surveillance. Apart from general principles that can be derived from Kennedy v. Ireland the
issue of surveillance can be seen in its most detailed form in the jurisprudence of the Convention.
Business Telephone Tapping
It is quite clear from the case law of the convention that calls from an office as well as a home phone fall
within the ambit of privacy protection. In Halford v. United Kingdom76 a case that arose as a result of a sex
discrimination claim by an assistant chief constable, the court opined that:
“In the Court's view, it is clear from its case-law that telephone calls made from business premises
as well as from the home may be covered by the notions of ‘private life’ and ‘correspondence’
within the meaning of Article 8...“

73: [2003] 1 ILRM 531

74: [1978] 2 EHRR

75: [1985] 7 EHRR 14

76: 24 EHRR 523

45

appendix2 final final 2:Layout 1

11/12/2007

10:20

Page 46

Appendix II Procedures

Crucially, and in finding a violation, in a passage of general import the court opined:
“In the context of secret measures of surveillance or interception of communications by public
authorities, because of the lack of public scrutiny and the risk of misuse of power, the domestic
law must provide some protection to the individual against arbitrary interference with Article 8
rights (art. 8). Thus, the domestic law must be sufficiently clear in its terms to give citizens an
adequate indication as to the circumstances in and conditions on which public authorities are
empowered to resort to any such secret measures.”
Listening Devices
In Khan v. United Kingdom77 a hidden listening device was installed in a hotel when there was no legislation
governing the use of such an apparatus in the UK. The court found a violation of Article 8.
Conclusions on Klass and Halford
The conclusions of Klass and Halford are crystal clear that in order for home or private telephone tapping or
listening devices to be sanctionable there have to be requisite procedural safeguards and a requisite degree of
justification such as the investigation of crime or an issue of state security. Moreover, judicial control and
monitoring is advisable. In Ireland, at present, such safeguards only exist in relation to telephone tapping.
Principles from Peck v. United Kingdom
A summary of recent extensions of the principle may be found in Peck v. United Kingdom78 which concerned
CCTV footage which was disclosed to the press. The following principles emerge from that case:
• Although monitoring through CCTV footage is a normal incident of everyday life. Private life considerations
may arise, however, once any systematic or permanent record comes into existence of such material from
the public domain.
• The compilation of data by security services on particular individuals, even without the use of covert
surveillance methods, may constitute an interference with the applicants' private lives.
• The permanent recording of voices in a police cell as police officers listened in and the recording of the
voices for further analysis was regarded as the processing of personal data amounting to an interference
with the right to respect for private lives.
• The disclosure of footage taken of an individual, who was not subsequently charged, to television
constituted a breach of privacy.
6.2.1. Email and Internet Monitoring
In Copland v. United79 Kingdom the European Court addressed for the first time comprehensively the question
of e-mail and internet monitoring and privacy. The court indicates that, as according to its case law, telephone
calls from business premises are prima facie covered by the notions of private life and correspondence for the
purposes of Article 8 (Citing Halford):
“It follows logically that e-mails sent from work should be similarly protected under Article 8, as
should information derived from the monitoring of personal internet usage.”
The court concluded, on the facts, as the UK at the time of the incident had no legal regulation of e-mail and
internet usage the interference by a school vice principal in a state statutorily regulated college was not in
accordance with law. Though the court did also conclude that:
“The court would not exclude that the monitoring of an employee’s use of a telephone, e-mail or
internet usage at the place of work may be considered “necessary in a democratic society” in
certain situations in pursuit of a legitimate aim.”

77: [2001] 31 EHRR 1016

46

78: [2003] 36 EHRR 719

79: Judgement of the European Court of Human Rights, 3rd April 2007

appendix2 final final 2:Layout 1

11/12/2007

10:20

Page 47

Appendix II Procedures

6.2.2. Overall Conclusions
The position somewhat baldly stated is that bugging, listening devices, CCTV footage, email and internet
monitoring are all in violation of the Convention for the simple reason there is no adequate statutory
monitoring of same. What is required to ensure Convention compliance is a set of statutes that indicate such
measures are permissible where there is the investigation of serious crime or national security and such
measures would also have to have procedural safeguards built in, preferably though not necessarily judicial
monitoring. Thus in using such methods the Garda are at present in a difficult position in relation to the
Convention and it must be further stressed in the light of the incorporation of the Convention into domestic
law that an Irish Court may well apply the Convention and Constitutional privacy right and find a
constitutional and convention violation.

6.3. Entrapment
The ECHR has held that unfairness in the process of criminal investigation may lead to a breach of Article 6.1
of the Convention. In Teixeira De Castro v. Portugal80 the issue of entrapment was considered and a violation
found when undercover police officers offered money to the applicant to supply them with heroin. The court
contrasted the officer’s actions with those of true undercover agents who conceal their identity in order to
obtain information and evidence about a crime rather than inciting it.
In MM v. Netherlands81 the court found a violation of Article 8 in an entrapment scenario in that:
“It is not in dispute that it was the police who made the suggestion to the Mrs S. to record
telephone conversations with the applicant. With the prior permission of the public prosecutor,
police officers connected a tape recorder to Mrs S.'s telephone. They suggested that she steer her
conversations with the applicant towards the latter's sexual approaches. They instructed Mrs S. in
the operation of the tape recorder. They came to her home and collected the recordings. It was
left to Mrs S. to entrap the applicant into making statements amounting to admissions of guilt
and to activate the tape recorder.”
Thus entrapment techniques of the types cited are in violation of the Convention.

80: [1999] 28 EHRR 101

81: Application No. 39339/98

Appendix II Procedures

Section Seven Storage of Evidence Relevant to Major
Criminal Investigations
Section 2 of the Criminal Procedure Act, 1993 provides for a review by the Court of Criminal Appeal of alleged
miscarriage of justice or exclusive sentences in respect of certain convictions and sentences and relates to
persons who are either convicted on indictment or sent forward for sentence after signing a plea of guilty.
Section 2 of the Act allows a convicted person to apply to the Court of Criminal Appeal for an order quashing their
conviction or reviewing their sentence where a newly discovered fact indicates a miscarriage of justice in relation to
the conviction or that the sentence imposed was excessive.There is no time limit in taking such an appeal.
Section 3(3)(b) of the Act provides that on the hearing of an application, the Court of Criminal Appeal may
order the production of any document or other thing connected with the proceedings. Thus, the Act places an
onus on An Garda Siochana to preserve relevant items of evidential value.
To ensure compliance with this statutory duty relating to the safe custody of files, exhibits and other
documents the Garda National Repository was established and is based at Santry Garda Station. All items of
evidential value to be retained at the National Repository must have been processed through the relevant
District Office.
Where a prosecution in court results in a conviction and the time for lodging an appeal has lapsed, all items
connected with the prosecution should be forwarded to the National Repository, Santry. They should be
packaged, labelled and transferred to the National Repository in tamper-free plastic evidence boxes.
The Inspector in charge of the Garda Museum/Archives will advise as to the proper procedures relating to the
storage and packaging of evidence for transfer to the National Repository.
Where an investigation is ongoing with little prospect of an immediate resolution, relevant items of evidential
value may be transferred from the relevant District Office to the National Repository after a reasonable period
of time. A period of two years from the commencement of the investigation is the relevant period in this
context. Flexibility will be afforded to the senior member in charge of the investigation and in exceptional
circumstances, the ‘open’ file may be retained locally at the respective District HQ for a longer period.
An Evidential Documents/Exhibits List has been compiled as a guideline to District Officers as to the categories
of documents and exhibits which must be retained to ensure compliance with relevant legislation and formal
instructions. All relevant items of evidential value will be contained in specially devised tamper-free plastic
evidence boxes, which are available from the Procurement Office, Garda Headquarters, on request. Large
items of evidential value listed may also be retained at the National Repository in appropriate circumstances.
The list includes the following evidential items:
• Original Statements
(i.e. Witness statements, Statements of Accused Persons, Statements not included in the Book of Evidence.
Note: best practice is to keep all statements)
• ‘Locus in Quo’ Record
• Notebooks (photocopy of relevant entries where applicable)
• Forensic Reports
• Fingerprints
• Ballistic Reports
• Medical Reports
• State Pathologist Reports
• Custody Records (photocopy of relevant entries where applicable)
• Warrants

In preparation for the transfer of paper-based documentary evidence the following guidelines will apply:
• Only items of evidential value suitable for retention in the appropriate sealed boxes should be transferred
to the National Repository.
• The File Owner is the District Officer in charge of the investigation.
• All items of evidence, as outlined above, must be placed in tamper-free evidence boxes, properly sealed.
• Large items of evidence, not suitable for inclusion in the evidence box should be appropriately wrapped
and labelled.
• Each evidence box must contain a full Inventory List of contents signed by the File Owner and should be
properly identified on the side of each box. This list of contents will include accompanying large items not
suitable for storage in evidence boxes and relating to the same case.
• No boxed evidence forwarded to the National Repository will be accepted unless accompanied by Form
CPA1, signed by the File Owner. Original Form CPA1 will be retained at the National Repository, a copy will
be forwarded to the relevant District Headquarters.
